1.8: dynamis (dynamis) is not courage or willpower but the divine capacity to perform what exceeds human ability — the same word used for the Spirit's creative power in the Incarnation (Luke 1:35). The promise is not merely empowerment but an endowment that makes the disciples constitutively capable witnesses. martyres (martyres) will become the defining identity-word for believers in Acts (1:22; 2:32; 3:15; 5:32; 10:39; 13:31; 22:15; 26:16): those who testify to what they have personally seen. The geographic progression — Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of the earth — is the structural outline of all twenty-eight chapters.
1.11: ho Galilaioi (hoi Galilaioi): the angelic address specifies their provincial origin; in first-century Judaism, Galileans were regarded with mild condescension by Jerusalem's educated elite. The disciples who will shake the empire are identified as uneducated provincials — Acts' characteristic irony. analepsis (analēpsis) — the same word Luke used in 9:51 for the 'assumption' toward which Jesus set his face — frames the ascension as completion of the entire travel-narrative begun in Luke's central section.
1.1: ton proton logon (ton prōton logon, 'the first account/word'): the preface ties Acts to Luke's Gospel (proton implies at least a second); logos covers both narrative account and theological word. Theophile (Theophile): the dedicatee appears in Luke 1:1-4 as kratiste (most excellent), a formal address for high-status patrons. Whether Theophilus is a historical patron, a literary figure ('lover of God'), or both, Luke addresses a literate Greco-Roman audience throughout.
1.2: dia pneumatos hagiou enteila meno (dia pneumatos hagiou enteilamenos, 'having commanded through the Holy Spirit'): the ascension instructions are Spirit-mediated — the risen Christ's final directives are simultaneously the Spirit's directives, anticipating the Spirit's continued voice through the apostolic community in Acts. apostolois (apostolois, 'apostles'): the term emphasizes their commissioned-sender relationship to Christ, not merely discipleship.
1.3: tekmeriois pollois (tekmēriois pollois, 'many convincing proofs'): tekmērion is a legal term for conclusive evidence (as opposed to sēmeion, sign, or martyria, testimony alone). Luke signals that the resurrection appearances carry evidentiary weight, not merely experiential weight. di' hēmerōn tesserakonta (forty days): the same count as Moses at Sinai (Exod 24:18), Elijah at Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8), and Jesus's wilderness temptation (Luke 4:2); the number marks a divinely structured period of preparation before a new covenant stage.
1.4: sunalizomenos (sunalizomenos): disputed reading — either 'eating together with' (syn + halizo from hals, salt = sharing salt/eating) or 'assembling together with' (syn + athroizō). The first reading (shared meals) fits Luke's 'eating' resurrection appearances (Luke 24:30, 41-43) and was preferred by Chrysostom. epangelian tou patros (epangelian tou patros, 'the promise of the Father'): the Spirit is pre-promised before being poured out — Acts is the story of a kept promise.
1.5: en pneumati baptisthesesthe hagio (en pneumati baptisthēsesthe hagiō, 'you will be baptized in the Holy Spirit'): the contrast with John's water-baptism frames Spirit-reception as a distinct, superior act. The passive baptisthēsesthe (you will be baptized) makes God the agent: Pentecost is not something the disciples achieve but something done to them. ou meta pollas tautas hemeras ('not many days from now'): the imminence creates urgency — remain, wait, receive.
1.6: apokathistaneis ten basileian to Israel (apokathistaneis tēn basileian tō Israēl, 'will you restore the kingdom to Israel?'): apokathistēmi (restore) is the LXX's standard word for Israel's return from exile and covenant restoration (Jer 15:19; 16:15; Hos 11:11). The disciples ask a legitimate prophetic question — OT promises do include national restoration for Israel. Jesus's answer does not deny the promise but reframes the timing and the community through whom it works.
1.7: chronous e kairous (chronous ē kairous, 'times or seasons'): chronos refers to measured clock-time; kairos to appointed, decisive moments. The pair covers both calendar-time and moment-time. en te idia exousia (en tē idia exousia, 'by his own authority'): exousia is the freedom of decision that belongs to the Father alone — the same word for Jesus's own authority (Luke 4:36) but here asserted for the Father's sole prerogative over eschatological timing.
1.9: nephele hypelaben auton (nephelē hypelaben auton, 'a cloud received/took him up'): the cloud is the OT cloud of divine presence (Exod 13:21; 40:34; 1 Kgs 8:10-11). The ascension is not merely departure but entry into the divine presence-sphere. Hypolambanō (receive, take up) is used for a cloud receiving someone only here — the technical vocabulary signals a unique divine act. Luke's eyewitness note ('before their very eyes') grounds the ascension as visible historical event, not purely spiritual experience.
1.10: duo andres en esthesesi leukais (duo andres en esthēsesi leukais, 'two men in white garments'): the same angelic pair-formula as the transfiguration (Luke 9:30-31, where Moses and Elijah appear alongside Jesus) and the resurrection tomb (Luke 24:4). White garments consistently mark heavenly messengers in apocalyptic literature (Dan 7:9; Rev 1:14). The two-witness requirement (Deut 19:15) may be embedded in the double-messenger pattern.
1.12: sabbatou echon hodon (sabbatou echon hodon, 'a Sabbath day's journey'): approximately 2,000 cubits (~880 meters), derived from Num 35:5's boundary-distance around Levitical cities. Luke's geographic precision grounds the narrative; the Sabbath-distance was not simply 'walking distance' but a legal halakic category — the disciples returned within the permitted limit, observing Torah even as the new age was dawning.
1.13: to hyperoon (to hypērōon, 'the upper room'): Greek hyperōon (upper story) occurs in Acts at 9:37, 39 and 20:8-9; the upper room is the characteristic space for the Jerusalem community's private meetings. The Twelve minus Judas (now eleven) are listed; James son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus are included though they nearly disappear from the Acts narrative, replaced by the figure of James the Lord's brother who emerges as the Jerusalem leader by ch. 15.
1.14: homothumadon te proseuche (homothymadon tē proseuchē, 'with one accord in prayer'): the same homothymadon that will characterize Pentecost (2:1, 46) begins here with prayer. Prayer is the posture of the community before the Spirit's coming; the sequence — prayer → Spirit → witness — is the structural pattern Acts will repeat. syn gynaixin ('with the women') is significant: the women who witnessed the resurrection (Luke 24) are integral to the praying community, not peripheral to it.
1.15: en tais hemerais tautais (en tais hēmerais tautais, 'in those days'): Luke's temporal marker for the pre-Pentecost period; the same phrase frames eschatological fulfillment in Peter's Joel-citation (2:17: 'in the last days'). ochlos onomatōn (ochlos onomatōn, 'a crowd of names/persons'): an unusual Semitism (names = persons, Num 1:18, 20 LXX). The 120 may represent the core number required to establish a juridical community in Jewish law (a bet din requires 23; 120 allows for five such courts).
1.16: edei plērōthenai tēn graphēn (edei plērōthēnai tēn graphēn, 'it was necessary that the Scripture be fulfilled'): the same dei of divine necessity that drove the entire passion narrative (Luke 24:26; 9:22). Peter's hermeneutic: Scripture speaks not merely as historical record but as prophetic directive — Judas's betrayal was foreseen and therefore the vacant apostolic place must be filled. elalasen dia stomatos David ('spoke through the mouth of David'): David as the Spirit's instrument, not the source; the pneumatological understanding of Scripture that 2 Pet 1:21 makes explicit.
1.17: kateprithmenos en hemin (katērithmenos en hēmin, 'numbered/counted among us'): the verb implies formal membership — Judas was not a marginal follower but an enrolled member of the Twelve with a kleros (lot/share) in the apostolic ministry. ton kleron tes diakonias tautes ('the lot of this ministry'): klēros is both the lot cast to choose and the assigned portion/inheritance; its use here anticipates the lot-casting of v.26 and confirms that apostolic ministry is a divinely assigned inheritance.
1.18: misthou tes adikias (misthou tēs adikias, 'wages of wickedness'): misthos (wage, pay) is darkly ironic — Judas received payment for betrayal; his payment purchased the field that became his burial place. The account of his death (falling headlong, bowels burst open) is vivid compared to Matthew's hanging (27:5); the two accounts are compatible if the body later fell after the rope broke or the body was cut down. Akeldama: an Aramaic transliteration (haqel dema, 'field of blood').
1.19: gnōston egeneto pasin tois katoikousin Ierousalem ('became known to all the residents of Jerusalem'): Luke's corroborative note that Judas's end was widely known; the field's name preserved the memory publicly. The parenthetical is Luke's narrator-voice (not Peter's speech), explaining for Theophilus's non-Aramaic audience what Akeldama means. The linguistic notation — 'in their language' — is one of Luke's ethnographic markers throughout Acts.
1.20: en biblo psalmōn (en biblō psalmōn, 'in the book of Psalms'): Peter's two citations (Ps 69:25 and Ps 109:8) from the same canonical collection are not random proof-texts but form a hermeneutical case: the psalms of the righteous sufferer betrayed by his companions predict not only the passion but the community's necessary response to the vacant place. Ginetō (let it become) translates the LXX jussive, treated as directive: the Scripture's optative becomes the community's mandate.
1.21: dei oun ton sunelthonta hemin (dei oun ton synelthonta hēmin, 'it is therefore necessary for one of the men who accompanied us'): dei again — the replacement of Judas is divinely necessary, not merely administratively convenient. The qualification 'who accompanied us throughout the time the Lord Jesus went in and out among us' (eisēlthen kai exēlthen) defines apostleship as eyewitness companionship with the historical Jesus, not just post-resurrection encounter.
1.22: martys anastaseōs autou (martyra tēs anastaseōs autou, 'a witness of his resurrection'): the resurrection witness is the defining apostolic credential; the criteria of vv.21-22 are not merely selection criteria but a definition of apostolic identity. One who has not been a witness of the resurrection cannot fill the Twelve's function, which is precisely testimony (martyria). This is why Paul's apostleship required the Damascus road appearance (1 Cor 9:1; 15:8).
1.23: estēsan duo Ioseph ton kaloumenon Barsabban (estēsan duo, 'they nominated two'): the community nominates; the Lord chooses (v.24). The division of human discernment and divine decision is characteristic of Acts' understanding of church governance. Barsabbas (son of the Sabbath) and Matthias are otherwise unknown in the NT; Eusebius (HE 1.12) mentions Matthias as one of the Seventy, but the identification is uncertain.
1.24: Kardiognosta panton (kardiognōsta pantōn, 'Knower-of-all-hearts'): a compound noun for divine omniscience concentrated in one attribute. The community prays to the one who knows what their own discernment cannot reach — the interior disposition of the two candidates. The same title appears in Acts 15:8 (God the 'heart-knower' bears witness to Gentile faith). The prayer is not passive but specific: 'show us which of these two you have chosen.'
1.25: ten diakonias kai apostoles (tēn diakonian kai apostolēn, 'the ministry and apostleship'): the pairing of diakonia (service) and apostolē (apostleship) defines the role as both serving and sent — the two dimensions that will characterize the entire Acts mission. idia topon (idion topon, 'his own place'): the place 'belonging to' Judas — an understated phrase for his fate. The contrast with the kleros (inherited lot) of apostolic ministry is sharp.
1.26: edōkan klērous autois (edōkan klērous autois, 'they gave lots for them'): lot-casting as a decision mechanism has OT precedent (Lev 16:8; Josh 7:14; 1 Chr 24:5; Prov 16:33) and affirms that the outcome is in God's hands. This is the last lot-casting in the NT; after Pentecost, the Spirit directs the community directly (13:2; 15:28; 16:6-7). The lot falls to Matthias, completing the restoration of the Twelve as the symbolic foundation-number of the new covenant community.

2.1: homothymadon (homothymadon, 'with one accord/purpose') appears eleven times in Acts and only once elsewhere in the NT (Rom 15:6). It is not merely spatial togetherness but a deliberate unity of will and disposition — the kind of communal harmony that Ps 133:1 calls 'good and pleasant'. Luke signals that Pentecost descends on a community already in relational order.
2.4: heterais glossais (heterais glōssais, 'other/different tongues') identifies actual foreign languages — confirmed by the catalogue in vv. 9–11 of identifiable nations who each hear 'in our own tongue' (tē hēmeterā dialektō). This is distinct from the ecstatic glossolalia of 1 Cor 14. The Pentecost miracle partially reverses Babel (Gen 11:1–9): where Babel dispersed by language confusion, the Spirit unifies across language boundaries. apophthengesthai (apophtheggesthai, 'to utter', 2:4, 14) is a semi-technical term for inspired prophetic speech; used of the Delphic oracle and in LXX for prophetic utterance (Mic 5:12; Ezek 13:9).
2.38: metanoeite (metanoeite) is the same imperative John and Jesus used (Mark 1:15; Luke 13:3,5); here it opens the community's first public invitation. baptistheto ... eis aphesin hamartion: the preposition eis marks direction or purpose — baptism toward/for forgiveness of sins, not a meritorious cause. dorea (dōrea, 'gift') marks the Spirit as freely bestowed, not earned; the same word for God's indescribable gift in 2 Cor 9:15. The sequence metanoia → baptism → forgiveness → Spirit-gift became the pattern by which Acts measures conversion.
2.42: te didache ton apostolon ... te koinonia ... te klasei tou artou ... tais proseuchais: Luke lists four activities of the Jerusalem community in asyndeton (no conjunctions), giving them equal structural weight. koinonia (koinōnia) is not merely 'fellowship' but participatory sharing — of goods (v. 44–45), of the Lord's Supper, of the Spirit (2 Cor 13:14). klasis tou artou is a Semitic phrase for breaking bread at meals; in Luke 24:35 it was the Emmaus recognition moment, suggesting eucharistic weight here.
2.2: ēchos hōsper pheromenēs pnoēs biaias (ēchos hōsper pheromenēs pnoēs biaias, 'a sound like a rushing violent wind'): pnoē (wind/breath) echoes the creation-breath of Gen 2:7 (pnoēn zōēs) and Ezek 37:9's life-giving wind; biaios (violent/forceful) underscores that this is not gentle inspiration but overwhelming divine power. The sound fills the whole house before any visible sign appears — the Spirit is first heard, then seen.
2.3: diamerizomenai glōssai hōsei pyros (diamerizomenai glōssai hōsei pyros, 'tongues distributing/dividing like fire'): the fire distributes to individuals — diamerizomenai indicates division into separate portions, one per person. The fire resting on each person recalls the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18) and Elijah's fire-baptism prediction (Luke 3:16), but the individual distribution is new: the divine presence is no longer centralized in the temple but personally imparted to each believer.
2.5: Ioudaioi eulabeis apo pantos ethnous ton hypo ton ouranon (Ioudaioi eulabeis apo pantos ethnous tōn hypo ton ouranon, 'God-fearing Jews from every nation under heaven'): eulabeis (devout/reverent) is a characteristic Lukan virtue-word (Simeon, Luke 2:25; Cornelius, Acts 10:2). The diaspora Jews' presence in Jerusalem for Pentecost explains why representatives of so many nations could be present simultaneously; they are not Gentile God-fearers but diaspora Jews whose languages reflect their dispersion.
2.6: synēlthe to plēthos kai synechy the ('the crowd came together and was bewildered'): sygchyō (bewildered/confused) is the same verb as Gen 11:9 LXX for the Babel confusion (synecheen). Luke may intend the etymological echo: what God confused at Babel the Spirit now unconfuses. Each person hears in their own dialect (tē idia dialektō) — the miracle is not the speaking but the hearing-in-comprehension.
2.7: exīstanto de kai ethaumazon (existanto de kai ethaumazon, 'they were astonished and amazed'): Luke uses the imperfect — the astonishment was ongoing as they kept hearing. hoi Galilaioi (the Galileans): the audience's identification of the speakers as Galileans sets up the social irony Luke has prepared since 1:11 — the provincial uneducated disciples are the vehicles of the Spirit's work that crosses all linguistic and ethnic boundaries.
2.8: hēmeterā dialektō en hē egennēthēmen (hēmeterā dialektō en hē egennēthēmen, 'our own dialect in which we were born'): dialektos is specifically regional dialect, not merely language; the miracle reaches to the phonological particularity of each person's birth-tongue. The emphasis on birth-tongue rather than learned language intensifies the personal character of the Pentecost reversal — the Spirit meets each person in their most intimate communicative medium.
2.9: Parthoi kai Mēdoi kai Elamitai: the catalog moves roughly from east (Parthians, Medes, Elamites — Mesopotamia) through the Levant (Judea, Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia) to the diaspora West. The list is not exhaustive but representative; scholars have proposed various structural principles (zodiac signs, Roman administrative regions) none of which fully accounts for it. The theological point is the comprehensiveness: pan ethnous (every nation) is being demonstrated concretely.
2.10: proselytos (prosēlytos, 'proselyte/convert'): the inclusion of proselytes alongside Jews in the crowd's self-description signals that the new community will include both ethnic Jews and those who have formally joined Israel. The Roman visitors (both Ioudaioi te kai prosēlytoi) anticipate the Cornelius episode (ch.10) where the Roman Gentile connection will become central.
2.11: ta megaleia tou theou (ta megaleia tou theou, 'the great works of God'): the content of the Spirit-inspired speech is not glossolalic ecstasy but intelligible proclamation of God's great deeds — the same content as OT hymns (Ps 71:19; 96:3; Luke 1:49). The Pentecost miracle is fundamentally about comprehensible communication of the divine acts, not private spiritual experience.
2.12: diēporoun (diēporoun, 'they were perplexed/at a loss'): diaporeō (be completely at a loss) is a strong term — not mere puzzlement but genuine cognitive inability to categorize the event. The question ti thelei touto einai ('what does this mean?') is a genuinely open interpretive question; Peter's speech (v.14ff.) will provide the answer that resolves the diaporia.
2.13: gleukous memestōmenoi eisin (gleukous memestōmenoi eisin, 'they are full of sweet new wine'): gleukos is fresh grape juice, not necessarily fermented wine; the charge may be that they are intoxicated by the excitement of the new vintage festival. The accusation provides the foil for Peter's counterargument (v.15: 'it's only nine in the morning') — too early for drunkenness even by ancient standards.
2.14: statheis de ho Petros syn tois hendeka epēren tēn phōnen (statheis de ho Petros syn tois hendeka epēren tēn phōnēn, 'Peter standing with the Eleven raised his voice'): the verb statheis (stood) marks the beginning of formal public address. The Eleven are in solidarity — Peter speaks as representative, not soloist. Epēren tēn phōnēn (raised his voice) is the oratorical gesture of formal speech; Peter claims the public forum of the gathered diaspora crowd.
2.15: hē trite hōra (hē tritē hōra, 'the third hour'): 9 a.m. by Roman reckoning; the first of the three Jewish prayer hours (Ps 55:17; Dan 6:10), when public drinking would not have begun. Peter's argument is pragmatic — the observable sobriety of 120 people excludes the drunkenness charge. The specific hour grounds the narrative in ordinary public time, not private visionary experience.
2.16: touto estin to eiremenon dia tou prophētou Iōēl (touto estin to eirēmenon dia tou prophētou Iōēl, 'this is what was spoken through the prophet Joel'): touto estin ('this is') is the pesher formula — the observed phenomenon is identified as the fulfillment of the scriptural prediction. Peter does not say Joel predicted Pentecost; he says Pentecost is what Joel predicted. The interpretation moves from event to text, not text to prediction.
2.17: en tais eschatais hemerais (en tais eschatais hēmerais, 'in the last days'): Peter changes Joel's 'after these things' (meta tauta) to 'in the last days' (en tais eschatais hēmerais), making the eschatological orientation explicit — Pentecost inaugurates the last days. The Spirit's outpouring on pasan sarka ('all flesh') breaks the previous restriction of prophecy to designated prophets and designated times.
2.18: kai ge epi tous doulous mou kai epi tas doulas mou (kai ge epi tous doulous mou kai epi tas doulas mou, 'even on my male and female slaves/servants'): the lowest social category receives the Spirit equally. The kai ge (even!) emphasizes the unexpected inclusion; the slave has no social standing and yet receives the identical Spirit-gift as the free citizen. The Spirit's gift inverts social hierarchies without abolishing them.
2.19: terata kai semeia (terata kai sēmeia, 'wonders and signs'): Joel's cosmic portents are not necessarily literal but belong to the apocalyptic register for eschatological disruption (Isa 13:10; Ezek 32:7-8). Peter's Joel-citation extends the fulfillment claim beyond Pentecost to the whole last-days era, within which the signs continue. Luke does not stop to explain how much of Joel is fulfilled at Pentecost vs. deferred to the parousia — the category is inaugurated eschatology.
2.20: hēmera kyriou tēn megalēn kai epiphanē (hēmera kyriou tēn megalēn kai epiphanē, 'the great and manifest day of the Lord'): the LXX's epiphanē (manifest/glorious) adds the public-display dimension to Joel's 'great and terrible' (gadol wenora). The day of the Lord is simultaneously glorious (for those who call on the name) and terrible (for those who do not) — Peter's sermon is urgently evangelistic because the day has been inaugurated.
2.21: pas hos an epikalesētai to onoma kyriou sōthēsetai (pas hos an epikalesētai to onoma kyriou sōthēsetai, 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved'): the pas (everyone) and the onoma kyriou (name of the Lord) are the theological hinges. Joel 2:32 in the MT uses YHWH; Peter's audience hears kyrios = YHWH, and v.36 will identify Jesus as Kyrios. The Joel citation thus sets up the Christological identification that concludes the sermon.
2.22: apodedeigmenon apo tou theou (apodedeigmenon apo tou theou, 'having been demonstrated/accredited by God'): apodeiknymi is the term for proving something publicly through evidence — the miracles were God's letter of recommendation for Jesus. dynamesin kai terasin kai sēmeiois (powers, wonders, and signs): the three-fold designation for authenticating signs; the same trio used for the apostles' ministry (2:43; 5:12) and Paul's (2 Cor 12:12).
2.23: tē hōrismenē boulē kai prognōsei tou theou (tē hōrismenē boulē kai prognōsei tou theou, 'the determined plan and foreknowledge of God'): horizō (determine, set the boundary) is strong — not merely foreknown but decreed. Boulē (counsel/plan) and prognōsis (foreknowledge) cover both the eternal intention and its temporal anticipation. The cross is neither an accident nor a coup; it is the execution of the divine counsel. The human agency (you crucified him) is fully real alongside the divine necessity — Luke does not soften either side.
2.24: adynaton ēn krateisthai auton hyp' autou (adynaton ēn krateisthai auton hyp' autou, 'it was impossible for him to be held by it'): adynatos (impossible) is a strong word — death had no capacity to retain the author of life. The grounds of the impossibility are implicit: God cannot be defeated by the creature he made; the one who created life cannot be ended by death permanently. The resurrection is not a surprise reversal but a logical necessity given who Jesus is.
2.25: proōrōmēn ton kyrion enōpion mou dia pantos (proōrōmēn ton kyrion enōpion mou dia pantos, 'I saw the Lord always before me'): Peter quotes Ps 16:8-11 as prophecy about Christ. Proōraō (see beforehand/set before oneself) can mean either to foresee or to keep in constant view; both meanings are at play. The Psalm of unshakeable confidence in YHWH's presence even at death is attributed to Christ — David speaks in Christ's person.
2.26: hē sarx mou kataskēnōsei ep' elpidi (hē sarx mou kataskēnōsei ep' elpidi, 'my flesh will dwell in hope'): the sarx (flesh/body) dwelling in hope is bodily resurrection expectation, not spiritual immortality. The hope is for the body's restoration — the LXX's kataskēnoō (to encamp/dwell) evokes the tabernacling presence of God. The body that hopes is the body that will be raised.
2.27: ou enkatalipseis tēn psychen mou eis hadou (ou enkatalipseis tēn psychēn mou eis Hadou, 'you will not abandon my soul to Hades'): Peter's exegetical argument (vv.29-31) rests on this verse: David's tomb is present to this day (v.29), so the hosios (holy one) who will not see corruption cannot be David himself. The argument is Rabbinic in form but pneumatological in ground: David spoke 'prophetically' about one other than himself.
2.28: plērophoreias meta tou prosōpou sou (plērōseis me euphrosynēs meta tou prosōpou sou, 'you will fill me with gladness in your presence'): plēroō (fill) applied to the joy of resurrection-presence; the fullness of eschatological joy is the goal of the paschal journey. The verse is the climax of Peter's Psalm 16 citation, pointing beyond resurrection to the permanent joy of the ascended Christ in the Father's presence.
2.29: exon eipein meta parrēsias (exon eipein meta parrēsias, 'it is possible to speak freely/boldly'): parrēsia (free speech, boldness) is one of Acts' key words for the apostolic proclamation — used for Peter before the Sanhedrin (4:13), Paul's final preaching (28:31), and the church's prayer (4:29). Peter's bold claim about David's tomb is the rhetorical pivot for the argument: he appeals to a fact his audience can verify.
2.30: prophētēs hyparchōn kai eidōs (prophētēs hyparchōn kai eidōs, 'being a prophet and knowing'): prophetic knowledge is what grounds David's messianic Psalms — not merely poetic inspiration but specific divine disclosure. The oath God swore (hōmosen autō ho theos, citing Ps 132:11 / 2 Sam 7:12-13) was known to David as binding covenant commitment; David's prophetic knowledge of the resurrection flows from his knowledge of the unbreakable Davidic covenant.
2.31: proïdōn elalēsen peri tēs anastaseōs tou Christou (proïdōn elalēsen peri tēs anastaseōs tou Christou, 'seeing this beforehand he spoke of the resurrection of the Christ'): Peter's interpretive move is decisive: Ps 16 is about the resurrection, and the Christ is its subject. Proïdōn (having foreseen/seen beforehand) is the prophet's special sight — David does not merely hope for resurrection in general but prophesies specifically about the Messiah's resurrection.
2.32: martyres pantes hēmeis esmen (martyres pantes hēmeis esmen, 'witnesses of this are we all'): the plural and the inclusive pantes (all) claim eyewitness status for the whole Eleven, not merely Peter. The resurrection witness is the apostolic foundation claim; its validity rests on multiple converging witnesses. The assertion closes the Psalm 16 argument with an empirical declaration: the text predicted it; we saw it.
2.33: hypsōtheis tē dexia tou theou (hypsōtheis tē dexia tou theou, 'having been exalted at the right hand of God'): the ascension-enthronement completes the resurrection-argument. Hypsoomai (be exalted) was Jesus's own term for the cross-and-resurrection (John 3:14; 12:32); now it applies to the ascension. The exalted Christ has received the Spirit from the Father — the Spirit poured out at Pentecost is evidence of the completed ascension, not a separate event.
2.34: ou gar David anebē eis tous ouranous (ou gar David anebē eis tous ouranous, 'for David did not ascend into the heavens'): the negative argument from Ps 110:1 mirrors the negative argument from Ps 16 (David's tomb is present = he did not rise). The two Psalms form Peter's double-proof: Psalm 16 establishes resurrection; Psalm 110 establishes enthronement. Together they are the OT ground for 'God has made this Jesus both Lord and Messiah.'
2.35: heos an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou (heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou, 'until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet'): the Ps 110:1 citation includes the temporal qualifier heōs (until) — the enthronement is current and will remain until the subjugation of enemies is complete. This generates the NT's inaugurated eschatology: Christ is enthroned now, the enemies are being subdued progressively, and the final submission awaits the parousia.
2.36: Kyrion kai Christon epoiēsen ho theos touton ton Iēsoun (Kyrion kai Christon epoiēsen ho theos touton ton Iēsoun, 'God has made this Jesus both Lord and Messiah'): the climax of Peter's sermon and Acts' first Christological affirmation. Kyrion (Lord) applied to Jesus while citing kyrios in Ps 110 LXX (= YHWH) implies the highest Christological identification. Epoiēsen (made): this is not eternal ontology but the declaration of what the resurrection-enthronement has confirmed — the installed messianic king is the YHWH-Lord of the Psalm.
2.37: katenygēsan tēn kardian (katenygēsan tēn kardian, 'they were cut to the heart'): katanyssō (pierce/stab) appears only here in the NT; in LXX it can describe sharp grief (Gen 34:7; Ps 109:16). The crowd's response is not curiosity but compunction — genuine recognition that they stand in the wrong. The question ti poiēsōmen ('what shall we do?') is the right response to conviction; Peter's answer (v.38) addresses it directly.
2.39: pasin tois eis makran (pasin tois eis makran, 'all who are far off'): a phrase with OT background (Isa 57:19; cf. Acts 22:21) that will become technical for Gentile inclusion (Eph 2:13,17). At this stage Peter may mean diaspora Jews; the full Gentile implication is not reached until Acts 10. hosous an proskalesētai kyrios ho theos ('as many as the Lord our God will call'): divine calling precedes and grounds human response.
2.40: tēs geneas tēs skoleias tautes (tēs geneas tēs skoliās tautēs, 'this crooked generation'): skolias (crooked, perverse) echoes Deut 32:5's Song of Moses ('a perverse and crooked generation') applied to faithless Israel. Peter uses Moses's own language for covenantal apostasy to describe the generation that rejected and crucified Jesus; repentance and baptism are the exodus from that generation into the new covenant community.
2.41: prose tethēsan tē hēmera ekeinē psychai hōsei trischiliai (prosetethe tē hēmerā ekeinē psychai hōsei trischiliai, 'there were added that day about three thousand persons'): prosethēsan (were added) is the divine passive — God adds to the community (v.47 confirms: 'the Lord added'). Psychai (souls/persons) counts individuals. The contrast with Sinai's three thousand dead (Exod 32:28) on the day the Law was given is the kind of intertextual irony Luke crafts throughout Acts.
2.43: egineto de pase psyche phobos (egineto de pasē psychē phobos, 'awe came on every soul'): phobos (fear/awe) in Luke-Acts is typically the response to divine presence and divine power (Luke 1:12, 65; 2:9; 5:26; 7:16; Acts 5:5, 11). The wonders and signs performed through the apostles are the continuation of Jesus's miracle-ministry; the apostolic authority is not self-generated but derived from the risen Christ. terata kai sēmeia: the signs are not ends in themselves but witnesses that confirm the resurrection proclamation.
2.44: pantes de hoi pisteuontes ēsan epi to auto kai eichon hapanta koina (pantes de hoi pisteuontes ēsan epi to auto kai eichon hapanta koina, 'all the believers were together and had all things in common'): epi to auto ('in the same place') is a phrase that recurs in Acts for community assembly (1:15; 2:1, 44, 47). Koina (common/shared) is the Hellenistic ideal of true friendship; Luke presents the Jerusalem church as the community where the friendship-ideal is actually achieved — not communism by mandate but generosity by Spirit-prompted love.
2.45: epipraskōn ktēmata kai hyparcheis (epipraskon ktēmata kai hyparcheis, 'they were selling possessions and property'): the imperfects (epipraskōn, diemerization) indicate ongoing, repeated action as needs arose — not a single distribution but a habitual practice. Acts 5:4 confirms that ownership was retained; the selling was voluntary and ad hoc. The pastoral ideal here is the complete availability of property to meet need, not the abolition of private ownership.
2.46: kath' hēmeran te proskarterountes homothymadon en tō hierō (kath' hēmeran te proskarterountes homothymadon en tō hierō, 'daily continuing with one accord in the temple'): proskartereō (continue steadfastly, devote oneself to) is one of Luke's characteristic discipleship verbs (1:14; 2:42). The community maintains both temple (public Jewish worship) and house-table (private Eucharistic fellowship) — not yet separated from Judaism but already distinctively new. agoalliasis (agalliasis, exultant joy): the eschatological joy of those who know the Messiah has come and the Spirit has arrived.
2.47: echontes charin pros holon ton laon (echontes charin pros holon ton laon, 'having favor with all the people'): charis (grace/favor) here means social goodwill — the community's generosity, worship life, and miracle-attestation created favorable public impression. ho de kyrios prosetithei tous sōzomenous ('the Lord added those who were being saved'): the divine passive continues — salvation and addition to the community is the Lord's act, not merely the apostles'. The imperfect prosetithei (was adding) indicates continuous daily growth.

3.15: archegos tes zoes (archēgon tēs zōēs): archēgos means pioneer, founder, or champion — one who blazes a trail others follow. In Heb 2:10 and 12:2, Christ is archēgos of salvation and faith. Peter's phrase is therefore not merely 'author of life' (as if life were a text Jesus wrote) but 'pioneer of life' — the one who broke through death to open the way. The audience had killed the pioneer of life; God reversed the verdict by resurrection. The irony is Luke's sharpest Christological paradox in Acts.
3.22: propheten ... hos eme (prophētēn ... hōs eme): Peter quotes Deut 18:15 and links it to Jesus. The 'prophet like Moses' in Deuteronomic expectation was to bring a new word from YHWH with authority like the Sinai revelation. The rabbinic tradition expected this prophet at the eschaton (1QS 9:11 — the Qumran community awaited the prophet along with the Messiah). Peter claims the eschatological prophet has arrived: the new Moses not merely like him but surpassing him.
3.1: tēn hōran tēs proseuchēs tēn enatēn (tēn hōran tēs proseuchēs tēn enatēn, 'the hour of prayer, the ninth hour'): 3 p.m., the hour of the minchah offering and one of the three daily prayer times (Ps 55:17). The miracle happens within Israel's structured worship life, not outside it; Peter and John are observant Jews who continue temple practice. The crucifixion was at the sixth hour (noon), the darkness lasted until the ninth hour (Luke 23:44-46) — the hour of prayer is also the hour of Christ's death.
3.2: pros tēn thyran tou hierou tēn legomenēn Hōraian (pros tēn thyran tou hierou tēn legomenēn Hōraian, 'at the temple gate called Beautiful'): the gate's identity is disputed (possibly the Nicanor Gate or the Eastern Gate); Hōraia (beautiful) may preserve a punning connection with the healing — hōra (hour, right time) as well as beauty. Beggars positioned themselves where worshippers would feel obligated to give; the gate threshold was the liminal space between the clean and the common.
3.3: eleēmosynēn labein (eleēmosynēn labein, 'to receive alms'): eleēmosyne is 'merciful giving', derived from eleos (mercy/lovingkindness = chesed). Almsgiving at the temple gate was a standard piety practice (Tob 4:7-11; Sir 35:22); the man's request is within the normal social expectation. What he receives exceeds what he asked by the kind of ratio that characterizes the kingdom economy throughout Luke-Acts.
3.4: atenisas de Petros eis auton (atenisas de Petros eis auton, 'having fixed his gaze on him'): atenizō (to gaze fixedly) is a Lukan characteristic (Acts 1:10; 3:12; 6:15; 7:55; 10:4; 11:6; 13:9; 14:9); it indicates the focused attention that precedes a significant act or revelation. Peter's 'look at us' (blepson eis hēmas) is a command to the beggar to attend — to shift from peripheral passivity to full engagement before the gift is given.
3.5: epeiches autois prosdokōn ti par' autōn labein (epeiches autois prosdokōn ti par' autōn labein, 'he gave them attention, expecting to receive something from them'): prosdokaō (expect, await) is the same word used for eschatological expectation in Luke-Acts. The man's expectation is perfectly reasonable and even necessary to the miracle — he must look up and receive. What he will receive is something other than what he expects; the kingdom repeatedly delivers the unexpected within genuine expectation.
3.6: argyrion kai chrysion ouch hyparchei moi (argyrion kai chrysion ouch hyparchei moi, 'silver and gold do not belong to me'): Peter's declaration is not poverty-complaint but Christological redirection; what he has is incomparably greater than what was asked. en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou ('in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth'): the healing is not Peter's own power but the authority invested in the name — the name that carries the person, character, and power of the risen Christ.
3.7: ai baseis autou kai ta sphydra estereothesan (hai baseis autou kai ta sphydra estereōthēsan, 'his feet and ankles were made firm/strong'): Luke's medical precision — baseis (feet/soles) and sphydra (ankles) are the specific anatomical terms for the parts that had been congenitally weak. Stereōō (make firm/solid) is used for the spiritual strengthening of faith (Acts 16:5) and here for physical restoration; the solidity given to the ankles mirrors the spiritual solidity that characterizes the healed community.
3.8: exallomenos estē kai periepatei kai eisēlthen syn autois eis to hieron peripatōn kai hallomenos (exallomenos estē kai periepatei, 'leaping up he stood and walked'): the verbs pile up in an exuberant verbal sequence — leaping, standing, walking, entering, walking, leaping, praising. The man who had never walked is now celebrating in the very temple from which his condition had excluded him from full participation. The healing is simultaneously physical restoration and social-religious reintegration.
3.9: eidon pantes hoi laoi auton peripatounta kai ainounta ton theon ('all the people saw him walking and praising God'): the witnessing community (pantes hoi laoi) is the necessary context for the miracle's sign-function. The healing is not a private benefit but a public declaration; the praising (ainounta) directs the crowd's attention to God, not to Peter. Luke's consistent pattern: signs point beyond themselves to the God who acts through human agents.
3.10: plēsthēsan thambous kai ekstaselōs (eplēsthēsan thambous kai ekstaselōs, 'they were filled with amazement and wonder'): thampos (amazement) and ekstasis (astonishment, being outside oneself) together describe the overwhelmed response to the unexpected divine act. The same man they had seen begging at the Beautiful Gate is now walking — the specificity of the recognition ('this is the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate') intensifies the miracle's impact: the sign is verifiable.
3.11: Stoa Solomōntos (Stoā Solomōntos, 'Solomon's Colonnade'): the eastern portico of the temple precincts, mentioned also in John 10:23; it became the preferred location for the Jerusalem church's public teaching (Acts 5:12). The colonnade's association with Solomon evokes the Davidic-temple tradition; the church teaching under Solomon's Colonnade is the community of the new covenant gathered in the place that symbolized David's dynastic promise.
3.12: ti atenidzete hēmin (ti atenidzete hēmin, 'why do you stare at us'): Peter's first words deflect the crowd's attention from the apostles to God; the same deflection recurs when the crowd at Lystra tries to worship Paul and Barnabas (14:14-15). idia dynamei ē eusebeia ('our own power or piety'): dynamis (power) and eusebeia (piety/godliness) are the two things Peter explicitly denies as the source — rejecting both the thaumaturgical and the meritorious explanations.
3.13: ho theos Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb ('the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob'): Peter's sermon opens with the Exodus formula (Exod 3:6, 15; 4:5) — the God who speaks in Pentecost is the same covenant God who heard Israel in Egypt. pais (pais, 'servant/child'): the ambiguous Greek term encompasses both 'servant' (LXX Isa 52-53) and 'child/son'; the Isaianic servant-connotation is primary but the sonship nuance is present. Peter uses pais four times in Acts 3-4 as his primary Christological category.
3.14: ton hagion kai dikaion (ton hagion kai dikaion, 'the Holy and Righteous One'): both are divine-quality terms applied to Jesus. Hagios (holy, set apart for God) was the demon's identification at Capernaum (Luke 4:34) and Gabriel's announcement (Luke 1:35); dikaios (righteous) evokes Isa 53:11's righteous Servant. The demand to release a murderer (andra phonea) in exchange for the Holy and Righteous One is the ultimate moral paradox of the trial.
3.16: epi tē pistei tou onomatos autou (epi tē pistei tou onomatos autou, 'on the faith of his name'): the grammatical syntax is compressed — it is debated whether the faith is the man's faith in Jesus, or the faith that Jesus's name itself exercises. Both readings have exegetical defenders; the most natural reading is that the faith-channel (epi tē pistei) through which the name's power flows includes the healed man's own response. Holoklaesia (complete soundness) is a hapax legomenon — total restoration, nothing lacking.
3.17: oid' hoti kata agnoian epraxate (oid' hoti kata agnoian epraxate, 'I know that in ignorance you acted'): agnoia (ignorance) is a mitigating category in both Jewish and Roman law. Peter's attribution is pastoral: the audience present at the sermon is not the Sanhedrin that deliberated but the crowd that followed the wave of religious pressure. The Lev 4:2/Num 15:22-29 distinction between sins of ignorance and deliberate rebellion underlies the appeal — ignorance opens the door to repentance that deliberate sin would close.
3.18: prokatanggeilas dia stomatos pantōn tōn prophētōn pathein ton Christon autou (prokatanggeilas dia stomatos pantōn tōn prophētōn pathein ton Christon autou, 'having foretold through the mouth of all his prophets that his Messiah would suffer'): the universal prophetic witness to a suffering Messiah is Peter's interpretive claim — all the prophets collectively pointed to the passion. The MKT's 'all his prophets' (dia stomatos pantōn) emphasizes comprehensiveness; no single prophet is named because the whole canonical trajectory, not a single text, is the ground of the claim.
3.19: exaleiphthēnai hymōn hai hamartiai (exaleiphthēnai hymōn hai hamartiai, 'your sins may be wiped out'): exaleiphō (blot out, erase) is the vocabulary of erasing a written record — the metaphor of sin as debt that is cancelled. The same word in Col 2:14 for the 'certificate of debt' nailed to the cross. kairoi anapsyxeōs (kairoi anapsyxeōs, 'times of refreshing'): anapsyxis (refreshment, respite) is a rare word; it describes the cooling relief of shade in the heat (LXX Exod 8:15). The messianic era is framed as eschatological refreshment — relief from the weight of the old order.
3.20: ton prokecheiri smenon hymin Christon Iēsoun (ton prokecheirismo menon hymin Christon Iēsoun, 'the Messiah appointed for you — even Jesus'): procheirizomai (appoint beforehand, designate) implies prior divine action; the Messiah was designated for Israel in particular. The syntax is striking: 'send the Christ, who has been designated for you, even Jesus' — the definite article and the personal name confirm that the 'designated Messiah' and 'Jesus of Nazareth' are identical.
3.21: apokatastaseōs pantōn (apokatastaseōs pantōn, 'restoration of all things'): apokatastasis appears only here in the NT but was an important eschatological category in Jewish thought (Mal 4:6; Isa 65:17-25; Ezek 36-37). The cosmic scope ('all things') goes beyond mere national restoration to include the new creation. The heaven's must receive (dei ... ouranou ... dexasthai) Christ until this restoration — ascension and parousia are the brackets of the eschatological interval in which the church lives and witnesses.
3.23: eksolethreuthēsetai ek tou laou (eksoleuthreuthēsetai ek tou laou, 'will be utterly destroyed from the people'): the verb combines ex- (completeness) with olothreuo (destroy) for total exclusion — the LXX's intensified karet penalty (Lev 23:29). Peter applies the Deuteronomic prophet-rejection penalty (Deut 18:19) to refusal of Jesus; to reject the prophet-like-Moses is to put oneself outside the covenant community.
3.24: apo Samouēl kai tōn kathexēs (apo Samouēl kai tōn kathexēs, 'from Samuel and those following in order'): Samuel is chosen as the first in the sequence of prophets who 'foretold these days'; he is the first writing-adjacent prophet to speak specifically about David and the Davidic dynasty. The kathexēs (in order, successively) implies a continuous prophetic chain from Samuel through the Major and Minor Prophets — all pointing toward the Christ who has now arrived.
3.25: huioi tōn prophētōn kai tēs diathēkēs (huioi tōn prophētōn kai tēs diathēkēs, 'sons of the prophets and of the covenant'): the double huioi (sons) is a Semitic idiom for members of a group or inheritors of a tradition. The audience stands within the prophetic inheritance (they are heirs of the Scriptures Peter has been citing) and within the Abrahamic covenant (diathēkē here is the covenant of Gen 12:3; 22:18). The gospel comes 'to you first' (v.26) because the covenant priority is Israel's.
3.26: anastēsas ho theos ton paida autou (anastēsas ho theos ton paida autou, 'God having raised up his servant'): anastēsas (having raised up) can refer either to resurrection or to the OT idiom 'raise up a prophet' (Deut 18:15, 18); the ambiguity may be intentional — both senses are present. Apestellen (sent): the Servant is sent specifically to bless Israel first (protōn), by turning each person from wickedness. Repentance is the specific content of the blessing.

4.12: ouk estin en allo oudeni he soteria (ouk estin en allō oudeni hē sōtēria): 'in no other is there salvation' — Peter's exclusive soteriological claim before the Sanhedrin. onoma (onoma) carries Semitic weight: the name is the person, their character and authority concentrated. hypo ton ouranon ('under heaven'): the spatial comprehensiveness marks this as a universal claim, not a parochial Jewish sectarian position. The statement is the more remarkable for being made to the very council that approved Jesus's execution.
4.32: kardia kai psyche mia (kardia kai psychē mia, 'one heart and soul'): a Hellenistic friendship ideal (Aristotle's 'one soul in two bodies') now applied to the entire community. Luke's vocabulary of voluntary property-sharing (koina, all things in common; 2:44) differs structurally from the Qumran model where members transferred property to the community permanently. Jerusalem believers retained ownership (idios) and voluntarily brought proceeds as needs arose (5:4 confirms private property was not abolished).
4.1: strategos tou hierou (stratēgos tou hierou, 'captain of the temple') was the second-ranking authority in Jerusalem after the high priest, responsible for temple security and public order. His appearance alongside priests and Sadducees signals the full weight of the Jerusalem establishment mobilizing against the apostles; this is not a minor local altercation but an institutional response.
4.2: kataggellein en to Iesou ten anastasin ('proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead'): the preposition en is significant — the resurrection is proclaimed in Jesus, using him as the concrete instance that validates the resurrection reality. The Sadducees denied resurrection on principle; the apostles were not preaching a general philosophical resurrection but pointing to a specific man who had risen and thereby demonstrated the doctrine in historical fact.
4.3: eis teresin (eis tērēsin, 'into custody'): a technical legal term for preventive detention pending investigation, distinct from punitive imprisonment. Luke's precision — 'because it was already evening' — notes that the timing prevented immediate trial; Sanhedrin procedure required daylight hearings. The detail is historically credible and marks Luke's familiarity with Jewish juridical practice.
4.4: arithmos ton andron ('the number of the men'): Luke counts men (andrōn) specifically, following Jewish convention that tallied males for quorum and membership purposes. The number 5,000 represents a significant fraction of Jerusalem's population; the community has grown from 120 (1:15) to 3,000 (2:41) to 5,000 in rapid succession. Luke structures his growth notices as theological progress reports, not merely sociological data.
4.5: archontes kai presbyteroi kai grammateis ('rulers and elders and scribes'): the three constituent groups of the Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council of 71 members that held judicial, legislative, and administrative authority. Luke uses this tripartite formula deliberately — the identical body that condemned Jesus is now summoned against his apostles. The narrative parallelism is intentional: the disciples face the same tribunal that their Lord faced.
4.6: Annas had been deposed as high priest by the Romans in AD 15 but retained enormous personal authority and the loyalty of the priestly aristocracy; his sons and son-in-law Caiaphas (still serving as high priest, cf. Luke 3:2; John 18:13) constituted a dynastic priestly faction. Luke's list of names signals historical specificity and suggests access to accurate sources about Jerusalem's power structure in the 30s.
4.7: en poia dynamei e en poio onomati ('by what power or by what name'): the two questions are functionally equivalent in Jewish thought — the shem (name) was the concentrated reality and authority of a person, so action performed 'in a name' was action powered by that name's authority. The Sanhedrin wants to know whose delegated power the apostles have invoked, assuming some authorized intermediary. Peter's answer (v.10) uses precisely this framework but names a name they did not authorize.
4.8: plesthe Pneumatos Hagiou (plēstheis Pneumatos Hagiou, 'filled with the Holy Spirit'): Luke's aorist passive marks this as a fresh, specific endowment for this occasion, not simply the ongoing condition of 2:4. Peter before the Sanhedrin enacts the promise of Luke 12:11-12 ('when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself... the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say'). The fulfillment is immediate and precise.
4.9: euergesia (euergesia, 'good deed/act of benefaction'): Peter uses the standard Hellenistic vocabulary of public benefaction — the euergetēs (benefactor) was a patron whose good deeds benefited the city. By framing the healing as a euergesia, Peter subtly argues within the council's own civic framework: they are prosecuting a case of public benefit, not public harm. Luke's Greek shows Peter adapting register to his audience.
4.10: en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou ('in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene'): the full messianic title with geographic qualifier. Nazōraios in Acts (2:22; 3:6; 4:10; 6:14; 22:8; 24:5; 26:9) maintains continuity with the ministry of Jesus of Nazareth against any tendency to abstract the risen Christ from his historical particularity. The name is simultaneously claim, invocation, and challenge: the council crucified this man, and God raised him.
4.11: ho lithos ho exouthenetheis ... eis kephalēn gōnias (Ps 118:22): Peter applies the psalm directly to the council. Kephalē gōnias ('head of corner') is disputed — either the capstone completing an arch or the massive foundation stone at the base of a corner. Either meaning emphasizes indispensability: the stone the builders discarded is the one on which the whole structure depends. The quotation reframes the crucifixion as a builder's error that God corrected by resurrection-exaltation.
4.13: agrammatos kai idiōtai ('unlettered and untrained'): agrammatos means lacking formal scribal education — the ability to read, interpret, and teach Torah in the scribal tradition. Idiōtēs is a layperson relative to a professional; in rhetorical contexts it means an untrained speaker vs a trained orator. The council's astonishment is that these men argue with force and coherence despite lacking the credentials that would normally explain such performance. Their only explanation is the training of Jesus (v.13b).
4.14: theōrountes (theōrountes, 'seeing/beholding') is Luke's vocabulary for significant observation — more than mere glancing. The healed man's physical presence as a living exhibit makes refutation impossible; any argument the council might construct against the healing's reality is undercut by the 40-year-old man standing with his healed limbs on display. Luke notes the council's rhetorical problem: evidence stood in front of them.
4.15: The Sanhedrin's private deliberation (keleusantes autous exō tou synedriou apelthein) is a narrative concession to realism: Luke acknowledges he cannot have attended. The detail suggests either that a participant later disclosed the discussion (a sympathizer like Nicodemus or the Gamaliel of ch.5) or that the substance of the decision became known from its public outcome. Luke does not claim omniscience; he reports what became known.
4.16: semeion gnōston ('notable sign'): the council's internal framing is revealing — they accept the sign's factual reality (gnōston, 'known/evident') but treat it as a political problem rather than a theological datum. This is the pattern Luke identifies as culpable unbelief: those with sufficient evidence who choose political calculation over truth. Their question 'what shall we do?' (v.16) echoes the crowd's question in 2:37, but leads to suppression rather than repentance.
4.17: apeilē (apeilē, 'threat') + me phthenggesthai ('not to utter'): phthenggesthai is a significant word — used in LXX for prophetic utterance (Mic 5:12; Ezek 13:9; Ps 19:3) and by Luke in 2:4 and 2:14 for inspired Pentecost speech. The council's command to stop phthenggesthai is unknowingly a command to cease prophetic proclamation. Luke's word choice signals that the apostles' speech has a higher category — suppressing it is suppressing prophecy.
4.18: kathōlos ('at all/entirely'): the absolute prohibition — not merely reduced teaching but a complete ban on speaking or teaching in this name. The council's overreach establishes the conflict of loyalties that v.19-20 will address. The command is total, which is why Peter's answer (dei, 'it is necessary') must be equally total: divine necessity overrides human prohibition.
4.19: ei dikaion estin ('whether it is just'): Peter invokes the council's own standards. In Greek political philosophy and Jewish legal tradition, obedience to unjust commands was not obligatory. Peter does not refuse outright but poses the logical prior question — is this command lawful? He then appeals to God's court, leaving the evaluation to the audience. The argument has Socratic precedent (Plato, Apology 29D) and Jewish martyr tradition (2 Macc 7).
4.20: ou dynametha ('we are not able'): the phrase carries both epistemic force (we cannot deny what we have seen) and volitional force (we cannot choose to keep silent about it). The apostles do not merely say 'we will not' but 'we cannot' — their witness is constrained by what they have directly seen and heard (ha eidamen kai ēkousamen). Eyewitness testimony is their irreducible ground; it cannot be surrendered by command.
4.21: kolasosin (kolasōsin, 'to punish/chastise'): the council wanted to punish the apostles but was constrained by public opinion (ton laon). Luke's narrative repeatedly notes this popular-political check on official action (cf. 5:26; Luke 20:6; 22:2). Doxazon ('glorifying') — the people's response to the healing is praise for God, precisely what the council cannot argue against without alienating the population they need.
4.22: Luke's parenthetical note that the healed man was over forty years old functions as evidentiary detail: the duration of the disability eliminates the possibility of spontaneous healing or misdiagnosis. The Sanhedrin cannot argue statistical coincidence against a man congenitally lame from birth (3:2) for over four decades. Luke's attention to ages throughout Acts (the man here; Paul in various passages) reflects Hellenistic historical writing's use of concrete detail to establish credibility.
4.23: tous idious ('their own people'): the phrase signals communal identity — the apostles' primary accountability is to their own community rather than the council. Luke's use of idios in Acts (2:6; 4:23, 32; 24:24; 25:19) consistently marks belonging. The community gathers to hear the report and turns immediately to prayer, modeling the pattern of apostolic community: shared experience leads to corporate prayer leads to renewed mission.
4.24: Despota (Despota, 'Sovereign Master'): less frequent than Kyrios, this address emphasizes absolute sovereignty and ownership — the relationship between a master and his household. The prayer opens by anchoring God's authority in creation (citing Ps 146:6 / Exod 20:11) before petitioning him to act in history. The logic is implicit: if you made all things, you govern all things; therefore the council's opposition is within your sovereign management.
4.25: pais (pais, 'servant/child') is applied to David (v.25) and Jesus (v.27, 30). The word bridges the Davidic covenant and the Servant Songs — it can mean both 'son' (in the sense of Ps 2) and 'servant' (in the sense of Isa 52-53). Luke's consistent application of pais to Jesus (3:13, 26; 4:27, 30) is his preferred Servant-Christology term in these early speeches, keeping the dual resonance of royal son and suffering servant in view simultaneously.
4.26: The community's prayer cites Ps 2:1-2 (parestathesan hoi basileis tēs gēs kai hoi archontes synēchthēsan epi to auto kata tou kyriou kai kata tou christou autou) and then immediately identifies its historical referent in vv.27-28. This is the apostolic hermeneutic in concentrated form: take a psalm, identify its referent as Christ, then read the psalm's terms as the grid for reading history. Herod and Pilate are the 'kings and rulers' of Ps 2; the Gentiles and Israel are the nations; the conspiracy was the Passion.
4.27: aleipsas (hōn echrisen, 'whom you anointed'): the verb chriō (to anoint) links Jesus directly to the title Christos (the Anointed One, Messiah). The anointing referred to may be the Spirit's descent at baptism (Luke 3:22; 4:18 — 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me'), the incarnation itself, or the resurrection-exaltation. The prayer assumes that Ps 2's royal anointing has been applied to Jesus, making every subsequent opposition to Jesus a fulfillment of the psalm's scenario.
4.28: proōrisen (proōrisen, 'foreordained/predetermined'): Luke's strongest statement of divine determinism in Acts, alongside 2:23 ('delivered up by the definite plan and foreknowledge of God'). The council's decision, Pilate's capitulation, Herod's involvement — all were 'predetermined by your hand and your counsel' (tē sē boulē). This is not fatalism that removes human responsibility (the actors are still culpable) but providence that guarantees divine purpose through human free action. The tension is held without resolution, as in the OT wisdom tradition.
4.29: meta parresias pases (meta parrēsias pasēs, 'with all boldness'): parrēsia (boldness, freedom of speech) is a key term in Acts (2:29; 4:13, 29, 31; 9:27, 28; 13:46; 14:3; 18:26; 19:8; 26:26; 28:31). In Hellenistic political philosophy it was the freedom of a citizen to speak freely before the assembly; in Acts it is the Spirit-given freedom to speak the gospel before any audience, regardless of threat. The community does not pray for deliverance from opposition but for boldness to continue speaking within it.
4.30: en tō ekteinein se ten cheira sou ('as you stretch out your hand'): the divine hand as agent of miraculous power is OT idiom (Exod 3:20; 9:15; Deut 26:8; Ps 138:7). The prayer frames the apostles' healing ministry as God's own hand working through them — they are instruments, not autonomous agents. sēmeia kai terata ('signs and wonders') is the standard OT theophany-vocabulary (Exod 7:3; Deut 4:34; 6:22; 7:19; Ps 78:43) now applied to apostolic ministry.
4.31: esaleuthē ho topos ('the place was shaken'): physical phenomena marking divine response to prayer echoes OT theophanies (Exod 19:18 — Sinai shaking at the divine descent; Isa 6:4 — the temple threshold shaking at the divine glory). The community is refilled with the Spirit (eplēsthēsan hapantes) — Luke does not treat Pentecost as a once-for-all endowment never needing renewal. The Spirit is continuously given in fresh measure for fresh occasions of witness.
4.33: megale charis en epi pantas autous ('great grace was upon them all'): charis in Luke-Acts carries both divine favor (God's gracious disposition) and the concrete expression of that favor in community life. The phrase functions as a summary theological assessment of the community: their unity, generosity, and witness are the marks of divine charis resting on them. The construction echoes similar summaries in Luke's Gospel (Luke 2:40 of the child Jesus — 'the grace of God was upon him').
4.34: ouk en endees tis en autois ('there was not a needy person among them'): the exact fulfillment of Deut 15:4 LXX ('there shall be no poor among you') — a striking echo that Luke almost certainly intends. The Deuteronomic ideal of a community without poverty, which Israel never fully realized in the land, is enacted in the Jerusalem church. Luke presents the community's economic life as the actualization of covenantal promise.
4.35: para tous podas ton apostolōn ('at the feet of the apostles'): the gesture of placing something at someone's feet is a posture of voluntary submission and recognition of authority. The Twelve receive and administer the common fund — not by compulsion but by communal agreement. The prepositional phrase establishes apostolic authority as the structural center of the community's economic life, a detail that prepares for the crisis of ch.5 and the administrative expansion of ch.6.
4.36: pais paraklēseōs (huios paraklēseōs, 'son of encouragement'): the Semitic bar- construction (son of X = characterized by X) indicates that Barnabas was known as an encourager — one who exhorted, consoled, and strengthened others. Paraklēsis is the same word-family as paraklētos (the Advocate/Spirit, John 14:16); here it identifies a human ministry of comfort and exhortation. Luke introduces Barnabas here because he will become Paul's first significant partner in mission (9:27; 11:22-30; 13-15).
4.37: The Barnabas episode (hyparchontos autō agrou, 'having a field') functions as the positive exemplar that immediately precedes the Ananias and Sapphira counter-example of ch.5. Luke's narrative pairs the genuine and the counterfeit: Barnabas's wholehearted surrender of the proceeds is the model; Ananias and Sapphira's deceptive partial surrender is the perversion. The field/land vocabulary links both narratives, making the contrast structural.

5.29: peitharchein dei theo mallon e anthropois (peitharchein dei theō mallon ē anthrōpois): 'we must obey God rather than human beings'. dei is divine necessity, not preference — the same dei that drove Jesus's entire ministry (Luke 2:49; 4:43; 9:22). peitharchein (obey authority) is the compound of peitho (persuade/trust) + archē (ruling authority), carrying the nuance that legitimate authority deserves compliance — but God's authority supersedes all human authority structures.
5.1: Hananias (Ananias) and Sappheira (Sapphira) — both Hebrew names. Luke records their names, unlike many anonymous figures in Acts, signaling that this event had historical specificity in community memory. The narrative introduces the community's first internal moral failure — not external persecution but internal deception. The threat to the church from within is as grave as the threat from the Sanhedrin without.
5.2: enosphisato (enosphisato, 'he embezzled/misappropriated'): the same word used in the LXX of Josh 7:1 (enosphisato) for Achan's misappropriation of devoted things from Jericho's spoils. The resonance is almost certainly deliberate: Achan's sin introduced covenant violation into Israel immediately after entry into the land; Ananias's sin introduces covenant violation into the church immediately after Pentecost. Both disrupted a new covenantal beginning; both were exposed by divine revelation.
5.3: eplērōsen ho Satanas tēn kardian sou ('Satan has filled your heart'): the contrast with Spirit-filling is explicit — Pentecost filled the community with the Holy Spirit (2:4; 4:31); Ananias has been filled by Satan. The same verb (plēroō), the same location (the heart), the opposite agent and direction. Deception is not merely moral failure but a form of demonic collaboration. pseudesthai ('to lie'): lying to the Spirit is lying to God (v.4) — Peter's equation affirms the Spirit's full divinity.
5.4: ouchi menon soi emenen kai prathen en sē exousia hypērchen: Peter's double assertion — while it remained unsold it was yours; after it was sold the proceeds were at your disposal — confirms that the Jerusalem community did not require communal property. The sin was not keeping the money but lying about having given it all. The voluntary nature of the sharing is essential to understanding the community's economic ethic: it was genuine gift, not tax.
5.5: ekpneō (ekpneusas, 'he breathed his last/expired'): Luke uses clinical vocabulary — the same verb he uses for Jesus's death in Luke 23:46. The sudden death is not reported as miraculous in the ordinary sense; it is a divine judicial act. phobos megas ('great fear'): the fear that follows Ananias's death and then Sapphira's (v.11) is not terror but awe — the recognition that the community inhabits a space where divine holiness is operative and cannot be trifled with.
5.6: synesteilantes (systeilan, 'wrapping/shrouding'): the young men (hoi neōteroi) act immediately without deliberation. The speed of the burial is consistent with Jewish practice (burial before sundown) and adds to the solemnity of the narrative — there is no interval for appeal or reconsideration. Luke's brevity here reflects the event's shock value in community memory.
5.7: hōs hōrōn triōn diastēma ('after about three hours'): Luke's chronological precision creates dramatic irony — Sapphira has been going about her day unaware of her husband's death, and comes in after three hours to face Peter's question. The interval is long enough that the burial is complete but short enough that she still expects to reunite with her husband. Luke's narrative technique here produces the same tension as a trial scene.
5.8: Peter's question — tosoutou to chorion apedosthe ('did you sell the land for so much?') — is not a request for information but a forensic trap. Peter already knows the answer (through the Spirit's revelation, v.3). He gives Sapphira the opportunity to tell the truth; her confirmation of the lie is her own self-condemnation. The interrogation follows the pattern of divine testing in the OT (Gen 3:9-13; 4:9), where God questions not from ignorance but to expose what the heart already holds.
5.9: peirazo (peirasai, 'to test'): Peter's charge — 'why did you conspire to test the Spirit of the Lord?' — places the Ananias and Sapphira incident in the category of Israel's wilderness murmuring and testing of God (Exod 17:2, 7; Ps 78:18, 56; 95:9; 106:14). Testing the Spirit of the Lord was the characteristic sin of the wilderness generation that died before reaching the land; the same pattern is now enacted at the beginning of the new covenant community.
5.10: Sapphira's immediate death (epesen de parachrēma pros tous podas autou kai exepsyxen, 'immediately she fell at his feet and expired') parallels her husband's death structurally. Luke notes both that she fell 'at his feet' (cf. the apostles' feet as locus of authority in 4:35, 37) and that the same young men came and buried her beside her husband. The symmetry is deliberate: the couple acted together in deception; they died together in judgment.
5.11: ekklēsia (ekklēsia): this is the first unambiguous use of ekklēsia in Acts (the word appears in variant readings at 2:47 but is textually uncertain there). The word meant the civic assembly of citizens in Hellenistic cities but carried OT weight as the LXX translation of qahal (the assembly of Israel). Luke's application of ekklēsia to the messianic community identifies them as the renewed assembly of Israel, the covenant people gathered in the new exodus.
5.12: sēmeia kai terata polla ('many signs and wonders'): the third summary of apostolic miracles (2:43; 4:30; 5:12). Luke's repetition establishes a rhythm: the word is proclaimed, the Spirit acts in power, the community grows, opposition arises. Stoa tou Solomōnos ('Solomon's Porch'): the eastern colonnade of the temple complex (cf. John 10:23); the apostles used a public, recognizable location, not a private house, for teaching.
5.13: oudeis etolma kollasthai autois ('no one else dared to join them'): kollaō (attach oneself, join closely) is the same word used for the community's adherence to one another (v.36 in a different context). The awe following the Ananias and Sapphira judgment created a selective barrier: casual seekers kept their distance while those who genuinely believed were added (v.14). Luke presents holy fear as a purifying filter on membership, not a deterrent to genuine conversion.
5.14: prosetithento pisteuontes tō kyriō ('believers were added to the Lord'): the community's growth is consistently described as addition to the Lord (2:47; 5:14; 11:24) — not merely to the group — signaling that the community's center of gravity is personal relationship to the risen Christ. The explicit mention of both men and women (andrōn te kai gynaikōn) after Pentecost's inclusive outpouring (Joel 2:28-29) is Luke's narrative confirmation that gender is not a barrier to membership.
5.15: hina erchomenou Petrou kan hē skia episkiasē ('so that even Peter's shadow might fall on some of them'): Luke does not affirm whether the shadow healings worked; he reports the crowd's expectation. The pattern echoes the healings through Paul's handkerchiefs and aprons in 19:12 — the divine power operating through physical media associated with the apostle. Both passages reflect popular faith that God worked through proximity to his agents, as he worked through Elisha's bones (2 Kgs 13:21).
5.16: ochloi ('crowds'): the geographic expansion — from Jerusalem to surrounding towns — follows the Acts 1:8 pattern. The mission radiates outward naturally, not yet through intentional missionary strategy but through the reputation of the apostolic ministry. ochloumenous hypo pneumatōn akathartōn ('tormented by unclean spirits'): Luke's healing summaries always include exorcism alongside physical healing, maintaining the integrated Synoptic pattern of Jesus's ministry.
5.17: anastropheis zēlou (plēsthēnai zēlou, 'filled with jealousy'): the same term — filled (plēroō) — used for Spirit-filling now describes the jealousy that animates the high priest. The bitter irony is structural: the high priest, who should lead the community in discerning God's work, is filled with the anti-Spirit of jealousy while the apostles are filled with the Holy Spirit. Luke's vocabulary creates a pointed comparison.
5.18: epebalan tas cheiras ('laid their hands on'): the hostile laying on of hands contrasts with the community's gesture of commissioning and blessing (6:6; 13:3). The same physical act carries opposite meanings depending on the agent and intention. Luke's narrative sophistication regularly exploits these lexical contrasts to highlight the divided response to the apostolic mission.
5.19: angelos de Kyriou ('an angel of the Lord'): the divine rescue pattern — imprisonment followed by miraculous release — is established here (cf. 12:7-11; 16:26). Luke presents the angel as divine agent, not metaphor; the detail about opening locked doors (v.23) makes the miraculous nature explicit. The angel's message is not merely release but re-commission: 'go and speak in the temple all the words of this life' (v.20).
5.20: ta rhēmata tēs zōēs tautēs ('the words of this life'): the phrase 'this life' (tautēs) refers to the resurrection life — the eschatological life inaugurated by Christ's resurrection that is now available and is the content of the apostolic proclamation. The command returns the apostles immediately to the place of danger (the temple courts) rather than directing them to safety — witness continues in the theater of opposition.
5.21: gerousion (gerousian): a near-synonym for the Sanhedrin, literally 'assembly of elders' (cf. the Hebrew ziqnei Israel). Luke's doubling — 'Sanhedrin and all the senate of the sons of Israel' — may indicate the full formal convening of all associated leadership bodies, or may be literary intensification to indicate the comprehensive nature of the official response. Either way, it signals the maximum institutional weight arrayed against the apostles.
5.22: The officers' discovery of the empty prison — euron autous ou en tē phylakē ('they did not find them in the prison') — is narrated without explanation at this point; Luke delays the angelic rescue account structurally (it is implied but not yet stated). The sequence creates a moment of dramatic suspense: the officials know the prison is secure (v.23) but the apostles are absent. The resolution requires the theophanic framework of angelic intervention.
5.23: meta pasēs aspaleias kekleismenon ('locked with all security'): the guards' report emphasizes the physical security of the prison — doors locked, guards in place — to make the angelic release undeniable. Luke's detail serves an apologetic function: the release cannot be explained by bribery, guard negligence, or official connivance. The only available explanation is the miraculous intervention that the narrative has already identified (v.19).
5.24: diēporoun peri autōn ('they were perplexed about them'): diaporeō (be thoroughly perplexed) is Luke's term for the cognitive disruption that miraculous events produce in observers (cf. Luke 9:7; Acts 2:12; 10:17). The officials cannot fit the data into their interpretive framework; the prison is secure but empty. Luke presents such perplexity as the appropriate preliminary to genuine inquiry — it opens the question that only the gospel can answer.
5.25: The report — 'the men you put in prison are standing in the temple courts teaching the people' — is narrated at maximum dramatic compression. Luke does not explain the officers' emotional response; it is implied by the contrast: they locked the apostles away, and now find them publicly doing the exact thing they were imprisoned for doing. The apostles' obedience to the angel's command (v.20) is confirmed by the report.
5.26: ou meta bias ('without force/violence'): the captain's restraint reflects the same popular constraint noted in 4:21 — the authorities cannot act against the apostles openly without risking popular backlash. Luke's 'fear of being stoned' is a recognition that the crowd's sympathy for the apostles served as providential protection. The same dynamic will reappear in ch.21-26, where Paul navigates Roman and Jewish legal systems partly because of the crowd's divided loyalties.
5.27: The second appearance before the Sanhedrin parallels and escalates the first (ch.4). The formal setting — estēsan en tō synedriō ('they stood them in the Sanhedrin') — and the high priest's interrogation (eperōtēsen autous) reprises the earlier tribunal scene. Luke's narrative is building a pattern: the apostles are repeatedly arrested, repeatedly released, and repeatedly more bold, enacting the Ps 2 scenario of opposition that cannot ultimately prevail.
5.28: eplērōkate tēn Ierousalēm tēs didachēs hymōn ('you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching'): the accusation ironically confirms what Luke has been narrating — the gospel has indeed filled Jerusalem. The council's complaint is Luke's progress report. epagagein eph' hēmas to haima tou anthrōpou toutou ('bring this man's blood upon us'): an implicit acknowledgment of guilt; they recognize that the apostles are accusing them of Jesus's death, which they cannot flatly deny.
5.30: kremesantes epi xylou (kremasantes epi xylou, 'hanging on a tree'): the phrase evokes Deut 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a pole is under God's curse'). Paul makes the connection explicit in Gal 3:13 ('Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written: Cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree'). Luke's language here implies the same Deuteronomic background: Jesus bore the covenant curse, which is why God raised him from the dead rather than leaving him under it.
5.31: archēgon kai sōtēra (archēgon kai sōtēra, 'Pioneer and Savior'): the archēgos Christology continues from 3:15. A sōtēr (savior) in Hellenistic political usage was typically a king or general who delivered a city from enemies; Luke applies it to Jesus as the deliverer from sin and death. The pairing — Pioneer who goes first, Savior who brings others through — is Luke's comprehensive summary of Christ's redemptive work. metanoia and aphesis ('repentance' and 'forgiveness') are the twin gifts his exaltation makes available.
5.32: martyres again (cf. 1:8, 22; 2:32; 3:15; 4:33; 5:32): Luke's accumulating use of the witness-vocabulary emphasizes that apostolic testimony is grounded in personal encounter, not theological speculation. The Spirit's co-witness (kai to Pneuma to Hagion ho edōken ho theos tois peitharchousin autō) is significant: the Spirit testifies alongside the human witnesses, making the testimony doubly grounded — in eyewitness experience and in ongoing divine confirmation.
5.33: dieprionto (dieprionto, 'they were cut to the quick/sawn asunder'): the violent metaphor — literally 'to be sawn through' — describes the council's furious reaction. The word appears again in 7:54 at Stephen's speech. The council's rage stands in sharp contrast to the crowd's receptive fear (v.11) and prepares for Gamaliel's moderating intervention.
