5.34: Gamaliel: identified as a Pharisee and nomodidaskalos (teacher of the law), honored by all the people. The Gamaliel of Acts is almost certainly the Gamaliel I mentioned in later rabbinic tradition as a leading sage; Paul identifies himself as trained at Gamaliel's feet (22:3). Luke presents the Pharisees and Sadducees as divided in their response to the apostles — the Sadducees drive the persecution, while this leading Pharisee advocates restraint.
5.35: Gamaliel's speech addresses the Sanhedrin with the formal address andres Israēlitai ('men of Israel') and immediately counsels caution (prosechete heautois, 'take care for yourselves'). His appeal is to pragmatic historical precedent rather than theological principle — he does not assess whether the apostles' claims are true but whether suppression is strategically wise. Luke's inclusion of this speech is not a straightforward endorsement of Gamaliel's reasoning but a narrative device showing that even the council's own members could not unify against the apostles.
5.36: The Theudas reference creates a famous historical difficulty: Josephus mentions a Theudas who led a movement around AD 44-46, after the events of Acts 5. Either Luke's dating is imprecise, there were multiple figures named Theudas, or Luke has inverted the chronological sequence in constructing Gamaliel's speech. The historical problem does not undermine Luke's theological point but is worth noting for honest engagement with the text.
5.37: Judas the Galilean led a tax revolt at the census of AD 6 (Josephus, Antiquities 18.1.1), establishing the anti-Roman Zealot tradition. Gamaliel's point — that both movements dissolved when their leaders died — is historically accurate for Judas. His argument has the form of an analogy from recent history: if this is of human origin, it will follow the same pattern; if from God, you cannot stop it.
5.38: aphete autous ('leave them alone'): Gamaliel's counsel is restraint, not openness. His logic — ei estin ex anthrōpōn hē boulē hautē ē to ergon touto ('if this plan or work is from men') — is pragmatic probability-assessment, not theological discernment. The argument works as a rhetorical check on the council's anger without requiring Gamaliel to assess the apostles' claims. Luke does not endorse Gamaliel's methodology (it would apply equally to any movement that survived) but uses it to show that even the council's own traditions argued against violent suppression.
5.39: mēpote kai theomachoi heurethēte ('you might even be found fighting against God'): the theomachos (God-fighter) was a category of hubris in both Hellenistic and Jewish thought — the human who presumes to oppose divine action. Gamaliel's warning is the sharpest edge of his argument: if this is God's work, suppression is not merely futile but blasphemous. The council cannot be certain which it is; prudence therefore counsels restraint. The irony for Luke's reader is that it is God's work, making the council's subsequent flogging (v.40) a confirmed case of theomachic overreach.
5.40: mastigōsantes ('having flogged them'): the Sanhedrin did not follow Gamaliel's counsel completely — they flogged the apostles before releasing them. The Jewish judicial flogging was Deut 25:2-3's thirty-nine stripes (Paul refers to receiving this five times, 2 Cor 11:24). The flogging was intended as both punishment and deterrent; its actual effect (v.41) is the inverse of what was intended.
5.41: chairontes ('rejoicing'): the apostles' response to flogging is characterized by Luke as genuine joy, not stoic endurance. The grounds — hoti katēxiōthēsan atimasthēnai hyper tou onomatos ('that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the name') — invert the normal honor-shame calculus. In a society where public shame was devastating, the apostles experience their dishonor as a form of honor — sharing in their Lord's suffering, as Jesus had promised (Luke 6:22-23; 21:12-13). The beatitude is being enacted.
5.42: kath' hēmeran ('day by day') + kat' oikon ('from house to house') + en tō hierō ('in the temple'): Luke's closing summary describes the rhythm of apostolic ministry — daily, in both public space (temple) and private space (homes), never stopping (ouk epaūonto). The word 'teaching' (didaskontes) and 'proclaiming the good news' (euangelizomenoi) are paired; both catechesis of believers and evangelism of outsiders are continuous. The pattern will be disrupted only by the Hellenist controversy of ch.6.

7.55: hestota ek dexion tou theou (hestōta ek dexiōn tou theou, 'standing at the right hand of God'): every NT text on Christ's post-ascension position describes him seated (Ps 110:1; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1; Heb 1:3). Here alone he is standing. The contrast is intentional and has generated two main interpretations: (1) Christ rises to welcome Stephen the proto-martyr — a gesture of reception; (2) Christ stands as judge in the heavenly court to give favorable testimony on Stephen's behalf. Either reading shows the risen Christ responding actively to earthly events from his exalted position.
7.1: The high priest's question 'Are these charges true?' (ei tauta houtōs echei — lit. 'if these things have it thus') is the formal forensic opening that gives Stephen the floor for his defense-speech. Rather than defending himself, he delivers a salvation-history recapitulation — the longest speech in Acts. The MKT 'Are these charges true?' accurately renders the legal formula. Stephen's 'true' will be redefined through his speech: the charge that he speaks against Moses and the temple is true in a way that vindicates rather than condemns him.
7.2: 'The God of glory' (ho theos tēs doxēs) is a genitive of quality: God defined by his radiant self-manifestation. The same construction appears in Ps 29:3 LXX ('the voice of the LORD is upon the waters; the God of glory thunders'). The MKT 'God of glory appeared to our father Abraham' preserves the construction. 'Father Abraham' (tō patri hēmōn Abraam) acknowledges covenant-lineage while the speech will progressively question whether ethnic descent from Abraham guarantees covenant-faithfulness. 'In Mesopotamia, before he lived in Harran' — this pre-Harran call draws on Gen 15:7 rather than Gen 11:31, locating the call in Ur.
7.3: The divine command 'Leave your country and your people' quotes Gen 12:1 LXX (gē sou kai ek tēs syngenias sou) — 'your land and from your kindred.' The MKT 'go to the land I will show you' renders hēn an soi deixō accurately — the future subjunctive of divine direction: destination unknown until arrival. Stephen begins with the paradigm of obedient departure-in-faith as the characteristic form of covenant-response.
7.4: 'After the death of his father, God sent him (metōkisen auton) to this land' — metoikizō (to cause to migrate) is the key verb for Stephen's thesis about a God who is not confined to land or temple. God moves people; God himself moves. The MKT 'God sent him to this land where you are now living' adds the pointed 'where you are now living' (en hē hymeis nyn katoikeite) — a subtle contrast between the migrating Abraham and the land-settled accusers.
7.5: 'He gave him no inheritance here (klēronomian), not even enough ground to set his foot on (bēma podos)' — bēma podos echoes Deut 2:5 ('not even enough to set your foot on'). The God who promised the land to Abraham gave him none of it during his lifetime — the promise is eschatological, not immediately territorial. This is Stephen's first anti-temple-theology move: the God of Abraham is not tied to a territory because he fulfilled his promise beyond Abraham's earthly life.
7.6: 'God spoke to him in this way' (elalēsen de houtōs ho theos — lit. 'God spoke thus') introduces a near-quotation of Gen 15:13 LXX. 'Your descendants will be strangers in a country not their own' — paroikous (strangers, sojourners) is the key word: the patriarchs are defined as sojourners, not land-owners. The four-hundred-year figure follows the LXX of Gen 15:13 (not the 430 of Exod 12:40). The sojourner-status is the characteristic position of the covenant people.
7.7: 'The nation they serve as slaves' — the divine promise includes judgment on the enslaving nation and the subsequent worship at 'this place' (tō topō toutō — the same demonstrative used in the charge against Stephen, 6:13-14). Stephen's 'this place' is ambiguous: the original referent in Gen 15:14 is the promised land, not Jerusalem specifically, let alone the temple. The divine promise of worship-at-this-place predates any temple.
7.8: 'The covenant of circumcision' (diathēkēn peritomēs) — a genitive of content: the covenant whose sign/content is circumcision. Stephen names circumcision as the covenant-sign while the speech will conclude that the accusers are 'uncircumcised in heart' (v.51) — they have the sign without the reality. The patriarch-chain (Abraham → Isaac → Jacob → twelve patriarchs) establishes the covenant-lineage from which the Joseph story emerges.
7.9: 'The patriarchs were jealous (zēlōsantes) of Joseph' — zēloō carries both the positive sense (zealous for) and the negative sense (envious of); here it is clearly the negative: the brothers' jealousy drove them to sell Joseph into slavery. Stephen's speech consistently identifies the pattern of Israel rejecting God's appointed agents: they rejected Joseph, they rejected Moses (vv.27, 35, 39), they killed the prophets (v.52), and they killed Jesus. But God was with Joseph — the divine overruling of human rejection is the speech's counter-narrative.
7.10: 'He gave Joseph wisdom (sophia) and enabled him to gain the goodwill (charin) of Pharaoh' — charis here is rendered 'goodwill' in the MKT rather than 'grace' (disp=2: lit=grace, med=grace, tho=favor). The MKT opts for the social register of charis in a court context: the granting of favorable standing before a political superior. This is the correct contextual rendering — Gen 39:21 LXX uses the same word for Joseph finding favor in prison. The theological 'grace' sense would be an over-read here.
7.11: 'A famine struck all Egypt and Canaan' — the famine that drove Jacob's sons to Egypt is the providential mechanism of the Joseph story's resolution. 'Great suffering' (thlipsis megalē — great affliction) uses the same word used in Acts for the sufferings of the early church (8:1; 11:19). The ancestors' suffering in famine is the template for the community's suffering under persecution — both are moments where God's purpose is being accomplished despite (through) the tribulation.
7.12: 'When Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt' — Jacob sending 'our forefathers' (tous pateras hēmōn — our fathers) to Egypt continues the communal identification: Stephen speaks as a member of the covenant people, not as an outsider. The speech is Israel's own self-critique, not a Gentile's criticism from outside. The grain-in-Egypt (sition) that Israel's ancestors sought prefigures the spiritual food available where Israel's leadership did not expect it.
7.13: 'On their second visit, Joseph told his brothers who he was' — the anagnōrisis (recognition) of Joseph by his brothers on the second visit is the template for the recognition of Jesus by his disciples (and eventually Israel): rejected, sold for silver, hidden, then revealed to those who had rejected him. Stephen's audience has not yet made the 'second visit' that would bring recognition. The MKT 'Joseph told his brothers who he was' renders anegnōristhē Iōsēph tois adelphois autou — 'Joseph was made known to his brothers.'
7.14: 'Joseph sent for his father Jacob and his whole family, seventy-five in all' — the seventy-five figure follows the LXX of Gen 46:27 (which includes Joseph's Egyptian grandsons in the count) rather than the MT's seventy. Stephen quotes the LXX throughout his speech, the Scripture of diaspora Judaism and his audience in Acts. The family-number is a minor variant between LXX and MT traditions; Stephen follows the Greek Bible he and his audience used.
7.15: 'Then Jacob went down to Egypt, where he and our ancestors died' — the death of Jacob and the ancestors in Egypt is stated baldly. The patriarchs died outside the promised land. Their bones were eventually brought back (v.16), but the patriarchs themselves completed their lives as sojourners (paroikoi). Stephen's accumulating point: the foundational generation of the covenant community were sojourners who died outside the land and were still recipients of the covenant promises.
7.16: 'Their bodies were brought back to Shechem and placed in the tomb that Abraham had bought from the sons of Hamor' — Stephen conflates two OT burial traditions (Gen 50:13 — Jacob buried at Machpelah bought from Ephron; Josh 24:32 — Joseph buried at Shechem bought from Hamor's sons). Whether this is a speech-error, a textual tradition Stephen uses, or deliberate conflation for rhetorical purposes is debated. The MKT follows the text faithfully. The burial-at-Shechem motif is significant: Shechem, not Jerusalem, is where the patriarch-bones rest.
7.17: 'As the time drew near for God to fulfil his promise to Abraham' (hōs de ēngizen ho chronos tēs epangelias hēs ōmologēsen ho theos tō Abraam) — the promise-fulfillment is explicitly God's initiative: 'which God had guaranteed/sworn to Abraham' (hōmologēsen — confessed/sworn solemnly). The MKT 'fulfil his promise' renders epangelia accurately. The increase of the people is not their achievement but the organic fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise.
7.18: 'A new king, to whom Joseph meant nothing, came to power in Egypt' — the phrase 'who did not know Joseph' (hos ouk ēdei ton Iōsēph) quotes Exod 1:8 LXX. Institutional memory fails; the political successor to the regime Joseph served has no relationship with Joseph's legacy. Stephen's point: the covenant people's security in Egypt depended on the remembrance of Joseph; when that memory is lost, persecution follows. The pattern of a new power that forgets the previous generation's servant recurs in the speech.
7.19: 'He dealt treacherously with our people' (katastrophisato to genos hēmōn — deceived our people by cunning) — katastrophizomai means to deal craftily with; it appears only here in the NT. The MKT 'dealt treacherously' captures the cunning-exploitation sense. The abandonment of babies (ektheta — exposed infants, a common ancient practice of infant disposal) is the ultimate threat: the covenant people's continuity is at risk from Pharaoh's decree.
7.20: 'Moses was born, and he was no ordinary child' (asteios tō theō — lit. 'fair/beautiful to God' or 'pleasing to God') — the Hebraism asteios tō theō is a superlative idiom. Asteios means attractive, pleasing; tō theō is a dative of reference ('to God') used as an intensifier in LXX idiom: 'a city great to God' = 'an exceedingly great city' (Jon 3:3). The MKT 'no ordinary child' chooses readability over the literal divine-quality locution. The underlying claim: Moses's birth was marked with divine favor from the outset.
7.21: 'Pharaoh's daughter took him and brought him up as her own son' — the adoption of Moses by Pharaoh's daughter (anethepsato — reared as her own; the same word used for the rearing of children) is the reversal of the abandonment-decree: the very dynasty that ordered infant death raises the deliverer who will bring the dynasty down. The irony is structural in Exodus and Stephen's retelling amplifies it.
7.22: 'Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and was powerful in speech and action' — logos here (dynamios en logois kai ergois — lit. 'powerful in words and works') is the functional-practical sense of speech/word, not the Johannine Logos (disp=3: lit=word, tho=the Word). The MKT 'speech and action' correctly renders this as Moses's rhetorical and practical capability — a description of complete excellence in Egyptian court culture. That Moses was 'powerful in speech' directly contradicts Exod 4:10 ('I am not eloquent'); Stephen may be describing his potential or using a different tradition.
7.23: 'When Moses was forty years old, he decided to visit (epeskepsasthai — to look upon with care, to visit officially) his own people, the Israelites' — episkeptomai is the word used for God's gracious visitation of his people (Luke 1:68, 78; 7:16; Acts 15:14). Moses's official-visitation of his people mirrors the divine-visitation pattern. His 'going down' to see his people echoes God's 'coming down' to see Israel's affliction (Exod 3:7-8). Moses enacts the divine compassion before he has received the divine commission.
7.24: 'He went to his defence (ēmynato — he defended/helped) and avenged him (epoiēsen ekdikēsin — he made/performed justice/vengeance)' — ekdikēsis is the execution of justice; it can be either righteous vindication or personal vengeance depending on context. The MKT 'avenged him by killing the Egyptian' is accurate. Stephen neither condemns nor defends the killing; he presents it as Moses's premature attempt at the deliverer-role (v.25 — he thought they would understand that God was using him).
7.25: 'Moses thought that his own people would realize that God was using him to rescue them' — the MKT 'God was using him to rescue them' renders synienai hoti ho theos dia cheiros autou didōsin sōtērian autois — 'would understand that God was giving salvation through his hand.' Sōtēria (salvation) is the word used for the gospel's salvation throughout Acts. Moses's first attempt at delivering Israel is an image of the rejected deliverer who is eventually vindicated — the same pattern applied to Jesus.
7.26: 'Moses came upon two Israelites who were fighting. He tried to reconcile them' — the verb synēllassen (he was reconciling) is imperfect: he was in the process of reconciling, attempting peace. Moses as peacemaker among his own people is the role he is appointed for; his brothers' rejection of his peacemaking mirrors Israel's rejection of the prophets and Christ. The 'Men, you are brothers; why do you want to hurt each other?' foreshadows Christ's reconciling mission and the apostolic call to peace.
7.27: 'Who made you ruler and judge over us?' — the rejection-question (tis se katestēsen archonta kai dikastēn — who appointed you ruler and judge?) is quoted identically in v.35 as the founding rejection that paradoxically becomes his appointment-text: the one they rejected is the one God sent. The double use of the rejection-question (vv.27 and 35) creates the chiastic structure of the Moses-narrative: rejected by man, appointed by God; rejected by Israel, vindicated by God.
7.28: 'Are you thinking of killing me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' — the irony is that the Israelite who quotes Moses's killing of the Egyptian is himself the one who will 'kill' Moses through rejection — driving him from the community. The MKT 'Are you thinking of' renders mē anelein me thēleis — 'do you not desire to kill me?' The negative particle makes this a rhetorical question expecting a negative answer, but the subtext is accusatory.
7.29: 'When Moses heard this, he fled to Midian, where he settled as a foreigner (paroikos — sojourner) and had two sons' — paroikos (v.29) echoes the paroikoi of v.6: the covenant people's patriarchs were sojourners, and now Moses joins their number. The word of rejection drives Moses into sojourner-status. Logos in this verse (logos de houtos — 'at this word'; disp=3 for logos) is the concrete speech-act that precipitated Moses's flight, not the Johannine Word. MKT 'when Moses heard this' accurately renders the contextual sense.
7.30: 'An angel appeared to Moses in the flames of a burning bush in the desert near Mount Sinai' — the theophany at Sinai (Horeb/Sinai are the same mountain) occurs after Moses's forty-year sojourn in Midian. The angel of the LORD appears in flame (en phlogi pyros — in a flame of fire) — the same pneumatological manifestation form as Pentecost (Acts 2:3). The bush burns without being consumed: an eternal, unconsumable fire that is nonetheless real and present fire.
7.31: 'He was amazed at the sight' (ethaumazen to horama — he was marveling at the vision) — horama is the word used in Acts for visionary experiences (Acts 9:10, 12; 10:3, 17, 19; 11:5; 12:9; 16:9, 10; 18:9). The burning bush is a horama: not an ordinary natural phenomenon but a divinely-staged vision that becomes the occasion for divine address. 'As he went over to get a closer look, he heard the Lord's voice' — the approach that brings divine address is the posture of covenant-inquiry.
7.32: 'I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob' — the triple patriarchal formula (ho theos tōn paterōn sou, ho theos Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb) quotes Exod 3:6 LXX exactly. This is the burning-bush self-identification that Jesus later uses to prove resurrection (Mark 12:26 — 'I am the God of Abraham...'; the present-tense 'I am' implies the living covenant-relationship with those still alive). 'Moses trembled with fear and did not dare to look' — entromos genomenos (having become trembling), the standard posture before the divine presence (Ezek 1:28; Dan 8:17; 10:9).
7.33: 'The place where you are standing is holy ground' (hē gē hēs estēkas hagia gē estin) — the holiness of the ground at the burning bush predates any constructed temple. God declares particular ground holy by his presence; the temple's holiness is derived from the divine presence, not from its construction. Stephen's anti-temple argument gathers force: the burning bush (not the Jerusalem temple) is the paradigmatic holy place — it was in the desert, outside the land.
7.34: 'I have indeed seen the oppression of my people in Egypt. I have heard their groaning and have come down to set them free' — the divine seeing, hearing, and descending (idōn eidon... ēkousa... katebēn — I have seen, I heard, I came down) is the triple divine engagement formula from Exod 3:7-8 LXX. The divine descent to deliver is the pattern for the Incarnation (John 1:14) and for every divine intervention. 'Now come, I will send you back to Egypt' — the sending is the pattern of the mission: God descends and then sends human agents as his representatives.
7.35: 'This is the same Moses they had rejected' — the threefold repetition of 'this is the same Moses' (touton ton Mōusēn — this Moses, twice in v.35, once in v.36) creates the rhetorical drumbeat of rejected-then-vindicated. 'Ruler and deliverer' (archonta kai lytrōtēn) — lytrōtēs appears only here in the NT; it is the agent noun of lytroō (to redeem/ransom), connecting to the OT goel (kinsman-redeemer). The MKT 'deliverer' captures the ransom-redemption sense. God sent him through the angel in the burning bush — the same rejected man becomes the divinely-appointed redeemer.
7.36: 'He led them out of Egypt and performed wonders and signs (terata kai sēmeia) in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and for forty years in the wilderness' — the wonders-and-signs formula (used in the reverse order 'signs and wonders' more commonly) anchors the Mosaic ministry. Stephen's Moses-summary (vv.35-37) is the template for the apostolic ministry: sent by God, performing signs and wonders, leading a people through a wilderness, pointing to a coming prophet. The audience can hear the parallel with Jesus.
7.37: 'This is the Moses who told the Israelites, God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your own people' — the quotation of Deut 18:15 ('a prophet like me') is Stephen's Christological key. Moses himself prophesied the coming of a greater prophet. Stephen's accusers who honor Moses should hear Moses's own prophecy pointing to Jesus. The rejected deliverer prophesied the coming of another rejected deliverer. The speech's implicit argument: if you rejected Jesus, you rejected Moses's own prophecy.
7.38: 'He was in the assembly (ekklēsia — church/assembly) in the wilderness' — the wilderness gathering is called ekklēsia, the same word used for the Christian community throughout Acts. The church in the wilderness is the OT antecedent of the NT church; the continuity is lexical and theological. 'He received living words (logia zōnta) to pass on to us' — logia is the diminutive or specialized form of logos, specifically for divine oracles (Rom 3:2; 1 Pet 4:11). The MKT 'living words' correctly renders the divine-oracle register rather than 'words' generally.
7.39: 'Our ancestors refused to obey him. Instead, they rejected him and in their hearts turned back to Egypt' — the heart-return to Egypt (estrapsēsan tais kardiais autōn eis Aigypton) is the inner rejection: they still thought like Egypt-slaves even after the Exodus. The pattern is applied to Stephen's audience: they have the form of covenant-faithfulness (temple, law, circumcision) but their hearts are oriented toward the security-structures of the old order. The MKT 'in their hearts turned back to Egypt' correctly captures the internal apostasy.
7.40: 'Make us gods who will go before us. As for this fellow Moses' — the quotation of Exod 32:1 LXX ('make us gods, elohim, who will go before us') uses plural 'gods' — the golden calf is not merely one idol but the desire for a plurality of tangible mediators. 'This fellow Moses' (ho Mōusēs houtos) uses the contemptuous demonstrative: the same distancing 'this one' used by those who reject divinely-appointed figures (John 9:29 — 'this man, we do not know where he comes from'). The MKT 'this fellow Moses who led us out of Egypt' captures the contemptuous register.
7.41: 'They made an idol in the form of a calf' (emoschopoisēsan — they made a calf-idol; a hapax in the NT) — the compound verb (moschon + poieō) appears to be coined for this specific act of manufacturing the golden calf. 'They revelled (euphrainthēsan) in what their own hands had made' — the joy in self-made religion is the anti-type of joy in the God-given salvation. 'The works of their hands' (ta erga tōn cheirōn autōn) is the standard OT idolatry-formula (Deut 4:28; Ps 115:4; Isa 2:8).
7.42: 'God turned away (estrepsen) and gave them over (paredōken) to the worship of the sun, moon and stars' — the divine abandonment formula (paredōken — he gave them over) is the same word used in Rom 1:24-28 for God's judgment on Gentile idolatry. Israel in the wilderness is subjected to the same judgment as the Gentiles: God gives them over to their chosen idols. The Amos quotation that follows ('the book of the prophets') is from Amos 5:25-27 LXX, which Stephen quotes in the next verse.
7.43: 'You have taken up the tabernacle of Molek and the star of your god Rephan, the idols you made to worship. Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Babylon' — Stephen's quotation of Amos 5:26-27 LXX modifies the MT's 'beyond Damascus' to 'beyond Babylon,' extending the exile's scope. Rephan is the LXX's rendering of the MT's Kiyyun (Saturn deity). The point is the continuity of idolatry: the wilderness idolatry of the golden calf eventually leads to the Assyrian-Babylonian exile. Idolatry has consequences that accumulate across generations.
7.44: 'Our ancestors had the tabernacle (hē skēnē — the tent) of the covenant law with them in the wilderness. It had been made as God directed Moses, according to the pattern he had seen' — the tabernacle (skēnē = tent) is God's appointed meeting place — and it is a tent, mobile, non-permanent. 'According to the pattern (typos) he had seen' quotes Exod 25:40; the heavenly pattern (typos) is the reality; the earthly tabernacle is the earthly copy. Stephen's tabernacle-theology: the earthly structure is inherently derivative and temporary.
7.45: 'After receiving the tabernacle, our ancestors under Joshua brought it with them when they took the land from the nations God drove out before them. It remained in the land until the time of David' — Joshua (Iēsous — the same Greek name as Jesus) brings the tabernacle (the mobile tent-presence of God) into the land. The presence of God entered the land through Joshua before any temple was built; the tent-presence preceded and survived without the stone structure for centuries.
7.46: 'Who enjoyed God's favour (charin para theō — grace/favor before God) and asked that he might provide a dwelling place for the God of Jacob' — David's request to build a 'dwelling' (skēnōma — a tent-dwelling) for the God of Jacob is a tent-word, not a temple-word: David's original desire was for a better tent, which God redirected. Charin para theō uses charis (disp=2) in the favor-before-God sense — David's covenantal standing before God. The MKT 'enjoyed God's favour' accurately renders the social-favor dimension.
7.47: 'But it was Solomon who built a house (oikon — a house) for him' — oikos (house) is the ordinary word for dwelling, here used for the temple. Stephen's formulation is pointed: the temple was Solomon's project, not David's desire, and not God's demand. The shift from skēnē (tent, God's appointed form) to oikos (permanent stone house) is a step in the wrong direction for Stephen's argument — from mobile divine presence to fixed human architecture.
7.48: 'However, the Most High (ho hypsistos — the exalted one) does not live in houses made by human hands (cheiropoiētois — hand-made structures)' — cheiropoiētos (made by hand) is used in the NT specifically for the contrast between human-made religion and divine reality (Mark 14:58; Eph 2:11; Heb 9:11, 24). The word carries negative connotations: idols are cheiropoiēta (Lev 26:1 LXX). Stephen's implicit argument: the Jerusalem temple, as a cheiropoiēton, belongs to the same category as the wilderness idols that Israel made with its hands.
7.49: 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool. What kind of house will you build for me? says the Lord' — the Isaiah 66:1-2 quotation is the Scriptural climax of Stephen's anti-temple argument. The God who inhabits heaven and uses the entire earth as a footstool cannot be contained in a human structure. 'What kind of house will you build for me?' (poion oikon oikodométhē moi) is a rhetorical question expecting the answer: none adequate. The universe is smaller than God; no house made by human hands can contain him.
7.50: 'Has not my hand (hē cheir mou — my hand) made all these things?' — the divine hand that made the cosmos is contrasted with the human hands that made the temple (cheiropoiētois, v.48). God's hand made everything; human hands made a structure inside everything and claimed it could contain the Everything-maker. The Isaiah quotation ends Stephen's positive argument; what follows (v.51) is the direct accusation that functions as the speech's peroration.
7.51: 'You stiff-necked people! Your hearts and ears are still uncircumcised' — sklērotrachēloi (stiff-necked) is the exact LXX calque of the Hebrew qesheh-oref (Exod 32:9; 33:3, 5; 34:9; Deut 9:6, 13): the wilderness-generation's characteristic rebellion. 'Uncircumcised in hearts and ears' (apericktmētoi kardiais kai tois ōsin) quotes Lev 26:41; Deut 10:16; Jer 4:4; 6:10 — the prophetic accusation that Israel's ethnic circumcision has not produced the heart-obedience the sign points to. 'Holy Spirit' here (tō pneumati tō hagiō — the Holy Spirit; disp=3) is capitalized in the MKT correctly: the Spirit being resisted is the divine third person, not merely spiritual impetus.
7.52: 'Was there ever a prophet your ancestors did not persecute?' — the rhetorical question expects 'No' — the persecution of every prophet is the record. 'The Righteous One' (ton Dikaion — disp=4: lit=righteous, tho=just) is a messianic title in Acts (3:14; 22:14; cf. Isa 53:11 — 'my righteous servant'). The MKT 'Righteous One' captures the titular force: Stephen is not calling Jesus merely 'a righteous person' but using a recognized messianic designation. 'You have betrayed and murdered him' — the you-have-murdered formula applied directly to the Sanhedrin, the specific audience.
7.53: 'You who have received the law that was given through angels (eis diatagās angelōn — into ordinances of angels) but have not obeyed it' — the angelic mediation of the law (diatagas angelōn) is a Second Temple tradition (Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2; Deut 33:2 LXX) that elevated the law's authority by emphasizing its heavenly transmission. Stephen uses the tradition against his accusers: the very law they claim to honor came through angels; their persecution of the prophets and murder of Jesus violates the law they received through such august mediation. The law (nomos — disp=3) is 'the Law' in the MKT: Stephen treats it as a proper noun throughout.
7.54: 'They were furious (dieprionto tais kardiais autōn — they were sawn through in their hearts) and gnashed their teeth (ebrukon tous odontas — they gnashed/ground their teeth at him)' — dieprionto is the same verb used in Acts 5:33 for the Sanhedrin's reaction to Peter's testimony. The 'sawn through' metaphor captures inward fury that the controlled exterior barely contains. 'Gnashed their teeth' echoes Ps 35:16 and 37:12 (the wicked gnashing their teeth at the righteous); the Sanhedrin fulfills the Psalter's portrait of the wicked in their response to Stephen.
7.56: 'I see heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God' — the Son of Man 'standing' (hestōta) rather than sitting is distinctive: in Dan 7:13 and Psalm 110:1 he sits at God's right hand. The standing posture suggests either active witness (standing to testify on Stephen's behalf), welcome (rising to receive the martyr), or eschatological alertness. The MKT 'Son of Man standing at the right hand of God' preserves the unusual standing-posture which the text emphasizes. Stephen's vision is the mirror of Jesus's own claim at his trial (Luke 22:69).
7.57: 'They covered their ears and, yelling at the top of their voices, they all rushed at him' — the ear-covering gesture (sunéschan ta ōta autōn — they held/closed their ears) is the enacted form of 'uncircumcised ears' (v.51): they literally close their ears to the testimony they do not want to hear. The 'yelling at the top of their voices' (kraxantes phōnē megalē) overwhelms Stephen's testimony with noise — the mob's sonic suppression of prophetic witness.
7.58: 'Dragged him out of the city and began to stone him. Meanwhile, the witnesses laid their coats at the feet of a young man named Saul' — stoning required the witnesses to cast the first stones (Deut 17:7); the garment-laying at Saul's feet implies his approving supervision (8:1 — 'Saul was consenting to his death'). Luke introduces Saul/Paul here to establish the continuity between the persecution of Stephen and the persecutor who becomes the primary witness of Acts. The witness-coats at Saul's feet are a literary promise that Saul's story is not over.
7.59: 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit' (dexai to pneuma mou — receive my spirit) — Stephen's dying prayer mirrors Jesus's last words from Ps 31:5 ('Into your hand I commit my spirit,' Luke 23:46), redirected: where Jesus prays to 'Father,' Stephen prays to 'Lord Jesus.' The pneuma here (disp=3) is the human spirit — Stephen's own spirit — not the Holy Spirit. The MKT lowercase 'my spirit' correctly distinguishes this from the Holy Spirit of vv.51 and 55. Stephen dies in the posture of Jesus: committing his spirit, praying for his killers (v.60).
7.60: 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them' (mē stēsēs autois tautēn tēn hamartian — do not set against them this sin) — the intercessory prayer for the killers (Luke 23:34 — 'Father, forgive them') is made now to 'Lord' (Jesus). 'He fell asleep' (ekoimēthē — he fell asleep; the standard early Christian euphemism for death, using the Koine word for sleep that suggests resurrection-waking). The MKT 'fell asleep' preserves the sleep-death metaphor that the NT uses for Christian death (John 11:11-14; 1 Cor 15:18, 20, 51; 1 Thess 4:13-16). Luke's narrative of the first Christian martyr ends with the same double-forgiveness and rest that Jesus's death exhibited.

8.35: arxamenos apo tes graphes tautes euengelisato auto ton Iesoun (arxamenos apo tēs graphēs tautēs euēngelisato autō ton Iēsoun): 'beginning from this Scripture he proclaimed good news about Jesus'. Philip's method is the apostolic hermeneutic: Isa 53 → Jesus. euangelizomai here has its full weight as announcement of a decisive new development — the Servant's identity is now disclosed. The Ethiopian eunuch's question ('of himself or another?') echoes the pesher method of Qumran: Scripture requires an identified referent to unlock its meaning.
8.1: syneudokōn (syneudokōn, 'consenting/approving'): Paul will later describe his own role at Stephen's death with the same verb (22:20) — he was not a passive bystander but an active approver. diōgmos megas (diōgmos megas, 'great persecution'): the first use of diōgmos in Acts; the persecution Paul later says he suffered becomes the persecution he first caused (Gal 1:13; 1 Cor 15:9).
8.2: andres eulabeis (andres eulabeis, 'devout men'): the same adjective used for Simeon (Luke 2:25) and Cornelius (Acts 10:2). These are Torah-observant Jews honoring a fellow-Jew whom they recognized as righteous; their mourning (penthountes) was public and costly — lamentation for one executed as a criminal was itself a social risk.
8.3: elymaineto (elymaineto, 'was ravaging/destroying'): a verb used in LXX for wild animals tearing flesh (Ps 80:13); its application to Saul's house-to-house arrests is deliberately brutal. syron (syrōn, 'dragging'): the violent participle conveys forced arrest — men and women dragged from their homes and thrown into prison. This is Paul's own retrospective self-condemnation (1 Tim 1:13: 'the chief of sinners').
8.4: hoi men oun diasparentes (hoi men oun diasparentes, 'those who were scattered'): diaspeirō (scatter, disperse) is the diaspora verb; the persecution that intended to suppress the gospel instead geographically expands it. Luke's narrative irony is deliberate: the enemy's strategy becomes the mission's vehicle. euangelizomenoi (proclaiming good news): the scattered are not refugees in silence but active evangelists.
8.5: Philippos ... ekērysso autois ton Christon (Philippos ... ekērysso autois ton Christon, 'Philip proclaimed the Messiah to them'): Philip is the deacon (6:5) whose mission reaches Samaria — the next step in Acts 1:8's geographic outline. Kēryssō (herald-proclamation) is the strong word for authoritative announcement; its object is the Messiah-title, the full Jewish category that requires hermeneutical explanation.
8.6: homothymadon (with one accord): the same word as Pentecost (2:1, 46), now describing the Samaritan crowd's unified attention. sēmeia ha epoiei (signs he was doing): the signs are the confirmation that the proclamation is authentic — same pattern as Peter and Paul. The Samaritans, traditionally estranged from Jerusalem, respond with the same unity as the Jerusalem crowd.
8.7: pneumata akatharta ... exērchetho (impure spirits were coming out): akatharta (unclean) is the ritual-purity vocabulary applied to the demonic realm — the mission in Samaria addresses both spiritual and physical disorder. parelymenoi kai chōloi etherapeuonto (paralyzed and lame were healed): the two categories appear in the Isa 35:6 messianic-sign list, echoing Peter's temple healing.
8.8: egeneto chara megalē (egeneto chara megalē, 'there was great joy'): joy is Luke's theological signal for eschatological arrival (Luke 2:10; 24:52; Acts 2:46; 13:52; 15:3; 16:34). The Samaritan city's joy marks the mission's reach into the traditionally excluded Samaritans; the kingdom's arrival is experienced as joy wherever it goes.
8.9: mageuōn (mageuōn, 'practicing sorcery/magic'): the root gives us 'magic'; Simon is not merely a conjurer but someone operating within the framework of Hellenistic magical practice — invoking divine names and powers for results. existanōn (amazing): the same verb Philip's signs produce (v.6); Simon's amazement-capacity is about to be displaced by a greater power.
8.10: hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē (hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē, 'the power of God called Great'): Simon's title is a Samaritan divine-name, likely connected to Simonian Gnosticism's later 'Great Power' theology. Luke preserves the exact wording — a measure of accuracy about a religious milieu his audience would recognize. Philip's proclamation of Jesus as Lord displaces Simon's self-claimed divine-power identity.
8.11: dia to hikanō chronō tais mageiais existakenai autous ('because for a long time he had amazed them with his sorceries'): hikanos chronos (a long/considerable time) measures the depth of Simon's hold on the community; the Samaritans are not casually credulous but have been formed by years of his practice. Philip's sudden displacement of Simon signals the superior power of the gospel's witness.
8.12: euangelizomenō peri tēs basileias tou theou kai tou onomatos Iēsou Christou ('proclaiming the good news about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ'): the two objects — kingdom and name — are the content of the Samaritan proclamation. Both men and women (andres te kai gynaikes) are baptized; the mission to Samaria includes women from the beginning, consistent with Acts' consistent inclusion of women in the covenant community.
8.13: ho de Simōn kai autos episteusen ('Simon himself also believed'): Luke records Simon's belief and baptism factually; the subsequent exchange (vv.18-24) does not retract the belief but reveals its inadequate root. theōrōn ... dynameis kai sēmeia ginomena ('seeing the signs and great miracles happening'): Simon's continued astonishment is not penitential but acquisitive — he watches as a professional assessor of power.
8.14: aposteilan pros autous Petron kai Iōannēn (sent Peter and John): Jerusalem's apostolic response to Samaritan conversion establishes continuity with the mother-community; the Samaritans are not a breakaway sect but an extension of the one church. The Jerusalem apostles' visit is the structural incorporation of Samaria into the body that began at Pentecost.
8.15: hopōs labōsin pneuma hagion (so that they might receive the Holy Spirit): the separation between belief/baptism and Spirit-reception here is the strongest in Acts; it has generated debate about whether this was a temporary transitional phenomenon (incorporating Samaria required apostolic laying on of hands) or a normative two-stage pattern. The delay is not explained; Luke presents the apostolic prayer and hands as the channel for the Spirit's arrival.
8.16: oupō gar ēn ep' oudeni autōn epipeptōkos ('for he had not yet fallen on any of them'): epipiptō (fall upon) is the Pentecost-verb (10:44; 11:15); its conspicuous absence here makes the contrast pointed. The Spirit who fell on 120 in Jerusalem without laying on of hands had not fallen on the Samaritan believers who had received baptism — the anomaly drives the narrative forward.
8.17: epetithoun tas cheiras (they were laying their hands on them): the apostolic laying on of hands as the instrument for Spirit-reception appears here for the first time in Acts. The gesture has OT precedent (Num 8:10; 27:18-23) for commissioning and authorization; its use here extends that tradition to Spirit-reception, making the apostles' bodily action the channel of the divine gift.
8.18: theōn hoti dia tēs epithesesōs tōn cheirōn tōn apostolōn didotai to Pneuma ('seeing that through the laying on of the apostles' hands the Spirit was given'): Simon sees something visible — the Spirit's reception was evidentially manifest, likely through speaking in tongues. His response reveals that he has processed Philip's entire ministry as a power-system with a controlling mechanism he could learn to operate.
8.19: dos kai emoi tēn exousian tautēn ('give me also this authority'): exousia (authority, right) is precisely what Simon thinks he wants — the ability to transmit the Spirit by personal action. He misidentifies the Spirit's gift as a power that belongs to the apostles and can be purchased from them. The sin of simony names a category of ecclesiastical corruption after this episode.
8.20: to argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian ('may your money go to destruction with you'): Peter's response is the sharpest rebuke in Acts — not a question but a wish-formula invoking divine judgment. hoti tēn dōrean tou theou enomisas dia chrēmatōn ktasthai ('because you thought you could acquire the gift of God with money'): dōrea (gift, v.20) echoes 2:38; the Spirit is given, never purchased.
8.21: ouk estin soi meris oud' klēros en tō logō toutō ('you have no part or share in this word'): meris and klēros are the inheritance-vocabulary of covenant participation — the same terms used for Judas's forfeited portion (1:17, 25). Simon's heart (hē kardia sou ouk estin eutheia enanti tou theou) is the diagnostic: not the money but the disposition of heart is what disqualifies.
8.22: metanōeson apo tēs kakias tautes ('repent of this wickedness'): Peter's imperative is conditional but genuine — repentance is possible and offered. ei ara aphethēsetai soi hē epinoia tēs kardias sou ('if perhaps the thought of your heart may be forgiven'): the ei ara (if perhaps) is an unusual conditional that indicates uncertainty about whether the intent is forgivable — not denying God's forgiveness but questioning Simon's sincerity.
8.23: cholēn pikrias kai syndesmō adikias ('gall of bitterness and bond of wickedness'): cholē pikria (gall of bitterness) echoes Deut 29:18 LXX for the root of apostasy — a root of poison-plant. Syndesmos adikias (bond of unrighteousness) describes being bound up in sin's system. Peter's diagnosis reaches back to OT prophetic categories for describing the state of someone whose orientation is fundamentally against the covenant.
8.24: deēthēte hymeis hyper emou pros ton kyrion ('pray to the Lord for me'): Simon's request — asking others to pray for him rather than praying himself — may indicate genuine fear without genuine repentance. The ambiguity is deliberate; Luke does not close the question of Simon's ultimate state. The narrative serves as a warning rather than a resolution.
8.25: hypestraphon eis Ierousalēm ... euēngelizonto pollas kōmas tōn Samaritōn ('they returned to Jerusalem, proclaiming good news to many Samaritan villages'): the apostles' return journey through Samaritan villages is a quiet expansion of the mission. The same apostles sent from Jerusalem to evaluate the Samaritan mission become missionaries in Samaritan villages on their way home — the mission spreads through every movement.
8.26: angelos de kyriou elalēsen pros Philippon (an angel of the Lord spoke to Philip): divine direction by angel (also 5:19; 10:3; 12:7-11; 27:23) is Luke's way of marking pivotal mission-transitions. The road to Gaza is described as autē estin erēmos ('this is a desert [road]') — either the road is remote or Gaza itself was ruined after its destruction in 96 BC. Philip is redirected from successful urban mission to an isolated road encounter.
8.27: dynastēs Kandakēs basylissēs Aithiopōn ('an official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians'): dynastēs (high official, powerful person) is a court title; Candace was the title of the queen mother in Meroitic Nubia, who effectively ruled. Ethiopia (Aithiopia) represented the southern end of the known world; this official is both the 'ends of the earth' (Acts 1:8) and a diaspora worshipper connecting the mission to its furthest geographic range.
8.28: anaginōskōn ton prophētēn Esaian (reading the prophet Isaiah): ancient reading was typically oral — anaginōskōn implies reading aloud, which is why Philip could hear what the eunuch was reading from a distance. The Isaiah scroll was among the most expensive texts in the first century; this official was both literate and wealthy enough to own the text. Luke's detail explains the mechanics of the encounter.
8.29: eipen de to Pneuma tō Philippō (the Spirit said to Philip): the Spirit who directed Philip to leave Samaria now specifically directs him to approach the chariot. The mission is driven entirely by divine initiative — Philip did not select this target, assess the eunuch's readiness, or choose the moment. Luke uses 'Spirit said' (as opposed to 'angel said' in v.26) to mark the direct Spirit-prompting of the apostolic word.
8.30: ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis (ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis, 'do you understand what you are reading?'): the ara ge formula is an inferential particle combination suggesting 'I take it then' — Philip's question is not impolite but engaged. The paronomasia (the same root ginōskō/anaginōskō) puns on knowing and reading: understanding requires more than literacy.
8.31: pōs gar an dynaimen ean mē tis hodēgēsei me ('how can I, unless someone guides me?'): hodēgēsē (guide, lead the way) is the road-guide verb; the metaphor fits the chariot journey. The eunuch's honest acknowledgment of interpretive need is exactly the posture that opens him to Philip's instruction. He invites Philip up — the physical proximity enables the hermeneutical conversation.
8.32: periochē tēs graphēs hēn anaginōsken ('the passage of Scripture he was reading'): periochē (section, passage) is a technical term for a scriptural unit. Isa 53:7-8 LXX is quoted verbatim; the LXX's tēn genean autou tis diēgēsetai diverges from the MT but makes the same point about the Servant's obscure destiny. The sheep-to-slaughter image and the silence of the shearee make Isa 53 the perfect locus for the eunuch's question.
8.33: en tē tapeinōsei autou hē krisis autou ērthē ('in his humiliation his judgment was taken away'): the LXX reads differently from MT at this point; the Greek version the eunuch was reading has 'in his humiliation his judgment was removed' — his justice was denied, not that he was condemned. The verse supports the proclamation of the innocent sufferer. tis diēgēsetai tēn genean autou: 'who can tell of his descendants' — his life-line cut off, yet the community of believers is precisely that continuation.
8.34: peri tinos ho prophētēs legei touto; peri heautou ē peri heterou tinos ('about whom does the prophet say this, about himself or about someone else?'): the eunuch's question is the decisive hermeneutical query — the same question the Qumran community asked of Isaiah. Philip's answer begins 'from this Scripture' (apo tēs graphēs tautes, v.35) and proceeds to Jesus: the prophet spoke of another, and that other is Jesus.
8.36: idou hydōr; ti kōlyei me baptisthēnai ('look, water! what prevents me from being baptized?'): kōlyō (prevent, hinder) is Acts' baptism-readiness question; it reappears in 10:47 (Peter: 'can anyone withhold water?') and 11:17. The eunuch's spontaneous recognition that Philip's proclamation leads to baptism shows genuine understanding — he has connected the Isaiah Servant with Jesus with himself and with the covenant rite.
8.37: Verse 37 (Philip's 'if you believe with all your heart' exchange) is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts (P45, P74, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is likely a scribal addition providing a baptismal confession formula for liturgical use. Its inclusion in later manuscripts reflects the growing importance of explicit pre-baptismal confession in church practice. The MKT notes its presence because some traditions rely on it.
8.38: katebēsan amphoteroi eis to hydōr ('both went down into the water'): the immersion implied by eis to hydōr (into the water) and the parallel rising (anebēsan ek tou hydatos, v.39) supports baptism by immersion as the early form. The Sahidic Coptic and Ethiopian liturgical traditions made this the definitive text for immersion-practice; Philip's gesture of accompanying the eunuch into the water marks pastoral solidarity.
8.39: pneuma kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon ('the Spirit of the Lord suddenly seized/snatched Philip'): harpazō (snatch, catch away) is the violent displacement verb — the same used for Paul's third heaven vision (2 Cor 12:2-4) and the Elijah/Elisha translation tradition (2 Kgs 2:16). Philip is providentially removed; the eunuch 'went on his way rejoicing' (charōn) — the joy-marker of arrival at salvation again.
8.40: Azōton (Azotus/Ashdod): the Philistine city on the coastal plain; Philip's post-harpazō ministry moves northward through all the coastal towns until Caesarea, where he will appear again as the host of Paul (21:8). The coastal plain mission foreshadows the Gentile mission to come; Philip's itinerary traces a line that will become the spine of the Western Gentile expansion.

10.34: prosopolemptes (prosōpolēmptēs, 'one who shows partiality/face-receiver'): a NT coinage from the LXX idiom lambanein prosopon (lift the face = receive favorably), itself a calque of Hebrew nasa' panim. The word appears in Paul (Rom 2:11; Eph 6:9; Col 3:25) and Jas 2:1. Peter's declaration that God is not a prosopolēmptēs marks the theological breakthrough of the Cornelius episode: the ethnic boundary between Jew and Gentile is not the boundary of divine acceptance.
10.44: epepesen to Pneuma to Hagion (epepesen to Pneuma to Hagion, 'the Holy Spirit fell upon'): the same verb as Acts 2 (2:3, epesen); Luke deliberately signals that Cornelius's household receives the identical Pentecost as the Jerusalem community. epipiptō (fall upon) is striking in its physicality — the Spirit is not merely invited or requested but descends sovereignly, surprising Peter mid-sermon. The circumcised believers are exestēsan (astonished) because the gift precedes any formal completion of conversion rites, vindicating God's impartial acceptance.
10.1: 'At Caesarea there was a man named Cornelius' — the introduction follows the standard Hellenistic 'person introduction' formula (aner tis... onomati Kornēlios — a certain man named Cornelius). Caesarea Maritima was the Roman administrative capital of Judaea, built by Herod the Great; a centurion stationed there commands approximately eighty men. The 'Italian Regiment' (speira Italikē) is a cohort designation suggesting Roman citizenship and elite status. Luke introduces Cornelius with as much social detail as he gave Lydia (16:14) or Zacchaeus (19:2) — the social register of the first Gentile convert is deliberate.
10.2: 'Devout and God-fearing' (eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon — pious and fearing God) — eusebēs appears only here in Acts; phoboumenos ton theon ('fearing God') is the technical designation for Gentile synagogue-adherents who accepted monotheism and Jewish ethics but had not become full proselytes. 'Gave generously to those in need' (poiōn eleēmosynas — doing acts of mercy/alms) — eleēmosyne is the standard NT word for almsgiving, connected to the Hebrew tsedaqah (righteous/justice-based generosity). 'Prayed to God regularly' (deomenos tou theou dia pantos — entreating God continually) — the combination of alms and prayer is the classic Jewish piety formula.
10.3: 'He had a vision. He distinctly saw (eiden en horamati phanerōs — he saw in a vision clearly) an angel of God' — the horama (vision) uses the technical term for prophetic/divine visionary experience (Acts 7:31; 9:10, 12; 16:9-10; 18:9). Phanerōs (clearly, openly, distinctly) emphasizes the vision's unambiguous quality — no dreamy vagueness. 'At about three in the afternoon' — the ninth hour (hōran tēs hōras tēs ennatas) is the time of the afternoon sacrifice in the temple — and presumably the time Cornelius was praying (v.30 confirms: 'at three in the afternoon'). The divine vision comes at the prayer-hour.
10.4: 'Your prayers and gifts to the poor have come up as a memorial offering (anabēsan eis mnēmosunon — have gone up as a reminder) before God' — anabainō (go up) is the standard verb for sacrificial smoke ascending; Cornelius's non-sacrificial offerings are treated as ascending sacrifices. Mnēmosunon (memorial offering, reminder) is the LXX technical term for the 'memorial portion' of grain offerings presented before YHWH (Lev 2:2; 5:12; 6:15; Num 5:26). Luke's language frames Cornelius's Gentile piety in temple-offering categories, legitimizing it before a Jewish audience.
10.5: 'Simon who is called Peter' — the double name (Simōna ton epikaloumenon Petron) reflects the Aramaic/Greek bilingualism of the early church: Simon (Hebrew/Aramaic) called Peter (Greek translation of the Aramaic Kepha = rock). Luke consistently uses both names, reminding readers that the Greek-speaking church's leadership was rooted in an Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish community. The naming of the town (Joppa) and the host (Simon the tanner, v.6) provides the verifiable detail typical of Luke's historical narration.
10.6: 'He is staying with Simon the tanner, whose house is by the sea' — the tanner (burseus) was ritually marginal in Jewish society: constant contact with animal hides made Levitical purity-observance difficult; tanners were required to live at the edge of settlements because of the smell. Peter's lodging with a socially marginal artisan already positions him at the boundary of conventional purity-consciousness — a preparation for the rooftop vision that will push him further beyond those boundaries.
10.7: 'When the angel who spoke to him had gone, Cornelius called two of his servants and a devout soldier (stratiōtēn eusebē — a pious soldier) who was one of his attendants' — the soldier is described with eusebēs (devout/pious), the same word applied to Cornelius in v.2. Cornelius's entire household participates in his God-fearing orientation. The sending of three men (v.8 — three: two servants + one soldier) corresponds to the Spirit's instruction to Peter in v.19 ('three men are looking for you').
10.8: 'He told them everything and sent them to Joppa' — the panta (everything) that Cornelius tells his servants — the vision, the angel, the instructions — is passed on in full to the sent party. The full communication is significant: these men are not mere messengers carrying a note; they know the divine context of their errand. When they arrive at Peter's house (v.22), their testimony will confirm the divine initiation of the Cornelius-encounter.
10.9: 'About noon the following day... Peter went up on the roof to pray' — the sixth hour (hōran hektēn) is midday. The rooftop (dōma) was the standard domestic prayer and rest space in Mediterranean houses; it provided elevation, privacy, and air circulation. Peter's midday prayer positions him in a state of regular devotion — the divine vision finds him already in prayer. The Spirit consistently appears to those who are praying in Acts: Cornelius at 3pm prayer (v.3), Peter at noon prayer, the disciples at Pentecost in prayer (1:14).
10.10: 'He fell into a trance' (epepesen ep' auton ekstasis — an ecstasy fell upon him) — ekstasis is the 'standing-outside-oneself' state; the verb epipiptō (to fall upon) is the same verb used for the Spirit falling upon the disciples (11:15 — 'the Holy Spirit fell on them'). The MKT 'trance' (conventional rendering) captures the dissociative quality but loses the spatial displacement sense of ek-stasis (standing-outside/beside oneself). The 'trance' is not self-induced but falls upon Peter as the Spirit falls upon the church.
10.11: 'He saw heaven opened and something like a large sheet being let down to earth by its four corners' — the ouranon aneōgmenon (opened heaven) is the standard prophetic-vision frame (Ezek 1:1; John 1:51; Rev 4:1; 19:11). The object (skeuos ti — a certain vessel/object) is described as 'like a large sheet' (othonē — a linen cloth, sail-like material) being lowered by its four corners (tesarsin archais — four beginnings/corners). The fourfold lowering from the four corners of heaven evokes the universalist imagery of gathering from the four winds (Isa 11:12; Ezek 37:9).
10.12: 'It contained all kinds of four-footed animals, as well as reptiles and birds' (panta ta tetrapoda kai ta herpeta tēs gēs kai ta peteina tou ouranou) — the three-category list (four-footed, reptiles, birds) recapitulates the creation-taxonomy of Gen 1:24-25 but deliberately scrambles the clean/unclean distinctions of Lev 11. The sheet contains 'all kinds' (panta — all) indiscriminately: both clean and unclean together. The vision's content is as significant as its form.
10.13: 'Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' (Anastas, Petre, thuson kai phage) — the command-form is terse and urgent. Anasta (rise!) is the same verb used in healings and resurrection-announcements (9:34; 9:40). Thuson (kill, slaughter) is the standard sacrificial-slaughter verb, here used for ordinary food preparation. The command to kill-and-eat reverses the Levitical clean-food legislation from within the framework of Jewish vocabulary — not by abolishing the law but by God's direct command overriding specific dietary prohibitions.
10.14: 'Surely not, Lord!' (Mēdamōs, Kyrie — by no means, Lord!) — mēdamōs is an emphatic negative of refusal; the exclamation appears in LXX in contexts of strong denial (Gen 44:7, 17; Josh 22:29). Peter's 'Lord' (Kyrie) combined with his refusal creates the paradox of disobedient address: he calls Jesus Lord while refusing the Lord's command. 'I have never eaten anything impure or unclean' (koinon ē akatharton — common/defiled or unclean) — koinos (common) has acquired the specialized meaning 'ritually defiled' in Jewish usage; it is the word used for 'common/profane' as opposed to 'set apart/holy.'
10.15: 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' (ha ho theos ekatharisern, su mē koinou — what God cleansed, you do not make common) — the divine passive ekatharisen (has made clean) is decisive: God has performed the cleansing action, not Peter. The counter-command reverses Peter's vocabulary: you used 'common' as a negative; God has used 'clean' as a positive over the same objects. The present-tense command (mē koinou) is continuous: stop making-common what God has cleansed.
10.16: 'This happened three times, and immediately the sheet was taken back to heaven' — the threefold repetition of the vision (tris — three times) is the standard biblical confirmation number (Gen 40:20; Dan 6:10, 13; Jonah 1:17; Matt 12:40). The triple command and triple refusal mirrors Peter's triple denial (John 18:17, 25, 27) and the triple commissioning (John 21:15-17); three is both the number of divine confirmation and the number of Peter's significant moments. The immediate withdrawal (anelēmphthē — was taken up) ends the vision before Peter has resolved its meaning.
10.17: 'While Peter was wondering about the meaning of the vision (ti an eiē to horama ho eiden — what the vision he saw might mean)' — the imperfect diēporei (he was greatly perplexed/at a loss) indicates ongoing, unresolved puzzlement. The vision's meaning is not immediately transparent even to the seer. Peter must interpret a symbolic vision while simultaneously acting on its practical direction (the three men are already at the gate). The combination of unresolved theological interpretation and immediate practical obedience is the apostolic pattern.
10.18: 'They called out, asking if Simon who was known as Peter was staying there' — the arriving messengers' inquiry (epunthanouto — were inquiring, imperfect = continued asking) at the gate coincides precisely with Peter's perplexity. The providential timing (Peter confused about the vision; three men arriving as the vision predicted) is Luke's characteristic demonstration that divine ordering underlies the surface of coincidence. The 'Simon called Peter' double name (v.5, 18) maintains the Aramaic-Greek bilingualism flag.
10.19: 'While Peter was still thinking about the vision, the Spirit said to him' — the Spirit's direct address (eipen autō to pneuma — the Spirit said to him) identifies the communicating agent as the same Spirit who orchestrated the vision. Pneuma here (disp=3) is capitalized in the MKT as 'Spirit' — the third person of the Trinity communicating directly. 'Three men are looking for you' — the precision confirms that the Spirit knows exactly what is happening at the gate while Peter contemplates the rooftop vision.
10.20: 'So get up and go downstairs. Do not hesitate to go with them, for I have sent them' (mē diakrinomenos — not discriminating/doubting) — diakrinō carries both the sense of 'doubt' and 'discriminate/make distinctions.' The MKT 'Do not hesitate' opts for the doubt sense. But the discriminating sense is equally present: do not make ethnic/purity distinctions in going with them. The ambiguity is productive: Peter must both overcome his doubt and overcome his ethnic-discrimination reflex. 'I have sent them' — the Spirit claims Cornelius's messengers as his own ambassadors.
10.21: 'Peter went down and said to the men, I'm the one you're looking for. Why have you come?' — the directness of Peter's question (tis hē aitia di' hēn pārestethe — what is the reason for which you have come?) uses legal/formal vocabulary (aitia = cause, reason; paristēmi = to present oneself officially). Peter is processing the vision while meeting visitors; his directness may reflect his attempt to understand the connection between the vision and these Gentile visitors.
10.22: 'A righteous and God-fearing man' (dikaios... kai phoboumenos ton theon) — dikaios (disp=4: lit=righteous, tho=just) applied to Cornelius is the highest evaluation available. The MKT 'righteous' preserves the OT covenant-standing sense: Cornelius is dikaios not merely in the moral sense but in the sense of one whose life aligns with God's standard. This is the same word used for John the Baptist's parents (Luke 1:6) and Simeon (Luke 2:25) — the OT-covenant-righteous who are ready to receive messianic fulfillment.
10.23: 'The next day Peter started out with them, and some of the believers from Joppa went along' — the 'six brothers' of 11:12 accompany Peter, providing the legal-testimony minimum (Deut 19:15 — two or three witnesses). Peter does not go alone; the witness-cluster that accompanies him will be the evidence of what happened at Cornelius's house. Luke's mention of their going along anticipates the role they will play in Peter's Jerusalem defense (11:12 — 'these six brothers also came with me').
10.24: 'Cornelius was expecting them and had called together his relatives and close friends' — Cornelius's gathering of relatives (syngeneis) and close friends (ananagkaious philous — necessary/close friends; ananankaios can mean 'compelled by close ties') mirrors the communal-reception pattern of Acts conversions: Lydia and her household (16:15), the Philippian jailer and his household (16:34). The gospel regularly arrives at households, not merely to individuals. The household is the social unit of reception.
10.25: 'As Peter entered the house, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet in reverence' (prosekunēsen — worshipped/did obeisance) — proskuneō is the standard Greek word for obeisance before divine figures, kings, and extraordinary persons. Cornelius does not necessarily intend to worship Peter as a deity; he may be performing the highest available honorific gesture before a divine messenger. Peter's immediate refusal (v.26) is the apostolic pattern: the servant deflects worship from himself to the Lord he represents.
10.26: 'Peter made him get up. Stand up, I am only a man myself' (anastēthi kai gar autēs anthrōpos eimi — rise, for I myself am also a man) — the kai gar autēs (I myself also) makes Peter's humanness explicit: I too belong to the human category; worshipping me misdirects the reverence that belongs to the Lord. The same deflection appears with Paul and Barnabas at Lystra (14:14-15). The refusal of worship is one of the consistent apostolic behaviors that distinguishes the apostles from the divine figures they serve.
10.27: 'Talking with him, Peter went inside and found a large gathering of people' — the conversation (synaphistanē autō — talking/conversing with him) as they walked in mirrors the dialogue-as-relationship pattern of the Emmaus road (Luke 24:14-15). Peter enters the Gentile house in conversation — the boundary-crossing is enacted dialogically before the formal teaching begins. The 'large gathering' (sunelēluthotas pollous) demonstrates that Cornelius's faithful obedience has produced a ready harvest.
10.28: 'You are well aware that it is against our law for a Jew to associate with or visit a Gentile' — Peter's explanation uses the language of his own formation: athemiton (against divine/natural law, unlawful) refers to prohibitions believed to be foundational to the social order. 'But God has shown me that I should not call anyone impure or unclean' — Peter's interpretation of the vision now becomes explicit: the vision was not about food but about people. The transition from food-categories to person-categories is the theological move the vision required.
10.29: 'So when I was sent for, I came without raising any objection. May I ask why you sent for me?' — the logos Peter asks about (dia tina logon — for what word/reason) uses logos (disp=3) in the 'reason/cause' sense rather than the Johannine 'Word.' The MKT 'why' accurately captures this contextual usage. Peter comes 'without raising any objection' (anadikritōs — without making a distinction/without doubting) — using the same diakrinō root as the Spirit's command in v.20.
10.30: 'Four days ago I was in my house praying at this hour, at three in the afternoon' — Cornelius's precise temporal reconstruction (apo tetartēs hēmeras mechri tautēs tēs hōras — from the fourth day until this hour) provides verifiable timeline. The 'three in the afternoon' prayer confirms that Cornelius was observing the Jewish temple prayer-hour — the God-fearer's participation in Jewish liturgical time even without temple access. The angel appeared at the very hour of his prayer.
10.31: 'Cornelius, your prayer has been heard and your gifts to the poor have been remembered before God' — mnēsthēsan (were remembered, aorist passive) uses the divine-memory verb from mnēmosunon (memorial offering) in v.4: God has remembered/called-to-mind Cornelius's prayer and alms. The divine remembering is the OT language of covenant attention: God 'remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1), Abraham (Gen 19:29), Rachel (Gen 30:22), Hannah (1 Sam 1:19). Cornelius's prayer has entered the divine memorial-register.
10.32: 'Now send to Joppa for Simon who is called Peter. He is a guest in the home of Simon the tanner, who lives by the sea' — the repetition of the full address (same as 10:5-6) serves as Luke's verification that the angelic instruction was specific and accurate. The narrative double-report (angel's instruction → Cornelius's report to Peter) is Luke's authentication technique: the story that can be confirmed from two sides is the reliable story.
10.33: 'Now we are all here in the presence of God (enōpion tou theou — before God) to listen to everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us' — ta prostetagmena soi hypo tou Kyriou (all the things commanded you by the Lord) — Cornelius presents his assembled household as ready-recipients of a divine command delivered through Peter. 'In the presence of God' positions the entire gathering as a theophanic assembly: they are not merely listening to a speaker but standing before God while a messenger delivers divine instruction.
10.35: 'Everyone who fears him and does what is right (dikaiosynēn ergazomenos — working righteousness)' — dikaiosynē (disp=4: lit=righteousness, tho=justification) here means practical covenant-alignment: the one who produces righteousness as a way of life. The MKT renders this 'does what is right' — the active, behavioral sense. This is the dikaiosynē that Cornelius embodies: not the forensic justification of Romans but the practical moral alignment with God's standards that genuine fear of God produces. 'Is acceptable to him' (dektos autō estin) — the same word used in Luke 4:24 for the prophet's acceptance in his homeland.
10.36: 'The message God sent to the people of Israel, announcing the good news of peace through Jesus Christ' — the logos (disp=3) in ton logon hon apesteilen is the 'message/word' in the proclamation sense: the specific content of the gospel announcement. The MKT 'message' captures the noun's proclamation-content sense. 'Peace through Jesus Christ' (eirēnēn dia Iēsou Christou) — the preached peace echoes Isa 52:7 ('how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace'). 'He is Lord of all' (houtos estin pantōn Kyrios) — the 'all' (pantōn) includes Gentiles: the Lord's domain is universal, not ethnically restricted.
10.37: 'You know what has happened throughout the province of Judaea, beginning in Galilee after the baptism that John preached' — the Cornelius-audience is assumed to have some knowledge of the Jesus-movement (the widespread character of Jesus's ministry made it regionally known). Peter's sermon begins from what they already know and builds toward what they do not know (the resurrection). The 'beginning in Galilee' framework matches the Gospel's narrative arc (Luke 4:14 — the Galilean beginning of Jesus's public ministry).
10.38: 'How God anointed (echrisen — anointed) Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power' — chriō (disp=2 for pneuma=Spirit) is the anointing-verb from which Christos (anointed/Christ) derives. The MKT 'anointed' correctly preserves the verbal root that names Jesus as the Christ: the naming and the action are etymologically continuous. The anointing is specified as the Jordan-baptism anointing (the Spirit descending, Luke 3:22). 'Who went around doing good (euergetōn — being a benefactor) and healing all who were under the power of the devil' — euergetōn is the benefactor-language of Hellenistic civic culture: Jesus is framed as the ultimate civic benefactor who heals rather than merely honors.
10.39: 'We are witnesses of everything he did in the country of the Jews and in Jerusalem' — the 'we' of apostolic testimony (hēmeis martyres — we witnesses) establishes the apostolic witness-claim on which the sermon's credibility rests. 'They killed him by hanging him on a cross (kremesantes epi xylou — hanging on a tree)' — xylon (tree/wood) rather than stauros (cross) is the word chosen for its LXX resonance: Deut 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse'). Paul explicitly applies this to the crucifixion in Gal 3:13. The tree-hanging language frames the cross in its covenantal-curse context.
10.40: 'But God raised him from the dead on the third day and caused him to be seen' — the passive ēgeiren (raised; God did the raising) and emphanē genesthai (to become visible/manifest) frame the resurrection as a divine act of presentation: God both raised him and made him visible. The resurrection is not merely an event that happened; it is an event that God caused to be witnessed. The 'third day' formula (tē tritē hēmera) is the Passion prediction's fulfillment-marker (Luke 9:22; 18:33; 24:7, 46).
10.41: 'Not to all the people, but to witnesses whom God had already chosen' — the selectivity of the resurrection appearances (ou panti tō laō — not to all the people) is stated explicitly here. God chose specific witnesses (prokeheirotonēmenois — previously appointed/elected, a rare term in the NT) to whom the risen Christ appeared. 'We who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead' — the bodily resurrection is authenticated by table-fellowship: eating and drinking with the risen Christ (Luke 24:41-43 — he ate a piece of broiled fish; Acts 1:4 — eating with them).
10.42: 'He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that he is the one whom God appointed as judge of the living and the dead (kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn — judge of the living and the dead)' — the appointed-judge formula echoes Dan 7:13-14 (the Son of Man given authority to judge all peoples). 'Living and dead' extends the judgment's scope beyond the present generation. The resurrection (v.40) is what qualifies Jesus for the judge-role: the one who conquered death is qualified to preside over the judgment of both the living and the dead.
10.43: 'All the prophets testify about him that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name' — the apostolic appeal to prophetic testimony (pantes hoi prophētai — all the prophets) is a sweeping claim: the entire prophetic corpus points to Christ. 'Everyone who believes' (panta ton pisteuonta — everyone who believes) is the universal-scope formulation that prepares for the Spirit's fall on the uncircumcised Gentiles in the next verse. The faith-criterion displaces the ethnicity-criterion as the condition for receiving forgiveness.
10.45: 'The circumcised believers who had come with Peter were astonished that the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out even on Gentiles' — epechythē (had been poured out — aorist passive of ekcheō, the same verb as Joel 2:28 LXX 'I will pour out my Spirit') connects the Cornelius Pentecost explicitly to the Joel-Pentecost promise. 'Circumcised believers' (hoi ek peritomēs pistoi — the faithful ones of the circumcision) marks the Jewish-Christian observers as the legitimate witnesses to what happened: their astonishment is the evidence's authentication.
10.46: 'For they heard them speaking in tongues and praising God' — the two signs (glossais lalountōn — speaking with tongues; megalunontōn ton theon — magnifying God) are the same twin-evidence as Pentecost (2:4 — spoke in other tongues; 2:11 — 'we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God'). The tongue-speech is the Spirit's authentication in recognizable form to the Jewish observers. 'Then Peter said' — Peter's question follows immediately from the evidence; he does not deliberate or delay.
10.47: 'Surely no one can stand in the way of their being baptised with water?' (mēti to hydōr kōlusai dynatai tis — can anyone withhold/prevent the water?) — kōlyō (to prevent/withhold) is the key baptism-verb in Acts: Philip uses it in the Ethiopian eunuch's baptism (8:36 — 'what prevents me from being baptized?'). The inability to prevent water-baptism follows from the inability to prevent Spirit-baptism that has already occurred. The Spirit's gift precedes the water-seal; the water-seal cannot be withheld after the Spirit has already given the inward reality.
10.48: 'So he ordered that they be baptised in the name of Jesus Christ' — the command-form (prosetaxen) makes baptism an apostolic command, not merely an option. 'In the name of Jesus Christ' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou) — the single-name formula (vs. the threefold formula of Matt 28:19) is the standard Acts baptism-formula; both formulas identify the same trinitarian authority. 'Then they asked Peter to stay with them for a few days' — the post-baptismal table-fellowship that follows (implied in the staying) is the seal of the social integration that the Spirit-gift and baptism have enacted.

13.39: en touto pas ho pisteuon dikaioutai (en toutō pas ho pisteuōn dikaioutai): Acts' only occurrence of dikaioō (justify). Luke's vocabulary is normally sōzō (save) and aphesis (forgiveness); the appearance of Pauline justification language in Paul's first major sermon confirms Luke knew Paul's theological categories and represents them accurately. apo pantōn hōn ouk ēdunēthēte en nomō Mōuseōs dikaiōthenai: 'from everything from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses' — the law's incompleteness is the foil that makes Christ's justification complete and universally available.
13.1: prophētai kai didaskaloi ('prophets and teachers'): the two foundational ministries of the Antioch community. The list of names is striking — Barnabas (Cypriot Levite), Simeon called Niger (perhaps African, Niger = 'black'), Lucius of Cyrene (North African), Manaen (a childhood companion of Herod Antipas — syntrophos), and Saul. The community's diversity — African, diaspora, royal-adjacent, Pharisee — is itself a sign of the Spirit's work transcending ethnic and social boundaries.
