13.2: leitourgountōn de autōn tō kyriō kai nēsteuontōn ('while they were serving the Lord and fasting'): leitourgeō is the LXX term for Levitical cultic service (Num 8:22; 16:9); Luke applies it to the church's corporate worship, framing the community's prayer and fasting as priestly ministry. to Pneuma to Hagion eipen ('the Holy Spirit said'): direct divine speech to the worshipping community — not through vision or dream but as direct prophetic address. This is the apostolic-age parallel to OT prophetic commissioning (Isa 6:8; Jer 1:5).
13.3: nēsteusantes kai proseuxamenoi kai epithentes tas cheiras autois apelysan: the commissioning sequence — fasting, prayer, handlaying, sending — is the church's missionary ordination rite. The same triad appears at Stephen and the Seven's appointment (6:6) and anticipates the Pastoral Epistles' eldership-ordination (1 Tim 4:14; 2 Tim 1:6). It is the community's formal act of releasing Barnabas and Saul from local accountability to a broader mission accountability, while the Spirit's commissioning (v.2) is the primary authorization.
13.4: ekpempomenoi hypo tou Hagiou Pneumatos ('sent out by the Holy Spirit'): the passive participle makes the Spirit the primary sender; the community's sending in v.3 is the human correlate. Luke's mission theology is consistently double-agented: the Spirit initiates and sends; the community confirms and releases. The route — Seleucia (port city of Antioch) → Cyprus → eventually Asia Minor — follows the natural sea routes of the eastern Mediterranean.
13.5: en tais synagōgais tōn Ioudaiōn katēngellon ton logon tou theou ('they proclaimed the word of God in the synagogues of the Jews'): Paul's consistent synagogue-first strategy throughout Acts (13:5, 14; 14:1; 17:1, 10, 17; 18:4, 19; 19:8) reflects both theology (Rom 1:16: 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek') and pragmatic access to biblically literate audiences. The synagogue provided a ready context: readers of Scripture who needed only to hear that the prophecies were fulfilled.
13.6: Bariēsoun ('Bar-Jesus', 'son of Jesus/salvation'): the name is ironic — a man named 'son of salvation' opposes the message of salvation. Luke identifies him as a magos (sorcerer/magician) and pseudoprophētēs (false prophet); the combination appears in Deut 13:1-5 as precisely the profile of those who lead Israel away from God. The confrontation will echo Moses's contest with Pharaoh's magicians (Exod 7-8) in its outcome.
13.7: Sergios Paulos, anēr synetōs ('Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man'): the designation synetos (intelligent, prudent) anticipates his receptivity to the gospel. Archaeological evidence corroborates a Sergius Paulus as a Roman official in Cyprus in roughly this period. The proconsul's summoning of Barnabas and Saul (epezētēsen akousai ton logon tou theou, 'sought to hear the word of God') shows the gospel's attraction to thoughtful Gentile officials — a recurring Lukan theme.
13.8: Elymas ho magos ('Elymas the sorcerer'): Luke's parenthetical explanation — 'for that is what his name means' — is puzzling since Elymas does not obviously derive from Bar-Jesus; the connection may be through an Aramaic word for 'wise man' or 'sorcerer'. Whatever its etymology, Luke presents the name as identifying his function. Anthistato ('opposed them continually'): the imperfect tense marks persistent, ongoing opposition — not a single objection but a sustained effort to prevent the proconsul's conversion.
13.9: Saulos de, ho kai Paulos ('Saul, who was also called Paul'): Luke's transition formula marks the permanent shift to the Hellenistic cognomen. 'Paul' (Paulos) was a standard Roman family name; as a Roman citizen, Saul likely always had both names. From this point in Acts, Luke uses 'Paul' consistently, reflecting the name's appropriateness for the Gentile mission context. Plēstheis Pneumatos Hagiou ('filled with the Holy Spirit'): the same Spirit-filling formula as Peter's (4:8; 13:9) before a confrontation — the power of discernment and judgment is explicitly divine.
13.10: huie diabolou, echthre pasēs dikaiosynēs ('son of the devil, enemy of all righteousness'): the denunciation is the antithesis of the community's identity markers. Bar-Jesus is named 'son of the devil' in contrast to the 'children of Abraham' (v.26); he is an 'enemy of righteousness' in contrast to the righteous who are vindicated; he is 'full of all deceit and fraud' in contrast to the Spirit-filled who speak truth. Diastrepheōn ('perverting/twisting'): the word for making crooked what God makes straight (Isa 40:4 reversed).
13.11: typhlōthēsē ('you will be blind'): the temporary blindness imposed on Elymas mirrors Paul's own experience on the Damascus Road (9:8-9) — a pointed irony. Where Paul's blindness preceded sight (physical and spiritual), Elymas's blindness marks judgment on resistance. Parachrēma de epesen ep' auton achlys kai skotos ('immediately a mist and darkness fell on him'): the vocabulary is medical — achlys (mist) is used in medical texts for visual clouding. The judgment is precise and immediate, demonstrating the Holy Spirit's authority over spiritual opposition.
13.12: episteusen ekplēssomenos epi tē didachē tou kyriou ('he believed, astonished at the teaching about the Lord'): the proconsul's conversion is triggered by what he saw (the sorcerer's blindness) but anchored in the teaching (didachē). Luke does not describe his response as astonishment at miracle alone but at the teaching confirmed by miracle — precisely the apostolic hermeneutic: signs authenticate the message, but the message is the content of faith.
13.13: hoi peri Paulon ('those around Paul'): the shift in grammatical priority — Paul named first, Barnabas following — marks the reversal from 13:2 where 'Barnabas and Saul' were listed in that order. John Mark's departure from Perga hupestrephsen eis Ierosolymata ('returned to Jerusalem') will become a source of significant dispute (15:37-39). Luke reports the departure without explanation; Paul later views it as abandonment; Barnabas will defend Mark. The seed of a future division is planted without commentary.
13.14: Pisidian Antioch: one of the Seleucid cities named Antioch; distinct from Syrian Antioch. The synagogue service on the Sabbath follows the standard Second Temple order: Scripture reading (anagnōsis nomou kai tōn prophētōn, v.15) followed by homiletical application. The invitation to speak — 'if you have a word of exhortation' (logos paraklēseōs) — is the standard opening for visiting teachers; the phrase will recur in Heb 13:22 as the self-description of that letter.
13.15: logos paraklēseōs ('word of encouragement/exhortation'): the synagogue's invitation uses the same word-family as Barnabas's name (huios paraklēseōs, 4:36), linking the missionary community's character to its proclamation. Paul's response in vv.16-41 is the longest speech of his early ministry in Acts and the most detailed example of his synagogue preaching — a survey of salvation history from election to resurrection.
13.16: kataseisas tē cheiri ('motioning with his hand'): the oratorial gesture signaling the audience to settle for formal speech — the same as Peter's in 12:17 and Paul's later in 21:40. The dual address — andres Israēlitai (Israelite men) and hoi phoboumenoi ton theon (those who fear God, i.e., Gentile sympathizers) — establishes from the opening that the gospel is addressed to both communities. Paul's speech to both audiences in one setting enacts the 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek' principle.
13.17: exelexato tous pateras hēmōn ('he chose our ancestors'): Paul's speech begins with election — God's sovereign choice of Israel — before moving through the history of redemption. Hypsōsen ton laon ('exalted/lifted up the people'): the LXX term for God's special attention to Israel, used in Exod 1:7 for Israel's growth and here for their providential elevation during the Egyptian sojourn. The speech covers election → exodus → wilderness → conquest → judges → monarchy → David → Christ in miniature salvation-history form.
13.18: etropophoresan (etropophoresan): a text-critical crux. The majority text reads etrophophoresan ('bore them as a nurse bears a child' — following Deut 1:31 LXX where God 'carried' Israel like a parent). The alternative etropophoresan (from tropos, manner) means 'endured their manner/conduct.' The MKT follows the nursing/bearing reading, which fits the exodus-care theme. Either reading emphasizes God's patient sustaining of Israel through the wilderness.
13.19: kateklēronomēsen tēn gēn autōn ('distributed their land as inheritance'): the conquest under Joshua is compressed into one phrase. Klēronomeō (inherit) and its cognates are Luke's vocabulary for the covenant land-promise's fulfillment; the New Testament applies the same vocabulary to the eschatological inheritance of the kingdom (Matt 5:5; 25:34; Gal 3:18). Paul's salvation-history sketch treats the land-inheritance as one stage in the larger divine economy.
13.20: hōs etesin tetrakosiois kai pentēkonta ('about 450 years'): the chronological summary integrating the periods from the patriarchs through the judges is notoriously difficult to reconcile with OT data (which, depending on how periods are calculated, yields different totals). The approximate 'about' (hōs) signals that Paul is not providing precise genealogical chronology but a rhetorical survey of God's faithfulness across the sweep of history.
13.21: Saoul huion Kis, andra ek phylēs Beniamin: the identification of Israel's first king by tribal origin connects to the Benjaminite identity of Paul himself (Phil 3:5, 'of the tribe of Benjamin'). Whether the name-echo between Saul the king and Saul/Paul the apostle is deliberate is debated; Luke may be drawing attention to the parallel — a Benjaminite named Saul who was set aside in God's purposes for a greater Davidic mission.
13.22: euron Dauid ton tou Iessai, andra kata tēn kardian mou: Paul blends 1 Sam 13:14 ('a man after God's own heart') with Ps 89:20 ('I have found David, my servant') — the same blend in the same application as Acts 4:25. The David testimony establishes the Davidic typology that leads to Christ: if David was the man after God's heart yet died and saw decay (v.36), the one whom God truly sought — the perfectly faithful Son of David — must be the one who did not see corruption.
13.23: toutou ho theos apo tou spermatos kat' epangelian ēgagen tō Israēl sōtēra Iēsoun ('from this man's seed God brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus, as promised'): the sentence is the hinge of the entire speech. Every line from Abraham to David was moving toward this claim. Kat' epangelian ('according to promise'): the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16) is the specific promise in view; sōtēra (Savior) is the title applied to Jesus that makes explicit the salvation-history logic Paul has been tracing.
13.24: pro prosōpou tēs eisodou autou ('before his coming'): the temporal idiom literally means 'before the face of his entry' — John's preparation ministry clears the way for Jesus as forerunner. Paul's inclusion of John's ministry in the synagogue sermon is a consistent element of apostolic preaching (10:37; cf. Mark 1:1-4); it establishes chronological anchoring for the Jesus-claim and draws the John-Jesus connection for audiences who knew John's movement.
13.25: erpomenon de Iōannou ton dromos ('as John was completing his course'): the racing metaphor — dromos (race-course) — appears here and in Paul's farewell at Miletus (20:24) and his self-assessment in 2 Tim 4:7. John completing his course is fulfilled preparation; Paul completing his course will be the apostolic fulfillment of the same pattern. The 'sandal' saying — 'I am not worthy to untie the sandals of his feet' — is verbatim consistent across all four Gospels and Acts, suggesting a fixed tradition.
13.26: andres adelphoi, huioi genous Abraam kai hoi en hymin phoboumenoi ton theon: Paul addresses both groups simultaneously — 'brothers, sons of the family of Abraham, and those among you who fear God' — making explicit that the message of salvation is sent to both (hēmin ho logos tēs sōtērias tautēs exapestalē). The second-person plural includes Gentile God-fearers in 'us,' which is the theological claim the subsequent mission will enact.
13.27: agnoēsantes ('not recognizing/in ignorance'): Paul, like Peter (3:17), attributes the Jerusalem authorities' action to ignorance — not exculpatory ignorance but culpable blindness. Tas phōnas tōn prophētōn ... katakrinantes eplērōsan ('fulfilling the prophets in their condemnation'): the paradox of fulfillment-through-opposition is central to Luke's theology: the very act of rejecting Jesus accomplished what the prophets had written about him. Opposition is not the frustration of the divine plan but its instrument.
13.28: mēdemian aitian thanatou heurontes ('finding no grounds for a death sentence'): the triple innocence declarations of the Passion (Luke 23:4, 14, 22 — Pilate) are echoed in Paul's summary; he emphasizes that the legal verdict was innocent while the execution proceeded regardless. This is not merely historical information but apologetic: Jesus was condemned without legal warrant, which means the condemnation was unjust and the resurrection is God's reversal of an injustice.
13.29: panta ta peri autou gegrammena ('all the things written about him'): the 'all things written' formula (Luke 24:44-46; Acts 13:27, 29) is Luke's comprehensive Scripture-fulfillment claim. Paul does not specify which texts — it is the entire scriptural trajectory, not proof-texts, that has been fulfilled. Ethekan eis mnēmeion ('placed in a tomb'): the burial is included in the kerygmatic sequence (cf. 1 Cor 15:4 — 'he was buried') because it establishes the physical reality of the death that the resurrection reverses.
13.30: ho de theos ēgeiren auton ek nekrōn ('but God raised him from the dead'): the structural de ('but') marks the dramatic reversal at the center of the kerygma. The resurrection proclamation is syntactically simple — subject (God), verb (raised), object (him), prepositional phrase (from the dead) — because its content is world-altering enough to require no rhetorical embellishment. Luke preserves this bare form consistently in apostolic speech (2:24, 32; 3:15; 4:10; 5:30; 10:40; 13:30) as the irreducible core.
13.31: hoi nyn eisin martyres autou pros ton laon ('who are now his witnesses to the people'): the Galilean witnesses — those who accompanied Jesus from Galilee to Jerusalem — constitute the apostolic testimony. Paul distinguishes himself from these witnesses while aligning with their message; he belongs to a different category of witness (the Damascus encounter, 9:3-6; 22:6-11; 26:12-18) but preaches the same risen Lord. The distinction is honest and significant: Paul's apostleship rests on a post-resurrection appearance, not on the pre-Passion companionship.
13.32: hēmeis hymas euangelizometha tēn pros tous pateras epangelian genomenēn ('we proclaim to you the good news of the promise made to the fathers'): the euangelion (good news) is explicitly identified as the fulfillment of the patriarchal promises. Paul's preaching is not the announcement of a new religion but the declaration that Israel's long-awaited inheritance has arrived. The promise-fulfillment schema is the interpretive grid for everything that follows in vv.33-37.
13.33: graphetai gar en tō psalmō tō deuterō ('for it is written in the second Psalm'): Ps 2:7 — 'You are my Son; today I have become your Father' — is applied to the resurrection. The resurrection is the 'today' of the divine declaration; God publicly declares Jesus to be the Son on the day he raises him from the dead (cf. Rom 1:4: 'declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead'). The psalm's coronation language is resurrection-coronation language for Paul here.
13.34: dōsō hymin ta hosia Dauid ta pista (Isa 55:3 LXX): 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David'. The LXX ta hosia ('the holy/sure things') translates Hebrew chesed (covenant-loyalty), with the LXX adding a layer of holiness (hosios) to the faithfulness concept. Paul's use of this text — the sure Davidic blessings that will not decay — grounds the resurrection in the Davidic covenant: the resurrection is God's faithfulness to his sworn commitment.
13.35: dioti kai en heterō legei, Ou dōseis ton hosion sou idein diaphthoran (Ps 16:10): Paul cites Ps 16:10 as confirmation of 13:34, just as Peter did in Acts 2:27. The hosion ('holy one') whose body does not see corruption (diaphthora) cannot be David, since David died, was buried, and his tomb is known (2:29). The only candidate for the Psalm's fulfillment is the one whom God raised without allowing his body to decay — Jesus of Nazareth.
13.36: Dauid men gar idiai genea hypeyretesan tē tou theou boulē ekoimēthē ('for David, having served God's purpose in his own generation, fell asleep'): the parallel contrast — David served his generation and died; the one God raised serves all generations — is the climax of Paul's argument. Ekoimēthē ('fell asleep'): the Christian euphemism for death rooted in Jewish practice (1 Kgs 2:10 LXX; Job 3:13; Ps 87:6 LXX). Prosetethē pros tous pateras autou kai eiden diaphthoran ('was gathered to his fathers and saw decay') is precisely what the resurrection-candidate of Ps 16:10 did not experience.
13.37: hon de ho theos ēgeiren ouk eiden diaphthoran ('but the one whom God raised did not see decay'): the stark contrast of v.36-37 is Paul's refutation of the suggestion that Ps 16:10 applies to David. The logic is simple: David saw decay; the Psalm promises the Holy One will not see decay; therefore David cannot be the Psalm's referent; the one who did not see decay — Jesus, raised by God — is the Psalm's fulfillment. The argument is identical to Peter's at Pentecost (2:29-31).
13.38: aphesis hamartiōn ('forgiveness of sins'): the offer that crystallizes the entire salvation-history argument. Through Christ — not through Moses (apo pantōn hōn ouk ēdunēthēte en nomō Mōuseōs dikaiōthenai, v.39) — forgiveness is proclaimed. Luke's aphesis vocabulary is his characteristic term for the new-covenant blessing (Luke 1:77; 3:3; 4:18; 24:47; Acts 2:38; 5:31; 10:43; 13:38; 26:18). It carries the sense of release, cancellation of debt — the judicial erasure of the record of sin.
13.40: blepete oun mē epelthē to eirēmenon en tois prophētais ('therefore take care that what is spoken by the prophets does not happen to you'): the warning marks the transition from invitation to judgment — those who hear and refuse the gospel face the prophetic warning's application to themselves. Paul's sermon moves in the same arc as the OT prophets: proclamation → invitation → warning. The Hab 1:5 citation (v.41) is the scriptural ground of the warning.
13.41: kataphronētai kai thaumasate kai aphanisthēte (Hab 1:5 LXX): 'scoffers, wonder and perish!' Paul applies Habakkuk's warning about the Babylonian invasion to the even more incredible 'work' of the resurrection. Habakkuk warned Israel that they would not believe even when told what God was doing through Babylon; Paul warns the synagogue that the same unbelief will prove fatal when confronted with the even more astonishing work of the risen Messiah.
13.42: The congregation's response — parekloun eis to metaxy sabbaton lalēthēnai autois ta rhēmata tauta ('begged that these words be spoken to them on the following Sabbath') — marks the initial positive reception that Luke regularly records before the opposition crystallizes (cf. 17:11-12, 32-33). The request for a return visit is the synagogue's standard practice for a compelling visitor-teacher; it gives Paul a second platform and extends the engagement.
13.43: proseilython polloi ... tois paroikois ('many... and proselytes followed'): paroikoi (resident aliens, sojourners) in the LXX describes Israel's status in Egypt; here it identifies Gentile proselytes who have fully converted to Judaism. Peithein autous prosmenein tē chariti tou theou ('persuading them to remain in the grace of God'): Paul's pastoral exhortation to the newly responsive — grace must be continued in, not merely initially received. The language anticipates the Pauline charis theology of the letters.
13.44: schedon hē pasa polis synēchthē akousai ton logon tou kyriou ('almost the whole city gathered to hear the word of the Lord'): Luke's 'almost the whole city' is his characteristic rhetorical hyperbole for civic-scale reception of the gospel (cf. 19:26). The scale of the gathering provokes the jealousy of v.45 — the Jewish leaders' opposition is reactive to the gospel's Gentile success, not a pre-existing theological position against Paul personally.
13.45: zēlou eplēsthēsan ('they were filled with jealousy'): the same plēroō-filling vocabulary used throughout Acts — here for jealousy rather than the Spirit. Luke's contrast is pointed: Spirit-filled witnesses and jealousy-filled opponents. Blasphēmountes ('blaspheming'): the opponents' attacks cross into blasphemy — not against the law or Paul personally but against the word of God and the one it proclaims.
13.46: hymin ēn anagkaion prōton lalēthēnai ton logon ('it was necessary to speak the word of God to you first'): anagkaios (necessary, obligatory) carries divine compulsion — not merely strategic priority but theological necessity. The 'to the Jew first' principle (Rom 1:16) is here stated as the church's non-negotiable missional order. The turning to the Gentiles is not abandonment of Israel but the fulfilment of Israel's missionary vocation: Israel was meant to be the vehicle through which the Gentiles received the blessing (Gen 12:3).
13.47: tetheka se eis phōs ethnōn (Isa 49:6 LXX): 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles.' Paul and Barnabas apply the Second Servant Song's mission directly to themselves: the Servant's commission to be a light to the Gentiles becomes the apostolic commission. The same text cited by Simeon at Jesus's presentation (Luke 2:32) is now cited by the apostles as their own mandate — they continue the Servant's work through Spirit-empowered mission.
13.48: tetagmenoi eis zōēn aiōnion ('appointed/ordained to eternal life'): the perfect passive participle — a completed, prior action with present effect — implies divine prior appointment. Those who believe are identified as those who had been appointed to eternal life; the appointment precedes the belief and is expressed in the belief. Luke does not resolve the tension between divine appointment and human response; he records both without philosophical qualification, as does Paul in his letters (Eph 1:4-5; Rom 8:29-30).
13.49: diephereito de ho logos tou kyriou di' holēs tēs chōras ('the word of the Lord spread through the whole region'): the fifth of Luke's growth-summary refrains (6:7; 12:24; 13:49; 19:20; 28:30-31). Each appears at a narrative turning point; here it marks the successful establishment of the gospel in the Pisidian region before the expulsion that follows. The word's spread is not contingent on the missionaries' continued presence — it has been planted and takes root independently.
13.50: epeseirasan tinas gynaikas tais euschēmonas kai tous prōtous tēs poleōs ('they stirred up devout women of high standing and the leading men of the city'): the opposition strategy — mobilizing socially prominent women and civic leaders against the missionaries — reflects the typical mechanics of community-level expulsion. Prominent women attached to the synagogue as God-fearers had social influence over their husbands; their agitation through their husbands produced the official civic response of expulsion.
13.51: ektinaxamenoi ton koniorton tōn podōn ('shaking the dust from their feet'): the gesture is Jesus's instruction for rejected messengers (Luke 10:11; Matt 10:14; Mark 6:11); the dust-shaking is a visible acted judgment — 'your town's dust, which we carry no further'. Paul and Barnabas perform it 'against them' (ep' autous) — not as personal grievance but as prophetic sign. The missionaries proceed to Iconium; the rejected city receives the sign of coming judgment.
13.52: hoi de mathētai eplērounto charas kai Pneumatos Hagiou ('the disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit'): Luke closes the Antioch episode with a double-filling — joy and the Holy Spirit together — as the condition of the community left behind. The pairing of joy (chara) and Spirit throughout Luke-Acts (Luke 2:10; 10:21; Acts 2:46; 8:8, 39; 13:52; 15:3) signals the authentic mark of Spirit-life. The missionaries are expelled; the community they leave is thriving. This is Luke's counterintuitive measure of mission success.

15.11: dia tes charitos tou kyriou Iesou pisteuomen sothenai (dia tēs charitos tou kyriou Iēsou pisteuomen sōthēnai): Peter's climactic speech at the Jerusalem council: 'we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we are saved, just as they are'. The kathos kakeinoi ('just as they also') is the decisive reversal — not Gentiles saved like Jews, but Jews saved like Gentiles: by grace through faith, not by Torah-observance. Charis (grace) as the mode, pisteuomen (we believe) as the means, sōthēnai (to be saved) as the end — Pauline soteriology in Petrine formulation.
15.1: ean mē peritmnēthēte tō ethei tō Mōuseōs ou dynasthe sōthēnai ('unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses you cannot be saved'): the Judaizers' statement ties salvation to Torah-observance via the covenant-entry rite. The ean mē conditional makes circumcision necessary for salvation — not merely beneficial but soteriologically essential. Paul's response in Galatians (3:1-5; 5:2-6) is his extended refutation of exactly this position.
15.2: staseōs kai zētēseōs ouk oligēs ('no small dispute and debate'): stasis (strife, sedition) and zētēsis (questioning, dispute) together describe a sharp public controversy. The Lukan understatement ouk oligēs (not small = considerable) mirrors Galatians' account of Paul 'withstanding Peter to the face' (Gal 2:11) — the confrontation was serious. The church's decision to send a delegation to Jerusalem acknowledges that this requires authoritative resolution beyond Antioch's capacity.
15.3: ekdiēgoumenoi tēn epistrophēn tōn ethnōn ('describing the conversion of the Gentiles'): epistrophē (turning, conversion) is the standard conversion-term (the noun form of epistrephō); the Gentiles are 'those who have turned' from idols to God. The reports they gave in Phoenicia and Samaria produce charan megalēn (great joy) in the communities there — the same joy-marker for eschatological arrival. The groundwork for Jerusalem's reception is laid by the journey itself.
15.4: eis Ierousalēm paredechthēsan apo tēs ekklēsias kai tōn apostolōn kai tōn presbyterōn ('they were welcomed by the church and the apostles and elders'): the triple reception (church + apostles + elders) indicates formal recognition, not just hospitality. The apostles and elders represent the theological and administrative authority; the whole church represents the community whose practice is at stake. Both dimensions — doctrinal and communal — are engaged in the council process.
15.5: exanestēsan de tines tōn apo tēs haireseōs tōn Pharisaiōn pepisteukotos ('some who belonged to the party of the Pharisees who had believed stood up'): hairesis (party, sect) is not pejorative in Luke's usage — simply a philosophical-religious school. The Pharisees are the Torah-observant wing of the Jerusalem church; their demand (dei: it is necessary) mirrors the Judaizers' Antioch position. Converted Pharisees brought both rigorous hermeneutical skill and intense covenant-loyalty to the church.
15.6: synēchthēsan te hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi idein peri tou logou toutou ('the apostles and elders gathered to consider this matter'): the Jerusalem council is an apostolic-presbyteral deliberative body; its structure mirrors the Jewish Sanhedrin's form while its authority derives from the apostolic eyewitness foundation. The verb idein (to see, consider) indicates careful discernment, not a hasty ruling.
15.7: pollēs de zētēseōs genomenes anastas Petros eipen ('after much discussion Peter stood up and said'): the deliberative process was genuine — much debate (pollēs zētēseōs) before Peter's intervention. Peter appeals to aph' hēmerōn archaīōn (from early days) — the Cornelius event as the foundational precedent, earlier even than the first missionary journey. God's choice of Peter for the Cornelius mission makes him the witness of God's prior action, not the author of a new policy.
15.8: kardiognōstēs ho theos (God the heart-knower): the same compound title as Acts 1:24, used in the prayer for Matthias's replacement — the God who knows hearts is the decisive witness to Cornelius's genuine faith. edōken to Pneuma to Hagion autois kathōs kai hēmin ('gave the Holy Spirit to them just as also to us'): the Spirit-gift to uncircumcised Gentiles is God's own verdict on their acceptability — an argument from divine action rather than theological deduction.
15.9: katharisas tē pistei tas kardias autōn ('cleansing their hearts by faith'): the heart-cleansing is the internal work corresponding to the ritual purity that circumcision-advocates were requiring externally. Pistis (faith) is the divine cleansing agent — not Torah-observance, not ritual, but faith. The verb diekrine (he made no distinction) insists on the absolute parity of the cleansing: identical hearts, identical Spirit, identical faith, identical purity.
15.10: nun oun ti peirazete ton theon ('so now why do you test God?'): testing God is the Exodus idiom for Israel's faithless complaint against God's provision (Exod 17:2; Ps 95:9). zygon (zygon, yoke): the Torah-as-yoke metaphor appears in rabbinic literature as positive ('the yoke of the kingdom of heaven'); Peter deploys it critically — a yoke hēmeis oute hoi pateres hēmōn ischysamen bastasai (neither we nor our fathers were able to bear). Jesus's contrasting easy yoke (Matt 11:29-30) is the implicit antithesis.
15.12: esigēsen de pan to plēthos ('the whole assembly became silent'): the silence after Peter's speech marks a rhetorical turning point — the question-raising has been addressed. Barnaban kai Paulon exēgoumenon hosa epoiēsen ho theos sēmeia kai terata en tois ethnesin di' autōn: Barnabas precedes Paul here (as in the first missionary journey's early chapters, before Paul's prominence grew); the signs and wonders confirm the mission's divine authorization to the Jerusalem audience.
15.13: meta de to sigēsai autous apekrithē Iakōbos ('after they fell silent James answered'): James's position as speaker last and as the one who delivers the judgment (krisis, v.19) confirms his leadership role in the Jerusalem church by Acts 15. As the Lord's brother, his authority is not apostolic in the Twelve-sense but familial and earned; his credibility with Torah-observant believers is essential for the ruling's acceptance.
15.14: Symeōn exēgēsato kathōs prōton ho theos epeskepsato labein ex ethnōn laon tō onomati autou ('Simeon described how God first visited to take from the Gentiles a people for his name'): James uses Symeōn — the Semitic/Aramaic form of Peter's name — signaling a Hebrew-language discourse register. Episkeptomai (visit, look after) is the OT divine-visit verb (Exod 4:31; Luke 1:68); God's Gentile-inclusion is an act of divine visitation, not human initiative.
15.15: toutō symphōnousin hoi logoi tōn prophētōn ('with this the words of the prophets agree'): James grounds the Cornelius-event interpretation in the prophetic scriptures — his hermeneutic is: divine act → scriptural confirmation. The prophets 'agree' (symphōnousin) with what has happened; the event is not a theological novelty but the realization of what Scripture anticipated. James's citation of Amos 9:11-12 LXX is the most important OT quotation in Acts after the Pentecost Joel-citation.
15.16: anorthōsō tēn skēnēn Dauid tēn peptōkuian ('I will rebuild the fallen tent of David'): James quotes Amos 9:11 LXX — the 'tent of David' (skēnē) is the fallen Davidic dynasty. The LXX differs significantly from the MT at vv.11-12; skēnē (tent/booth) reflects the LXX rendering of Hebrew sukkah. Anorthōsō (I will straighten up, rebuild) is the same word used for the lame man who stood straight (Luke 13:13); the Davidic restoration is a straightening-up of what had fallen.
15.17: hopōs an ekzētēsōsin hoi kataloipoi tōn anthrōpōn ton kyrion kai panta ta ethnē ('so that the remnant of humanity may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles'): the LXX of Amos 9:12 has 'humanity' (anthrōpōn) where the MT has 'Edom' — a reading difference that makes the prophecy explicitly universal. James's use of the LXX version is precise: he cites the form that supports the universal Gentile inclusion. Eph' hous epikeklētai to onoma mou ep' autous ('over whom my name is called') is the covenant-ownership formula applied to Gentile believers.
15.18: gnōsta ap' aiōnos ('known from of old'): the Gentile inclusion was not God's plan B but eternal intention now enacted. The statement is the capstone of James's scriptural argument: what is happening (Gentile conversion) is not a surprise development but the fulfillment of the divinely-foreknown plan. Gnōsta (known, manifest) — the same root as James's earlier 'God has made known' (egnōrisen) in his framing of v.14.
15.19: dio egō krinō mē parenochleisthai tois apo tōn ethnōn epistrephoysin epi ton theon ('therefore my judgment is not to trouble those from the Gentiles who are turning to God'): krinō (I judge, I decide) is James's formal ruling word — not a suggestion but a judicial determination. Parenochleō (trouble, harass, add burden) is strong: the Torah-requirement would constitute harassment of genuine converts. The ruling protects the Gentile believers from unnecessary burden while establishing minimal boundaries for table-fellowship.
15.20: all' episteilai autois tou apechesthai ... eidōlothuton kai porneia kai pniktou kai haimatos ('write to them to abstain from... food polluted by idols, sexual immorality, meat of strangled animals, blood'): the Apostolic Decree's four abstentions. The list draws on Lev 17-18, which applied certain prohibitions to the alien resident (ger) in Israel — the Gentile living within the covenant community. The Decree establishes table-fellowship conditions, not salvation conditions; it enables Gentile and Jewish believers to eat together.
15.21: Mōusēs gar ek geneōn archaīōn kata polin tous kērussontas auton echei ('Moses has had preachers in every city from ancient generations'): the reference to Moses being preached in every city gives the rationale for the Decree — Jewish communities throughout the diaspora already observe these practices, and Gentile believers who share table-fellowship with them need minimal common ground. The Decree is not soteriological but social-ecclesiological: enabling the mixed Jewish-Gentile church.
15.22: tote edoxe tois apostolois kai tois presbyterois syn holē tē ekklēsia ('then it seemed good to the apostles and elders with the whole church'): edoxe (seemed good, pleased) is the decision-consensus verb — the same root as v.28's edoxen tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin. The council's decision is simultaneously the whole church's decision: apostolic, presbyteral, and congregational authority converge in the ruling. Eklexamenous andras ex autōn (choosing men from among themselves): Judas Barsabbas and Silas are sent as official delegates.
15.23: hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi adelphoi tois kata tēn Antiocheian kai Syrian kai Kilikian ('the apostles and elders, brothers, to the brothers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia'): the letter's address form is the standard Greek epistolary salutation adapted for Christian use. The senders identify as adelphoi (brothers) writing to adelphoi — the familial equality is built into the greeting. The geographic scope includes Cilicia (Paul's home region) — foreshadowing his later ministry there.
15.24: epeidie ēkousamen hoti tines ex hēmōn exelthontes etaraxan hymas logois ('we have heard that some went out from us and troubled you with words'): the council explicitly distances itself from the Judaizers — they went out 'from us' but without authorization (ois ou diesteilametha, 'to whom we gave no instructions'). The phrase anaskeuazontes tas psychas hymōn (unsettling your minds/souls) is the pastoral damage-assessment: not merely theological error but personal destabilization.
15.25: edoxen hēmin genomenois homothumadon ('it seemed good to us having become of one accord'): homothymadon appears here one final time — the apostolic unity that characterized Pentecost (2:1) now characterizes the council's decision. The unanimity is itself a sign; the Decree is not a compromise position but a genuinely unified judgment. Eklexamenois andras pempsai pros hymas: the choice of delegates rather than a letter alone shows the council understood that the decree needed personal exposition.
15.26: anthrōpois paradedōkosi tas psychas autōn hyper tou onomatos tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou ('men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ'): paradidōmi tēn psychēn (give up one's life) is the martyr-language; the council's endorsement of Paul and Barnabas includes an official recognition of their life-risking service. The phrase validates their authority to the Antioch church: these are men whose credentials are written in physical danger.
15.27: Ioudan kai Silan kai autous dia logou apangelountas ta auta ('Judas and Silas who themselves will tell you the same things by word of mouth'): the letter needs oral amplification — written decrees require living interpreters who can answer questions and provide the pastoral rationale. Silas (Sylvanus in Paul's letters, 1 Thess 1:1; 2 Cor 1:19; 1 Pet 5:12) becomes Paul's companion in Acts 15:40-18:5.
15.28: edoxen gar tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin ('it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us'): the formula is the council's distinctive self-understanding — the ruling is not merely human consensus but Spirit-guided discernment. The Spirit who had been directing the mission (8:29; 10:44; 13:2; 15:8) is now co-author of the ruling. The phrase has become the template for Christian conciliar theology: church decisions can reflect Spirit-guidance through genuinely human deliberative processes.
15.29: apechesthai eidōlothutōn kai haimatos kai pniktōn kai porneias (abstain from idol-meat, blood, strangled animals, and sexual immorality): the four Apostolic Decree terms. The first three relate to food-purity (levitical requirements for resident aliens: Lev 17:8-16); porneia (sexual immorality) may include marriages within prohibited degrees or sexual practices associated with Gentile worship. The minimal list facilitates table-fellowship while protecting the community from idolatry-adjacent practices.
15.30: apelythēsan oun kateelthon eis Antiocheian kai synagagontes to plēthos epedōkan tēn epistolēn ('they were sent off and went to Antioch and gathering the congregation they delivered the letter'): the delegation's mission is the delivery and reception of the ruling within the whole community (plēthos, the entire congregation); the council decision becomes the church's shared possession through this communal reading. The formal assembly mirrors the synagogue's Torah-reading practice.
15.31: anagnontes de echarēsan epi tē paraklēsei ('when they read it they were glad for the encouragement'): chara (joy) again marks the arrival of the right resolution; the Antioch church's response to the letter is the joy of a burden removed. paraklēsei (encouragement, comfort): the letter is received not as a legal ruling but as paraklesis — the same Spirit-comfort that characterizes prophetic ministry throughout Acts.
15.32: Ioudas te kai Silas kai autoi prophētai ontes dia logou pollou parekalesan tous adelphous kai epestērixen ('Judas and Silas, being themselves prophets, with much speaking encouraged the brothers and strengthened them'): the prophetic gift (prophētai) is deployed in pastoral paraklēsis and epistērizō (strengthening, establishing). This is the OT prophetic role carried into the new covenant community — the prophet speaks for God's people's strengthening, not only for prediction.
15.33: apolytheisan met' eirēnēs apo tōn adelphōn pros tous aposteilantas autous ('they were sent off with peace from the brothers to those who had sent them'): the met' eirēnēs (with peace) farewell is the standard blessing-release for travelers and delegates; it carries the shalom-content of wholeness and right relationship. The mission's business is completed; the delegates return to their sending community while the Antioch church retains its own peace.
15.34: Verse 34 ('But Silas chose to remain there') is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts (p74, Sinaiticus) and is likely a scribal insertion to explain why Silas is available in Antioch for Paul's invitation in v.40. Without v.34, Luke's narrative has Silas departing with Judas (v.33) and being chosen by Paul from Antioch in v.40 — compatible if Silas returned to Antioch before Paul's invitation. The variant is text-critical, not theological.
15.35: Paulos de kai Barnabas dietribon en Antiocheia didaskontes kai euangelizomenoi meta kai heterōn pollōn ('Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch teaching and preaching the word of the Lord along with many others'): the imperfect dietribon (were staying, remaining) marks the extended period of settled ministry. The plural heterōn pollōn (many others) shows the Antioch church had a substantial teaching community, not dependent on apostolic figures alone for its ongoing formation.
15.36: episkefsōmetha tous adelphous kata polin pasan en hais katēngelamen ton logon tou kyriou pōs echousin ('let us visit the brothers in every city where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are'): Paul's pastoral instinct — the mission's goal is not proclamation only but the formation and ongoing health of communities. Pōs echousin (how they are faring) is the welfare-check language of family responsibility; Paul treats the planted churches as kin requiring continued attention.
15.37: Barnabas de ebouleto symparalabein kai ton Iōannēn ton kaloumenon Markon ('Barnabas wanted to take John called Mark with them'): the same Mark whose departure at Pamphylia (13:13) Paul regarded as desertion. Barnabas's advocacy for Mark is consistent with his character as huios paraklēseōs (son of encouragement, 4:36) — he had vouched for Paul (9:27) and now vouches for Mark. The conflict is not merely personal preference but a real missional disagreement about reliability under pressure.
15.38: Paulos de ēxiou ton apostanta ap' autōn apo Pamphylias kai mē synelthonta autois eis to ergon ('Paul thought it wrong to take with them one who had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia and had not continued with them in the work'): apostanta (having withdrawn, deserted) is Paul's characterization — aphistēmi is the apostasy verb. The issue is not the quality of Mark's future work but the precedent set by rewarding departure from a mission. Paul's standard is consistent: the ergon (the work) requires reliable commitment.
15.39: egeneto de paroxysmos hōste apochōristhēnai autous ap' allēlōn ('there was such a sharp disagreement that they parted company'): paroxysmos (sharp provocation, paroxysm) is a strong word — the medical term for a fever-crisis; in LXX it describes God's anger (Deut 29:28; Ps 106:32). Luke records the rupture without editorial verdict; the narrative outcome (two mission teams, doubled coverage) suggests God's providential use of the conflict, though this is implicit rather than stated.
15.40: Paulos de epilexamenos Silan exēlthen paradotheis tē chariti tou kyriou hypo tōn adelphōn ('Paul chose Silas and departed, being commended to the grace of the Lord by the brothers'): the parallel with the Antioch sending of 13:3 — community prayer and commendation — confirms Silas as the legitimate replacement partner. Tē chariti tou kyriou (to the grace of the Lord) frames the commission: the mission proceeds under divine grace-protection, not apostolic self-authorization.
15.41: diērcheto de tēn Syrian kai Kilikian epistērizōn tas ekklēsias ('he was going through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches'): the route through Syria and Cilicia revisits the communities formed in Paul's early post-conversion ministry (Gal 1:21); the epistērizōn (strengthening, establishing) verb matches 14:22 and 15:32 — consolidation is the constant companion of advance. The second missionary journey begins as continuation and consolidation before it becomes new expansion.

17.28: en autō gar zōmen kai kinoumentha kai esmen (en autō gar zōmen kai kinoumentha kai esmen): Paul quotes the Cretan poet Epimenides (in him we live and move and have our being). tou gar kai genos esmen: from Aratus's Phaenomena (for we are indeed his offspring). Paul's Areopagus speech uses pagan poets as partial witnesses to what the gospel discloses fully: universal human dependence on and kinship with the God who made from one (ex henos) all nations. The strategy is not syncretism but apologetic appropriation — common ground that the specific proclamation of resurrection then exceeds.
17.1: hōdoiporēsantes de tēn Amphipolin kai Apollōnian ('having passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia'): Luke's travel notation is characteristically specific — the Via Egnatia, the major Roman road through Macedonia, ran through these cities. The distance from Philippi to Thessalonica was about 100 miles. Luke does not report any mission activity in Amphipolis or Apollonia, implying either no significant synagogue or Gentile community, or a strategic focus on Thessalonica as the regional capital.
17.2: kata de to eiōthos tō Paulō eisēlthen pros autous ('as was Paul's custom, he went to them'): eiōthos (habitual custom) establishes synagogue-entry as Paul's standard operational method, not a one-time accommodation. Paul's synagogue-first pattern serves both theological principle (to the Jew first, Rom 1:16) and practical mission logic: Scriptural literacy, monotheism, and eschatological expectation created a receptive-or-rejecting audience in a defined venue. Epi sabbata tria ('for three Sabbaths') suggests a brief synagogue engagement before the broader Thessalonian mission.
17.3: dianoigōn kai paratithemenos hoti ton Christon edei pathein kai anastēnai ek nekrōn ('opening and demonstrating that it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer and rise from the dead'): the two-stage exegetical method — dianoigōn (opening the Scriptures, the same word as Luke 24:45) and paratithemenos (setting alongside, demonstrating by comparison) — is the apostolic interpretive technique. Edei (it was necessary) is Lukan divine necessity; the suffering and resurrection of the Messiah is not contingent but scripturally required. Paul then identifies Jesus as the one who fulfills these requirements.
17.4: tōn te sebomenōn Hellēnōn plēthos poly kai gynaikōn tōn prōtōn ouk oligai ('a great multitude of God-fearing Greeks and not a few prominent women'): Luke notes the social profile of the Thessalonian converts — a large Gentile contingent (the God-fearers) and socially prominent women. The prominent women's conversion replicates the pattern at Pisidian Antioch (13:50) and later at Berea (17:12), reflecting both the synagogue's attraction to elite Gentile women and Luke's consistent attention to women's role in the early mission.
17.5: zēlōsantes de hoi Ioudaioi ('the Jews being jealous'): the opposition pattern — Jewish leaders stirred by jealousy against Paul's mission success — recurs from 13:45 and will continue in Berea (v.13) and Corinth (18:12-17). anastatōsantes tēn polin ('having thrown the city into turmoil'): anastatōsantes will be used of Paul himself in 21:38 as a charge; here it is the accusers who create the disturbance. The charge is projection.
17.6: hoi tēn oikoumenēn anastatōsantes ('those who have turned the world upside down'): the charge is simultaneously the accusers' indictment and Luke's clearest description of the gospel's social impact. Anastatoō (overturn, unsettle, throw into confusion) was used of military and social upheaval; the crowd's accusation is that Paul's movement is destabilizing the imperial order. For Luke's reader, the charge is true in the deepest sense — the gospel does overturn the world's structures — but false as a political crime: the kingdom of God advances by proclamation, not revolt.
17.7: houtos pantes apenanti tōn dogmatōn Kaisaros prattousin basilea heteron legontes einai Iēsoun ('these all act against Caesar's decrees, saying there is another king, Jesus'): the political charge repurposes an actual apostolic claim. The missionaries did proclaim Jesus as kyrios (Lord) and king; in the Roman world, this is a political challenge to Caesar's title. Luke records the charge not to deny it but to show that it is misunderstood: the kingdom Jesus inaugurates does not compete with Rome on Rome's terms but transcends it entirely.
17.8: etaraxan de ton ochlon kai tous politarchas akouontas tauta ('they threw the crowd and the city officials into turmoil when they heard these things'): politarchas ('city officials') is a Macedonian civic title attested in inscriptions from Thessalonica — one of many Lukan place-name and title details confirmed by archaeology, supporting his historical reliability. The turmoil (etaraxan) is the crowd's and officials' reaction to the politically charged accusation, not to actual violence by the missionaries.
17.9: labontes to hikanon para tou Iasonos kai tōn loipōn apelysan autous ('having taken security from Jason and the others, they released them'): to hikanon ('sufficient/bond') is the legal term for bail or security deposit — a financial guarantee of good behavior. Jason's house had hosted the missionaries; he takes legal responsibility for the community's conduct. The mechanism is Roman legal process being used to manage a religious dispute — the kind of cross-category entanglement that characterizes Acts throughout.
17.10: hoi de adelphoi eutheos dia nyktos exepemyan ton te Paulon kai ton Silan eis Berroian ('the brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away to Berea by night'): the nighttime departure is a security measure against continued mob action. Berea (modern Veria) is about 50 miles southwest of Thessalonica — on a different road from the Via Egnatia and less exposed to Thessalonian influence. The brothers' initiative in arranging Paul's departure shows the community protecting the apostle while continuing their own witness in the city.
17.11: eugenesteroi tōn en Thessalonikē ('more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica'): eugenēs (noble/well-born) here describes intellectual openness and diligence rather than social class. anakrinō ('examining/investigating'): the judicial-interrogation word — used in Acts for official investigations (4:9; 12:19; 24:8; 25:26; 28:18) — is applied to Scripture study. The Bereans subjected Paul's claims to the same rigorous testing as a court would examine testimony. Luke presents this not as distrust but as exemplary biblical practice: testing apostolic claims against the text.
17.12: kai tōn Hellēnidōn gynaikōn tōn euschēmonōn kai andrōn ouk oligoi ('and not a few prominent Greek women and men'): the social profile again notes prominent women first. The Berean community seems weighted toward educated Hellenistic Gentiles who found the Scripture-based reasoning persuasive. This profile anticipates the Areopagus audience (vv.18-21) where educated philosophical discourse will meet the gospel.
17.13: saleuontes kai tarassontes tous ochlous ('shaking and stirring up the crowds'): the Thessalonian Jews follow Paul to Berea specifically to disrupt his mission there. Saleuō (shake) and tarassō (stir up) both describe deliberate agitation — not natural response but organized opposition. The pattern of Jewish opponents from one city following Paul to another will continue (cf. 20:3; 21:27). Luke records it without editorial comment; the opposition intensifies as the mission advances.
17.14: eutheos de tote ton Paulon exapesteilan hoi adelphoi poreuesthai hōs epi tēn thalassan ('the brothers immediately sent Paul away to go toward the sea'): Paul is separated from Silas and Timothy for strategic reasons — the opposition is directed personally at Paul. The community again protects the apostle by arranging his departure. Hōs epi tēn thalassan ('as far as the sea') may be misdirection — the phrase could mean toward the coast rather than definitively by sea — keeping Paul's exact route uncertain to his pursuers.
17.15: kathiston auton heōs Athēnōn ('they brought him as far as Athens'): the escorts' mission is complete when Paul reaches Athens. His instructions to send Silas and Timothy quickly (hōs tachista) reflects his awareness that he needs his co-workers to continue the Thessalonian mission. Paul's presence in Athens — the intellectual capital of the ancient world — is the result not of strategic planning but of persecution-driven movement. The Areopagus speech will not have been on Paul's itinerary.
17.16: parōxyneto to pneuma autou en autō ('his spirit was provoked within him'): paroxynō is the LXX term for God's anger at idolatry (Deut 9:18; Ps 78:58; Isa 65:3). Paul's response to Athens is not tourist admiration but the same moral-theological reaction that the prophets had to Israel's idolatry. Kateidōlon ousan tēn polin ('the city being full of idols'): Athens's religious density was proverbial in antiquity; Petronius commented that it was easier to find a god than a man there. Paul's grief mirrors God's own grief at the sight.
17.17: men oun en tē synagōgē dielegeto tois Ioudaiois kai tois sebomenois ('so in the synagogue he reasoned with the Jews and God-fearers'): Paul maintains his synagogue-first pattern even in Athens, where the main encounter will be with philosophers. Kai en tē agora kata pasan hēmeran ('and in the market-place every day'): the agora (marketplace) was the center of Athenian intellectual and civic life — where Socrates had conversed. Paul's daily engagement in the agora replicates the Socratic method of street-level philosophical dialogue.
17.18: spermologos ('seed-picker/babbler'): a term of contempt used for birds that pick up scattered seeds, then applied to people who pick up fragments of others' ideas without understanding them. The Epicureans (followers of Epicurus — pleasure-through-absence-of-pain, no divine providence, atomic materialism) and Stoics (rational divine principle permeating all things, virtue as the highest good) represent the two dominant Hellenistic philosophical schools. Ti an theloi ho spermologos houtos legein ('what would this seed-picker want to say?') is the philosophically condescending response to Paul's street preaching.
17.19: Areiou Pagou ('the Areopagus'): the ancient court on the hill northwest of the Acropolis, traditionally associated with judgment (mythology: Ares was tried there for murder). By Paul's day it functioned as an advisory council overseeing education, religious practices, and public morality in Athens. The invitation to speak there is a formal hearing, not merely a philosophical seminar — Paul is being evaluated as a new religious teacher. Xenē daimonia ('foreign divinities'): technically introducing new gods was a prosecutable offense in Athens (Socrates was convicted of it); the Areopagus appearance is a quasi-legal proceeding.
17.20: xenizonta gar tina eispherei eis tas akoais hēmōn ('for you are bringing some strange things to our hearing'): xenizō (to seem foreign/strange) is the Athenians' reaction to anastasis (resurrection). To Greek ears, resurrection was strange — not merely unfamiliar but conceptually incoherent within materialist or dualist frameworks. The Stoics believed in a cosmic recycling of souls; the Epicureans denied any afterlife; neither had conceptual categories for resurrection of bodies. Paul's proclamation genuinely 'sounds foreign' in the philosophers' ears.
17.21: Athēnaioi de pantes kai hoi epidēmountes xenoi eis ouden heteron ēukairoun e legein ti e akouein ti kainoteron ('all Athenians and the foreigners who lived there had time for nothing other than telling or hearing the very latest thing'): Luke's aside is his most explicitly satirical observation in Acts. The characterization of Athenians as addicted to intellectual novelty was a classical cliché (Demosthenes, Thucydides) — Luke's irony is gentle but pointed: the people who prided themselves on intellectual sophistication were consumed by the appetite for the new, making them simultaneously receptive to Paul's message and likely to dismiss it for the next novelty.
17.22: deisidaimonesterous hymas theōrō ('I observe that you are very religious/superstitious'): deisidaimōn is deliberately ambiguous — it can mean 'very religious' (compliment) or 'very superstitious' (criticism). Paul's opening honors Athenian piety while leaving room for the critique to follow. The rhetorical strategy is classical captatio benevolentiae — winning the audience's goodwill before making challenging claims. Paul adapts his rhetorical approach to his audience without compromising his message.
17.23: AGNŌSTŌ THEŌ ('to an unknown god'): archaeological evidence confirms the existence of altars to unknown gods in Athens and elsewhere; Pausanias (2nd century AD) mentions them. Paul's use of this altar as a point of contact is a masterpiece of apologetic method: he does not begin with Jewish Scripture (inappropriate for a non-Jewish audience) but with a void in their existing religious framework. The unknown God they have acknowledged by ignorance is the God he is about to make known.
17.24: ho theos ho poiēsas ton kosmon kai panta ta en autō ('the God who made the world and everything in it'): Paul begins with Creator-theology — the ground shared with OT tradition and partially with Stoicism. Kyrios ouranou kai gēs hyparchōn ('being Lord of heaven and earth'): the title echoes Acts 4:24 (the community's prayer) and OT creation-theology (Gen 14:19; Ps 146:6). Ouk en cheiropoiētois naois katoikei ('does not live in temples made by human hands'): citing Isa 66:1-2 and 1 Kgs 8:27 without citation — the transcendence of God over cultic structures, recognizable to any Stoic as well.
17.25: oude hypo cheirōn anthrōpinōn therapeudo prosdeomenos tinos ('nor is he served by human hands as if he needed anything'): the self-sufficiency of God (aseitas) is simultaneously OT theology (Ps 50:12-14) and Stoic theism. The Epicurean gods were famously self-sufficient and uninvolved; Paul's God is self-sufficient but supremely involved, being the source of all life. Autos didous pāsin zōēn kai pnoēn kai ta panta ('he himself gives to all life and breath and all things'): the life-giving Creator is the antithesis of the idol who receives.
17.26: ex henos epoiēsen pan ethnos anthrōpōn ('from one he made every nation of mankind'): ex henos (from one) — the one ancestor, Adam — establishes the unity of the human race against Athenian claims of autochthony (being born from their own soil, making them superior to other peoples). Paul's universalism has OT ground (Gen 1-3; Deut 32:8) and challenges Athenian ethnic pride. Horothesias tēs katoikias autōn ('the boundaries of their dwelling places'): divine sovereignty over national territories and times — the same sovereignty Paul named in 13:17.
17.27: zētein ton theon, ei ara ge psēlaphēseian auton kai heuroien ('to seek God, if perhaps they might feel/grope after him and find him'): psēlaphōō ('to feel/grope') is used for blind groping in the dark (LXX Deut 28:29; Job 5:14) and for Thomas's tactile examination of the risen Christ (John 20:27). Humanity's natural religious search is described as groping in partial darkness — not futile, since God has structured history toward finding, but not self-sufficient, since the proclamation of vv.30-31 is required for the search to conclude. Kai ge ou makran apo henos hekastou hēmōn hyparchonta ('though he is not far from each one of us') — the proximity language resonates with Stoic immanence.
17.29: ouk opheilomen nomizein chrysō e argyrō e lithō, charagmati technēs kai enthymēseōs anthrōpou, to theion einai homoion ('we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by human art and imagination'): Paul here deploys the OT idol polemic (Isa 44:9-20; Ps 115:4-8) in philosophical language accessible to Stoics (who also rejected idolatry conceptually). Charagmati technēs kai enthymēseōs ('image formed by craft and human imagination'): the Stoic position that the divine cannot be captured in material form aligns with Paul's argument, though for different reasons.
17.30: tous men oun chronous tēs agnoias hyperidōn ho theos ('the times of ignorance God overlooked'): hypereidon (overlooked/passed over) is a deliberate echo of Rom 3:25-26, where God 'passed over' sins in the time before Christ's atonement. The 'times of ignorance' (chronous tēs agnoias) correspond to what Paul calls 'the present time' preceding the full revelation of righteousness. Nyn de paraggellei tois anthrōpois pantas pantachou metanoein ('but now he commands all people everywhere to repent'): the eschatological 'now' — the new era of accountability that Christ's resurrection inaugurates.
17.31: en andri hō hōrisen ('by a man whom he appointed'): the universal judgment is mediated through a specific historical person — the particular-universal structure of the gospel's scandal. Pistin parescen pasin anastesas auton ek nekrōn ('he has given proof to all by raising him from the dead'): the resurrection is the public verification — pistis here means evidence/proof (the forensic sense, not the trust-sense), the publicly demonstrable credential that the appointed judge has been vindicated. The speech ends where all apostolic speeches end: the resurrection as the undeniable divine act requiring response.
17.32: anastasis nekrōn ('resurrection of the dead'): the philosophical stumbling block for Greeks. Stoics believed in a periodic cosmic recycling of souls; Epicureans denied any afterlife; both rejected the bodily resurrection as conceptually incoherent within their frameworks. Hoi men echleuazon ('some mocked'): chleuazō is open mockery — not polite skepticism but contemptuous dismissal. The divided response — mockery, deferral, belief — mirrors the range of responses throughout Acts. The gospel divides because its central claim (resurrection) is simultaneously the most incredible and the most decisive truth it asserts.
17.33: Paul's departure ek mesou autōn ('from their midst') is neither triumphant nor despairing; Luke narrates it as a simple withdrawal from a mixed-response audience. The Areopagus speech achieves no visible mass conversion — in contrast to Pentecost (3,000) or Pisidian Antioch. Luke records it without apology or explanation; the philosophical audience's response to the resurrection is what it is. Paul's self-reflection on his Corinthian approach (1 Cor 2:1-5) may reflect on the Athens experience.
17.34: Dionysios ho Areopagitēs kai gynē onomati Damaris kai heteroi syn autois ('Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris and others with them'): the named converts from the Areopagus are few but not negligible. Dionysius (Areopagitēs) was a member of the Areopagus council — a civic elite convert. Damaris is otherwise unknown; her presence in the named converts alongside a council member suggests she was also a person of some social standing. Luke's naming of converts throughout Acts is consistent with his access to community tradition that remembered the first members.

20.28: ten ekklesian tou theou hen peripoiesato dia tou haimatos tou idiou (tēn ekklēsian tou theou hēn peripoiesato dia tou haimatos tou idiou): arguably the most theologically dense sentence in Acts. Ekklesia tou theou is Paul's normal Corinthian usage. Peripoieomai (acquire for oneself) echoes LXX Isa 43:21 and Exod 19:5 where YHWH acquires Israel as his special possession. Critically: dia tou haimatos tou idiou — 'through his own blood'. Idios (own/personal) can modify the understood subject (God, whose Son's blood is 'his own') or be read as 'the blood of the one who is his own [Son]'. Either reading makes this the most explicit Acts Christology: God-who-owns-the-blood, or blood that belongs to God himself. The elders of Ephesus are charged to shepherd what God purchased at this cost.
20.1: metapempsAmenos tous mathetas (metapempsamenos tous mathētas): after the Ephesian riot subsides, Paul gathers the disciples and departs for Macedonia. Metapempsamenos (having summoned) shows pastoral deliberateness — Paul does not flee but formally commissions and greets the community before moving on. The Macedonian visit and return south through Greece occupies vv. 1-3 in summary travel notation.
20.2: parakalesAs autous logo pollo (parakalesas autous logō pollō): 'having encouraged them with much speech'. Parakalō is the pastoral strengthening-word that runs through Acts (14:22; 15:32; 18:27); logō pollō implies extended personal teaching rather than a brief farewell. Paul's ministry pattern across Macedonia was sustained instruction before departure — building communities able to stand after he leaves.
20.3: genomenou epiboules auto hypo ton Ioudaion (genomenēs epiboulēs autō hypo tōn Ioudaiōn): a plot against Paul by Jews in Corinth forces a route change northward instead of sailing directly to Syria. This is the fourth such plot in Acts (9:24; 20:3; 23:12-16; 25:3); the pattern of Jewish hostility to Paul's mission traces a consistent arc. The overruling of the plot by divine providence is implicit in genomenēs — it arose and was discovered before it succeeded.
20.4: Sopatros Pyrrhou Berraios: the delegation list of Paul's travel companions reads as a roll-call of the churches represented in the collection for Jerusalem (2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28). Sopater from Beroea, Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from Asia — each representative carries his church's gift. The journey to Jerusalem is thus a concrete embodiment of Gentile-church solidarity with Jewish believers.
20.5: houtoi proelthontes emeinan hemas en Troadi: the third-person shifts back to first at v.5 (the 'we' resumes after 16:17), placing the narrator back in the travel party. Troas was the site of Paul's Macedonian vision (16:8-9) — the gateway to Europe. The group reconvenes there, creating narrative symmetry: what began in Troas (European mission) is reviewed and completed there.
20.6: meta tas hemas ton azymOn (meta tas hēmeras tōn azymōn): 'after the Days of Unleavened Bread'. Luke marks the Passover-season calendar in a way that gives the Troas gathering a paschal frame — the breaking of bread in v.7 during this season carries eucharistic weight heightened by Passover's sacrificial context. The deliberate calendrical note is one of several Lukan liturgical markers.
20.7: en te mia ton sabbaton... tou klasai arton (en tē mia tōn sabbatōn... tou klasai arton): 'on the first day of the week... to break bread'. This is the earliest NT evidence for Sunday as the assembly day (1 Cor 16:2; Rev 1:10 confirm the pattern). Klasai arton carries the eucharistic resonance established in Luke 22:19 and 24:35. Paul's extended teaching — lasting until midnight — reflects the synagogue-then-meal pattern adapted to Sunday assemblies.
20.8: lampades hikanai en to hyperoo (lampades hikanai en tō hyperōō): 'many lamps in the upper room'. Luke's realistic detail — the room crowded, hot, poorly ventilated with oil-lamp smoke — sets up the Eutychus episode naturalistically. The hyperōon (upper story room) is the same word for the Jerusalem upper room of Acts 1:13; the gathering in Troas is structurally continuous with the original community.
20.9: Eutycho... katapheromenos hypno bathy (Eutychō... katapheromenos hypnō bathei): 'Eutychus... overcome by deep sleep'. Eutychus means 'fortunate' — ironic given his fall. Katapherō (overwhelm/weigh down) with hypnō bathei (deep sleep) conveys total incapacitation. The detail of his sitting in the window suggests he sought air; the three-story fall (apo tou tristegou) was fatal. Luke is careful: ērthē nekros — 'he was taken up dead'.
20.10: epipesoN auto kai symperilaboN eipen (epipesōn autō kai symperiabōn eipen): Paul's action echoes Elijah falling on the child of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:21) and Elisha stretching over the Shunammite's son (2 Kgs 4:34). Symperilambanō (embrace/enfold) is a hapax — the physical embrace conveys the life-restoration. Mē thorubeisthe, he psyche autou en autō estin: 'do not be troubled, his life is in him' — present tense, the miracle already complete before the crowd is reassured.
20.11: klasas ton arton kai geusamenos (klasas ton arton kai geusamenos): the miracle-interruption resolved, Paul returns to the eucharistic meal. The narrative sequence — eucharist disrupted by death, life restored, eucharist resumed — enacts the theology: the Lord's Supper is the ongoing celebration of life over death. HomilesAs epi poly (having spoken at length) — Paul preaches until dawn, an all-night catechetical vigil.
20.12: egagon de ton paida zonta (ēgagon de ton paida zōnta): 'they brought the young man away alive'. ZōnTa (living) echoes the resurrection vocabulary throughout Luke-Acts (zōn: Acts 1:3, 25:19). The community is 'not-a-little comforted' (pareклēthēsan ou metriōs) — litotes for overwhelming relief and strengthened faith. The Troas community receives the same life-over-death sign Paul proclaimed in word.
20.13: hēmeis de proelthontes epi to ploion anechthēmen epi ten Asson: the travel narrative resumes with 'we', Paul walking overland alone to Assos while the others sail. The deliberate separation — Paul's choice to walk — reflects the pastorally reflective rhythm that characterizes his ministry: theological instruction at night, solitary travel by day. The coastal geography (Assos, Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus) is precisely observed.
20.14: synballoN hēmin eis ten Asson... parelabomen auton: Paul joining the ship at Assos. Symballō (join/meet) is a nautical-travel term. The rapid place-list of vv. 14-15 (Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus) reflects a voyage hugging the Aegean coast. Luke's precision is consistent with the 'we' source's navigational detail — the narrator was present.
20.15: te de hestere erchomenoi anteikrys Chiou: passage past Chios — the island of Homer's reputed birthplace. Luke's geographic notation in the sea-journey sections is the most precise in the NT; the Miletus decision (v.16) to bypass Ephesus for time explains the coastal route taken.
20.16: kekrikei gar ho Paulos parapleusei ten Epheson (kekrikei gar ho Paulos parapleusei tēn Epheson): 'Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus'. Kekrikei (had decided/judged) is a perfect-pluperfect showing a prior firm decision. The reason: hopōs mē genētai autō chronotribēsai en tē Asia — 'so he would not lose time in Asia'. Paul's urgency to reach Jerusalem by Pentecost (the same pilgrimage-feast as Acts 2) drives the route. Yet he still calls the Ephesian elders to Miletus — pastoral obligation and eschatological urgency coexist.
20.17: apo de tes Miletou pempsas eis Epheson metekalesato tous presbyterous tes ekklesias: Paul's summons of the Ephesian elders to Miletus initiates the most sustained pastoral speech in Acts (vv.18-35). Presbyteroi (elders) here are interchangeable with episkopoi (overseers, v.28), establishing NT precedent for the same office designated by both terms. The Miletus address is Paul's only speech to Christians in Acts; all others address Jews or pagans.
20.18: pos meth hymon ton panta chronon egenomen (pōs meth' hymōn ton panta chronon egenomēn): 'how I was with you the whole time'. The Miletus speech opens with an autobiographical defense-and-testament that echoes the farewell discourses of Moses (Deut 29-31), Samuel (1 Sam 12), and Jesus (John 13-17). Paul's three-year Ephesian ministry (v.31) is summoned as evidence of integrity: his conduct was visible to all.
20.19: douleuon to kyrio meta pases tapeinophrosynes (douleuōn tō kyriō meta pasēs tapeinophrosynēs): 'serving the Lord with all humility'. Tapeinophrosynē (humility/lowliness of mind) is virtually a Pauline coinage (Phil 2:3; Eph 4:2; Col 2:18, 23; 3:12) — it occurs rarely before Paul and frequently after him. The tears (dakryōn) and trials from Jewish plots (peirasmōn tōn symbantōn moi en tais epiboulais tōn Ioudaiōn) authenticate the pastoral biography.
20.20: hōs ouden hypesteilamēn ton sympheron (hōs ouden hypesteilamēn tōn sympherontōn): 'I held nothing back of what was profitable'. Hypostellō (shrink back/withhold) is a strong word — to conceal or suppress what ought to be declared. Paul's claim is total disclosure: no withheld teaching, no pastorally convenient omissions. didaskon dēmosia kai kat oikous — public teaching in the synagogue/agora and private house-church instruction — reflect the dual educational venues of Pauline mission.
20.21: tēn eis theon metanoian kai pistin eis ton kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun: 'repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus' — the most compact summary of Pauline-Lukan gospel content in Acts. The double movement — eis theon (toward God) and eis ton kyrion (toward the Lord) — is not redundant: repentance is the reorientation toward God as sovereign; faith is the trust in Jesus as mediator. Both Jews and Greeks receive the same message, establishing its universality.
20.22: deo to pneumati poreuomai (deō tō pneumati poreuomai, 'bound in the spirit I go'): the ambiguity of pneuma here (divine Spirit or Paul's own spirit) is probably deliberate — Paul's own resolve is the vehicle of the Spirit's compulsion. The Jerusalem journey is framed as a Spirit-driven necessity (dei) that mirrors Jesus's own Jerusalem journey in Luke 9:51. Ta en autē synantēsonta moi mē eidōs: 'not knowing what will happen to me there' — Paul's ignorance of specifics, not of outcome (v.23 confirms suffering awaits).
20.23: to pneuma to hagion kata polin diamartyretai (to pneuma to hagion kata polin diamartyretai): 'the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city'. Diamartyromai (solemnly testify/warn) is the Spirit's prophetic voice in every community Paul visits. The consistent content: desma kai thlipseis me menousin — 'bonds and afflictions await me'. The Spirit does not warn Paul away from Jerusalem; he forewarns him of what obedience costs. This distincts Paul's prophetic awareness from the disciples' protective dissuasion (v.4; 21:12).
20.24: all oud logon poioumai ten psychen mou timian emaut (all' oud' logon poioumai tēn psychēn mou timian emautō): 'I consider my life of no value to myself'. Logon poioumai (reckon/make account of) is accounting language — Paul does not count his life an asset worth protecting at the expense of mission. dromos (dromos): the foot-race or chariot-course; Paul's ministry as a race to be finished appears in 2 Tim 4:7 and Phil 3:12-14. ten diakonian: ministry-as-service, the characteristic NT inversion of Greco-Roman status. diamartyrasthai to euangelion tes charitos tou theou: 'to testify fully to the gospel of the grace of God' — charis as the content that defines the message.
20.25: ouketi opsesthe to prosōpon mou (ouketi opsesthe to prosōpon mou): 'you will see my face no longer'. This farewell declaration is the rhetorical pivot of the speech — the eschatological imminence that makes the charge in vv.28-31 urgent. Paul's knowledge that the Ephesian elders will not see him again frames the remaining instruction as a testament. The solemn declaration of completion — diēlthon kēryssōn ten basileian (I went about preaching the kingdom) — marks the end of this chapter of mission.
20.26: katharos eimI apo tou haimatos pantōn (katharos eimi apo tou haimatos pantōn): 'I am clean of the blood of all'. The metaphor echoes Ezek 33:1-9 where the watchman who fails to warn the wicked is guilty of the blood of those who perish. Paul's three-year ministry of total disclosure (v.27: pasan ten boulen tou theou) absolves him of pastoral responsibility for any who turn away. The language is forensic — Paul has been a faithful watchman; his hands are clean.
20.27: pasan ten boulen tou theou (pasan tēn boulēn tou theou): 'the whole counsel/purpose of God'. Boulē (plan/will/counsel) is Luke's characteristic word for God's sovereign purpose unfolding through history (Luke 7:30; Acts 2:23; 4:28; 13:36; 20:27). The claim to have proclaimed 'all' of it is the claim of a complete apostolic commission faithfully discharged — nothing withheld or softened. This verse grounds the integrity of Pauline teaching as full-canonical, not selective.
20.29: eiseleusontai meta ten aphixin mou lykoi bareis eis hymas (eiseleusontai meta tēn aphixin mou lykoi bareis eis hymas): 'after my departure, savage wolves will come in among you'. Lykoi bareis (savage/heavy wolves) — the same pastoral metaphor Jesus used in Matt 7:15 for false prophets. Paul foresees two threats: external wolves (v.29) and internal distorters (v.30: ex hymōn autōn, 'from among yourselves'). The prediction proved accurate: the Ephesian letters (Ephesians, 1-2 Timothy, Revelation 2:1-7) all address precisely these threats.
20.30: ex hymōn autōn anastēsontai andres lalountes diestramena (ex hymōn autōn anastēsontai andres lalountes diestramMena): 'from among yourselves men will arise speaking perverse things'. Diestrammena (twisted/perverted) — truth bent out of shape, not openly denied but distorted. Apospān tous mathētas (to draw away the disciples) — the aim is not just error but faction. Paul's farewell foresees the Ephesian crisis that 1-2 Timothy address: teachers within the community who use their position to recruit followers to themselves.
20.31: dio grēgoreite, mnēmoneuontes hoti trietian nychta kai hēmeran ouk epausamēn: 'therefore be alert, remembering that for three years night and day I did not stop'. Grēgoreō (keep watch) is the eschatological watchfulness-word (Mark 13:35; Rev 3:2-3). Three years of pastoral presence — the longest Paul stayed anywhere except Corinth (18 months) — constitutes the model that the elders are to reproduce. Mnēmoneuontes (remembering) makes Paul's ministry an ongoing standard against which future teaching is measured.
20.32: to rhēma tes charitos autou to dynamenon oikodomesai kai dounai ten klēronomiAn (tō rhēmati tēs charitos autou tō dynamenō oikodomēsai kai dounai tēn klēronomiān): 'the word of his grace, which is able to build up and give the inheritance'. Paul commends the Ephesian elders not to their own competence but to the word as the agent of construction. Oikodomeō (build up/edify) and klēronomia (inheritance) together frame the church as a building-under-construction and a community with an eschatological destination. The phrase 'tois hēgiasmenois pasin' (all who are sanctified) grounds the inheritance in God's prior sanctifying work.
20.33: argyriou e chrysiou e himatismou oudenos epethymēsa: 'I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing'. The three categories — money and clothing — cover the standard forms of pastoral exploitation in antiquity. Paul's disclaimer echoes Samuel's farewell in 1 Sam 12:3 ('I have taken no one's ox or donkey, nor have I cheated or oppressed anyone'). The pastoral-integrity defence was a stock form in philosophical and rhetorical tradition; Paul fills it with genuine autobiographical content.
