20.34: autoi ginōskete hoti tais chreiais mou kai tois ousin met emou hyperetēsan hai cheires autai: 'you yourselves know that these hands provided for my needs and for those who were with me'. Hai cheires autai (these very hands) — the physical demonstration of self-support. Paul's tent-making is mentioned in Acts 18:3; the Corinthian letters show he made this self-support policy a deliberate mark of apostolic integrity distinguishing him from fee-taking sophists and wonder-workers.
20.35: dei antilambAnesthai ton asthenounton, mnēmoneuein te ton logon tou kyriou Iesou hoti autos eipen, Makarion estin mallon didonai e lambanein: 'you must help the weak, remembering the words of the Lord Jesus that he himself said, It is more blessed to give than to receive'. This saying of Jesus (rhēma tou kyriou Iēsou) is not found in any gospel — the only example of a Jesus-saying in Acts that is not in the canonical gospels but is attributed to Jesus by the earliest witness. Makarion estin didonai: the beatitude-form applied to generosity is continuous with Jesus's Sermon-on-the-Mount ethic.
20.36: kai tauta eipOn, theis ta gonata autou syn pasin autois proseuxato: 'having said these things, he knelt down with them all and prayed'. Theis ta gonata (kneeling) is the posture of intense intercession (Acts 7:60; 21:5; Eph 3:14). The prayer concludes the Miletus address without being recorded — Luke preserves the gesture, not the content, as the seal of the pastoral encounter. The corporate kneeling enacts the unity of purpose the address called for.
20.37: hikanos de klauthmos egeneto pantōn: 'there was much weeping from all'. The farewell scene — tears, embraces, kisses, grief at the finality — is the most emotionally detailed passage in Acts. Epipiptō (fall upon) for the embraces echoes the father in the parable (Luke 15:20); katephiloun (kissed repeatedly, imperfect) conveys sustained farewell-affection. The community Paul built loves him as a father.
20.38: odynomenoi malista epi to logō hō eirēken hoti ouketi mellouseN to prosōpon autou theōrein: 'grieving especially over the word he had spoken that they would see his face no longer'. The malista (especially) specifies what makes this parting unbearable: not the journey's danger but the finality of separation. Theōrein (behold) is Luke's word for attentive seeing; the elders will never again behold the pastor who gave them three years and the whole counsel of God. The grief is eschatologically realistic.

26.18: epistrephai apo skotous eis phos kai tes exousias tou Satana epi ton theon (epistrepsai apo skotous eis phōs kai tēs exousias tou Satana epi ton theon): Paul's mission-summary before Agrippa. Epistrephō (turn) is the LXX-standard verb for Israel's repentance/return (shub). The dual transfer — darkness to light, Satan's authority to God — frames conversion as a cosmic jurisdiction-change, not merely moral improvement. Aphesis hamartion (forgiveness of sins) and klēron en tois hēgiasmenois (inheritance among the sanctified) complete the fourfold result: light, God's side, forgiveness, inheritance. The inheritance-language echoes Deut 33:3-4 and the Wisdom literature of Second Temple Judaism.
26.1: Agrippos de pros ton Paulon ephē, Epitrepetai soi hyper seautou legein: 'Agrippa said to Paul: you are permitted to speak for yourself'. Epitrepetai (it is permitted) — Agrippa's formal grant of floor-time has the character of a royal indulgence rather than judicial procedure. Paul's response — ekteinAs tēn cheira (extending the hand) — is the orator's gesture (cf. 13:16; 21:40); Luke frames the speech as a formal rhetorical address, not an informal defense.
26.2: makarios egō heauton hēgēmai epi sou mellōn apologeisthai: 'I consider myself fortunate that I am going to make my defense before you'. Makarios (fortunate/blessed) — the opening compliment to Agrippa is standard rhetorical captatio benevolentiae (capturing goodwill), but Paul grounds it in Agrippa's specific competence: he is gnōstēn onta se pantōn tōn kata Ioudaious ethōn te kai zētēmatōn (an expert in all Jewish customs and disputes). Paul's case is genuinely intelligible only to someone with that background.
26.3: dio deomai sou makrothymōs akousai mou: 'therefore I beg you to hear me patiently'. Makrothymōs (patiently/with long-suffering) — Paul's speech will be extended and complex; he is asking for sustained attention. The word makrothymia (patience) in Paul's letters describes the divine forbearance that undergirds salvation history; here it describes the quality of attention the speech's subject matter requires.
26.4: ten men oun biōsin mou tēn ek neotētos tēn ap archēs genomenēn en tō ethnei mou en te Hierosolymois: Paul's autobiographical opening — his life from youth in the nation and in Jerusalem. Biōsis (way of life) is a rare term for the conduct and manner of life; Paul's biography is itself evidence for his position. The Jerusalem location is significant: Paul was educated at the center of Jewish learning, not on the diaspora periphery. What he says about Jewish hope comes from insider knowledge.
26.5: proginōskontes me anōthen... hoti kata tēn akribestatēn hairesin tēs hēmeteras thrēskeias ezēsa Pharisaios: 'they have known me from the beginning... that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived as a Pharisee'. Akribestatēn (strictest/most precise) — the superlative of akribes (exact/precise); the Pharisees were known for scrupulous Torah-observance and oral tradition development. Paul's Pharisaic credentials (Phil 3:5-6) make his resurrection-belief continuous with the best of Jewish tradition, not a departure from it.
26.6: kai nyn ep elpidi tēs eis tous pateras hēmōn epangelias genomenēs hypo tou theou hestēka krinomenos: 'and now I stand on trial because of the hope in the promise made by God to our fathers'. Elpidi (hope) as the legal ground of Paul's trial — he is not being tried for a crime but for a hope. The epangelia (promise) to the patriarchs is the Abrahamic promise-structure of the OT; Paul's entire mission is the announcement of its fulfillment in Christ. This is the theological center of the Agrippa speech.
26.7: eis hēn to dōdekaphulon hēmōn en ekteneia nychta kai hēmeran latreuon elpizei katantēsai: 'to which our twelve-tribe nation, earnestly worshipping night and day, hopes to attain'. Dōdekaphulon (twelve-tribe) — an archaic collective for all Israel, including the northern tribes scattered in the Assyrian exile. Paul's point: the resurrection-hope he proclaims is the same hope that animates Israel's whole liturgical life. The nation that prays night and day is praying toward the fulfillment Paul now announces. The charge against Paul is a charge against Israel's own prayer.
26.8: ti apiston krinete ei ho theos nekrous egeirei (ti apiston krinete ei ho theos nekrous egeirei): 'why is it judged incredible by you that God raises the dead?' The rhetorical question is the hinge of the argument. Apiston (unbelievable/incredible) — Paul attacks the presupposition behind the rejection. For Jews who believe in the God who created ex nihilo and parted the Red Sea, resurrection is not the most extraordinary thing imaginable. The incredibility is not epistemic but volitional — they do not want to believe what the evidence supports.
26.9: egō men oun edoxa emautō pros to onoma Iēsou tou Nazōraiou dein polla enantia praxai: 'I indeed supposed in myself that I had to do many things against the name of Jesus the Nazarene'. Edoxa emautō (I supposed in myself) — self-directed opinion that proved catastrophically wrong. Paul's persecution is now framed as a dei-driven misunderstanding: he thought it was necessary (dein) to oppose Christ, using the same divine-necessity vocabulary he now uses for his mission. The reversal is total.
26.10: ho kai epoiēsa en Hierosolymois kai pollous ton hagion egō en phylakais katekleisa: 'which I also did in Jerusalem — I locked up many of the saints in prison'. Ton hagiōn (the saints/holy ones) — Paul's term for believers is telling: the people he imprisoned were hagioi, consecrated to God. Ten para ton archiereōn exousian labōn (having received authority from the chief priests): the institutional backing for his persecution was official religious authority — he was the establishment's instrument.
26.11: pollakins timōrōn autous ēnankazOn blasphēmein: 'punishing them often in all the synagogues, I was trying to force them to blaspheme'. Ēnankazon (I was trying to compel/force) — imperfect tense, repeated attempted coercion. Blasphēmein — the forced blasphemy would be renouncing Jesus as Lord; Paul was trying to break faith under duress. Perissōs te emmainomenos autois (being enraged/madly furious against them): emmainomai (to be mad/enraged) is a strong term for irrational, obsessive fury — Luke portrays the pre-Damascus Paul as religious mania.
26.12: en hois poreuo menos eis tēn Damaskon met exousias kai epitropēs tēs ton archiereōn: the Damascus journey as the culmination of official persecution. Met exousias kai epitropēs (with authority and commission) — double credentialing for maximum legal force. The Damascus mission was not freelance aggression but institutional policy, which makes the reversal on the road all the more total: the official agent of suppression became the most effective apostle.
26.13: hēmeras mesēs kata tēn hodon eidon... phōs hyper tēn lampotēta tou hēliou: 'at midday on the road I saw... a light brighter than the sun'. Hēmeras mesēs (midday) — the brightest time of day, emphasizing the supernatural intensity. Hyper tēn lampotēta tou hēliou (beyond the brightness of the sun) — this detail only in the third telling. The three Damascus accounts (Acts 9, 22, 26) progressively intensify the theophanic elements; the third, before the royal audience, is the most explicitly visionary.
26.14: sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein (sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein): 'it is hard for you to kick against the goads'. A Greek proverbial expression (found in Pindar, Aeschylus, Euripides) for futile resistance to a superior power. Its use here (only in this third telling) suggests the risen Christ is using culturally intelligible language with Paul the Hellenistic Jew. Kentra (goads) are the sharp sticks used to drive oxen; the animal that kicks back only injures itself more. Paul's persecution was already futile resistance to a divine compulsion he couldn't yet recognize.
26.15: egō de eipon, Tis ei kyrie? Ho de kyrios eipen, Egō eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis: 'I said: who are you, lord? And the Lord said: I am Jesus whom you are persecuting'. The three Damascus accounts share this core exchange verbatim. Egō eimi (I am) echoes the divine self-disclosure formula; in John, Jesus uses it absolutely of his divine identity. The identification — Jesus with the persecuted church — is the theological payoff: Paul was persecuting God's own Son without knowing it.
26.16: all anastēthi kai stēthi epi tous podas sou... procheirisasthai se hyperetēn kai martyra: 'but rise and stand on your feet... I have appeared to you for this: to appoint you a servant and witness'. Procheirizō (appoint/choose beforehand) — the divine appointment precedes the mission; Paul is not a self-appointed apostle but a commissioned one. Hyperetēn kai martyra (servant and witness) — the two-term commission defines Paul's identity. Hyperetēs (under-rower, subordinate assistant) is deliberately humble; Paul is placed in service below the one who commissions him.
26.17: exairoumenos se ek tou laou kai ek tōn ethnōn eis ous egō apostello se: 'delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles to whom I am sending you'. Exaireō (deliver/rescue) — the rescue language frames Paul's entire ministry as sustained divine protection from the threats the trial-narrative has just documented. Eis ous egō apostello se (to whom I am sending you) — the apostolic apostellō (send) is from the risen Christ himself; Paul's authority derives from this direct commission.
26.19: hothen, basileu Agrippe, ouk egenomēn apeithēs tē ouranīō optasia: 'therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision'. Apeithēs (disobedient) — Paul's summary of his post-Damascus response is framed as obedience. Ouranīō optasia (heavenly vision) — the Damascus road as visionary revelation, not just physical event. Paul's entire missionary life is a continuous act of obedience to a single command received on one day: the logic of the speech leads from vision to obedience to trial.
26.20: alla tois en Damaskon prōton te kai Hierosolymois kai pasan tēn chōran tēs Ioudaias kai tois ethnesin apēggellon metanoein kai epistrephein epi ton theon: the geographic summary of Paul's mission matches the Acts narrative — Damascus (9:20-22), Jerusalem (9:28-29), Judea, and the Gentiles (the three journeys). Metanoein kai epistrephein epi ton theon (repent and turn to God) — the same two-movement gospel Paul proclaimed at Lystra (14:15) and Thessalonica. The content is consistent across all audiences.
26.21: heneka toutōn me Ioudaioi syllabenomenos en tō hierō epeirōnto diacheirisasthai: 'on account of these things the Jews seized me in the temple and were trying to kill me'. Diacheirizō (take in hand, kill) — the attempt to kill Paul in the temple (21:30-32) is what his obedience to the Damascus commission provoked. The sentence completes the logic: vision → obedience → mission → arrest. Paul is on trial for doing exactly what the risen Christ commanded him.
26.22: epikourias oun tychōn tēs apo tou theou achri tēs hēmeras tautēs hestēka martyromenos: 'having obtained help from God, I have stood to this day witnessing'. Epikourias tychōn (having obtained help) — epikouría is the word for divine or royal aid in a crisis. Paul's two years in prison have been sustained by the same God who commissioned him; his continued standing (hestēka) is itself evidence of the commission's validity. Martyromenos (witnessing) — Paul is witnessing even now, before this court, fulfilling the Damascus commission in the act of his defense.
26.23: ei pathētos ho Christos, ei prōtos ex anastaseōs nekrōn phōs mellei kataggellein tō te laō kai tois ethnesin: 'that the Christ would suffer, that first from the resurrection of the dead he would proclaim light to the people and to the Gentiles'. The two-part kerygmatic thesis: (1) the Messiah was to suffer (pathētos — morally possible/destined to suffer); (2) as the first-fruits of resurrection, he would bring light to both Jews and Gentiles. Prōtos ex anastaseōs (first from resurrection) — Christ as the pioneer-resurrection who opens the category for all who follow (1 Cor 15:20-23).
26.24: ho Phēstos megalē tē phonē ephē, Mainē Paule: ta polla se grammata eis manian peritrepei: 'Festus said with a loud voice: you are out of your mind, Paul — your great learning is turning you to madness'. Mainē (you are mad) — Festus's interruption at the resurrection claim is the educated Roman administrator's response to what sounds like metaphysical fantasy. Grammata (letters/learning) — Festus attributes Paul's views to scholarly overreach. The charge is the mirror-image of the Corinthians' objection (1 Cor 1:20-23): the wisdom of this age finds resurrection 'madness'.
26.25: ou mainomai, phēsin, kratiste Phēste, alla alētheias kai sōphrosynēs rhēmata apophthengomai: 'I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I utter words of truth and soundness of mind'. Sōphrosynēs (soundness of mind/sobriety) — the opposite of mania; Paul claims his is the reasoning that corresponds to reality. Apophthengomai (utter/declare) is the inspired-speech term Luke uses for the Pentecost proclamation (2:4, 14). Paul's response to the madness charge is: what I say is both true and rational — the failure is Festus's inability to process the evidence for resurrection.
26.26: epistamai gar autos ho basileus peri toutōn pros hon kai parrēsiasamenos lalō: 'for the king knows about these things, to whom I also speak freely'. Parrēsiasamenos (speaking freely/boldly) — parrēsia is the boldness of open, fearless speech; Paul speaks to Agrippa without diplomatic evasion because Agrippa has the Jewish background to evaluate the claims. Lanthanein auton ti toutōn ou peithomai (I am not persuaded that any of these things is hidden from him): Paul appeals to Agrippa's tacit knowledge — the events of Jesus's ministry were not done in a corner (en gōnia, v.26).
26.27: pisteueis basileu Agrippe tois prophētais? oida hoti pisteueis: 'do you believe the prophets, King Agrippa? I know that you believe'. Paul's direct challenge puts Agrippa in a bind: he cannot publicly deny the prophets without losing his standing with his Jewish subjects; if he affirms them, Paul's argument forces him toward acknowledging Christ. The rhetorical trap is elegant: Paul answers for Agrippa before the king can answer — 'I know that you believe' — removing the escape route of claimed ignorance.
26.28: ho de Agrippos pros ton Paulon, En oligō me peitheis Christianon poiēsai: 'Agrippa said to Paul: in a little you persuade me to make a Christian'. En oligō (in a little/briefly) — the phrase is ambiguous: 'in a short time', 'with little effort', or 'almost'. It is likely ironic — Agrippa deflects Paul's challenge with urbane humor rather than genuine engagement. Christianon — the third and only NT occurrence in Acts of the word 'Christian' (11:26; 26:28; 1 Pet 4:16); Agrippa uses it as an outsider term, which confirms its origin as a non-Christian designation.
26.29: ho de Paulos, Euxaimēn an tō theō kai en oligō kai en megalō ou monon se alla kai pantas tous akouontas mou sēmeron genesthai toioutous hopoios kai egō eimi parektos tōn desmōn toutōn: Paul's closing wish — that all present would become what he is, except for the chains. Euxaimēn (I would pray/wish) — optative of prayer. The inclusion of parektos tōn desmōn toutōn (except for these chains) is both self-deprecating humor and a pointed comparison: Paul is a prisoner speaking to the free, and he wishes them the freedom he has found.
26.30: anestē te ho basileus kai ho hēgemōn hē te Bernikē kai hoi synkathēmenoi autois: the formal dissolution of the audience — all stand, indicating the hearing is complete. The joint standing of king, governor, and Bernice is a mark of protocol. The subsequent private discussion (v.31) reflects genuine deliberation: Agrippa, Bernice, and Festus confer away from the formal setting.
26.31: kai anachōrēsantes elaloun pros allēlous legontes hoti Ouden thanatou axion e desmōn prAssei ho anthrōpos houtos: 'and having withdrawn they talked to one another saying: this man is doing nothing worthy of death or imprisonment'. Axios thanatou ē desmōn (worthy of death or bonds) — the standard Roman categories for capital and non-capital offenses. This is the fifth and clearest acquittal in the trial sequence; Agrippa and Festus reach the same conclusion that Lysias, Felix, and Festus had individually reached. The judicial verdict is unanimous: Paul is innocent.
26.32: Agrippos de tō Phēstō ephē, Apolelysthai edynato ho anthrōpos houtos ei mē epekeklēto Kaisara: 'Agrippa said to Festus: this man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar'. The conclusion of Acts' Roman judicial sequence: Paul is innocent by every court that has examined him; his appeal to Caesar is the one procedural step that makes release impossible. Luke's irony is precise — the appeal that Paul made for protection is also the mechanism that ensures he reaches Rome, fulfilling the Lord's promise of 23:11 by the most indirect possible route.

28.26: poreutheti pros ton laon touton kai eipon (poreuthēti pros ton laon touton kai eipon): Paul's final quotation of Isa 6:9-10 to the Roman Jewish community. This is the third time Acts invokes the Isaianic hardening: Jesus used it in Luke 8:10; Acts 28 closes the narrative with it. The pattern is the 'to you and to them' logic: the gospel comes first to the Jew (Rom 1:16 priority) and when resisted turns outward. Luke does not say Israel is abandoned — Paul is still quoting Scripture to Jews — but the structural conclusion of Acts is the Gentile mission unhindered (akōlytōs, the book's last word).
28.1: 'Once safely on shore, we found out that the island was called Malta' — diasōthentes (thoroughly saved/completely rescued — dia + sōzō, the salvation-verb) — the same verb root as Paul's promise 'you will be saved' (v.30, 31, 44). Luke's use of diasōzō at the moment of arrival on shore makes the landing a salvation-event: the promise of v.22-24 has been exactly fulfilled. Malta (Melitē — honey, from the Phoenician) was a Carthaginian and later Roman colony, strategically located in the central Mediterranean; its population was Punic-speaking, not Greek.
28.2: 'The islanders showed us unusual kindness (philanthropian — philanthropy/human-kindness). They built a fire and welcomed us all because it was raining and cold' — philanthropia (disp=0 but significant: the same root as Julius's philanthropōs in 27:3) is Luke's summary of Malta's reception: the pagan islanders demonstrate the human-kindness that the Roman centurion demonstrated — and that the Jerusalem mob failed to demonstrate. The fire (pyran — a bonfire), the welcome of 276 wet, cold, exhausted shipwreck survivors, is the practical form of the philanthropia. Luke repeatedly notes when pagan generosity exceeds religious community's self-protection.
28.3: 'Paul gathered a pile of brushwood and, as he put it on the fire, a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself on his hand' — Paul's participation in gathering firewood (phryganōn ti plēthos synstrephantos — gathering a multitude of sticks) is the apostolic pattern: he does not stand apart from manual labor (cf. 18:3; 20:34-35). The viper (echidna — a venomous snake) driven out by the fire's heat (apo tēs thermēs — from the heat) is a realistic detail; the creature was in the brushwood, seeking warmth, and biting defensively when disturbed. 'Fastened itself on his hand' (kathapsen tēs cheiros autou — seized/bit his hand) — the hold was enough to hang from his hand (v.4 — hanging from his hand).
28.4: 'When the islanders saw the snake hanging from his hand, they said to each other: This man must be a murderer; for though he escaped from the sea, Justice (Dikē) has not allowed him to live' — Dikē (Justice, Divine Justice) is the Greek goddess of retributive justice — the Maltese invoke her as the cosmic principle that punishes guilt even when human courts fail. The theological assumption is exact: the sea's survival proves innocence of sea-related sins; the snakebite suggests a guilt-beyond-survival. The Maltese reasoning is not ignorant superstition but follows a coherent Greek theological framework in which the gods close every escape route for the guilty.
28.5: 'But Paul shook the snake off into the fire and suffered no ill effects' — apotinaxas to thrion eis to pyr (shaking off the creature into the fire) — the casual, unhurried gesture ('shook off') rather than a panicked reaction suggests either divine calmness or divine protection; the narrative implies both. 'Suffered no ill effects' (ouden kakon epathen — he suffered nothing bad) — the Maltese expected swelling (prēsthai — to swell, v.6) and collapse. Neither came. The fulfillment of the snake-immunity promise (Luke 10:19 — 'I have given you authority to tread on serpents') enacted on Malta.
28.6: 'The people expected him to swell up or suddenly fall dead (nekron — dead; disp=2); but after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen to him, they changed their minds and said he was a god' — nekron (dead; disp=2) — the ordinary sense of a corpse, not the resurrection-technical sense. The Maltese theological inversion is complete: murderer → god; the same evidence (snake-bite + survival) that first confirmed his guilt now confirms his divinity. The crowd's oscillation mirrors the Lystra crowd's progression from 'the gods have come down to us' (14:11) to stoning (14:19) — pagan theological attribution is unstable. Luke records the divine-attribution without correcting it in this scene (contrast 14:14-15 where Paul and Barnabas refuse worship).
28.7: 'There was an estate nearby that belonged to Publius, the chief official (prōtō — first, leading official) of the island. He welcomed us to his home and showed us generous hospitality for three days' — 'Prōtos tēs nēsou' (the First of the island) is confirmed as an actual title by two Maltese inscriptions using exactly this Greek term. Luke's use of the official title is another authenticity marker: he records the correct administrative designation for Malta's Roman governor. Three days of generous hospitality (philophronōs — with lover-of-strangers kindness, philanthropy's sibling virtue) for 276 shipwreck survivors is a substantial logistical commitment.
28.8: 'His father (patēr — father; disp=2) was sick in bed, suffering from fever and dysentery. Paul went in to see him and, after prayer, placed his hands on him and healed him' — patēr (father; disp=2) in the domestic sense — Publius's biological father. The fever (pyretois — fevers, plural — indicating recurring or multiple fevers) combined with dysentery (dysenteria — bloody flux) is consistent with Malta Fever (brucellosis), endemic to Malta from unpasteurized goat's milk. 'After prayer' (proseuxamenos — having prayed) distinguishes Paul's healing from medical practice or magical technique: prayer acknowledges the divine source of the healing; 'placed his hands on him' is the physical channel; healing follows.
28.9: 'When this had happened, the rest of those on the island who had diseases came and were cured' — the healing of Publius's father becomes the occasion for a general healing mission: 'the rest of those who had diseases' (hoi loipoi hoi en tē nēsō echontes asthenias — the remaining ones on the island having illnesses) came to Paul. The pattern repeats: one high-status healing opens a community-wide healing mission (cf. Peter's mother-in-law healing → whole city gathers at sunset, Mark 1:32-34; Luke 4:40-41). Malta receives a ministry of healing that parallels the preaching-healing combination Paul conducted in cities throughout the journeys.
28.10: 'They honoured us in many ways; and when we were ready to sail, they furnished us with the supplies we needed' — 'honoured us in many ways' (pollais timais etīmēsan hēmas — they honoured us with many honours) — timē (honour, value) can refer to honorific recognition or to monetary compensation/payment; the latter is possible here (they paid them for the healing ministry). 'Furnished us with the supplies we needed' (tois pros tas chreias — the things toward the needs) — practical provisioning for the voyage continuation. The Malta interlude demonstrates the gospel's power to generate community (receiving, healing, mutual supply) even without a church established.
28.11: 'After three months we put out to sea in a ship that had wintered in the island — it was an Alexandrian ship with the figurehead of the twin gods Castor and Pollux' — the three months on Malta (October/November through January/February) is the closed-navigation period. The Alexandrian ship (another grain ship from the Egypt-Italy route, wintering at Malta) bears the figurehead Dioskouroi (Castor and Pollux — the twin sons of Zeus, patron deities of sailors, the constellation Gemini). The pagans on the ship travel under the protection of their sea-gods; Luke notes the figurehead without comment, letting the journey's safety under Paul's God speak for itself.
28.12: 'We put in at Syracuse and stayed there three days' — Syracuse (Syrakousas — the major Greek city on Sicily's SE coast) was the most important city in Sicily and a major port on the Italy-bound route. Three days there provided rest, provisioning, and perhaps contact with the local community. Luke's route-summary (Syracuse → Rhegium → Puteoli → Appian Way → Rome) is the standard Italy-arrival itinerary for Mediterranean ships and can be traced on a map with precision.
28.13: 'From there we set sail and arrived at Rhegium. The next day the south wind came up, and on the following day we reached Puteoli' — Rhegium (modern Reggio di Calabria) is at the toe of Italy, across the Strait of Messina from Sicily. The southerly (notos — south wind) that came up the next day at Rhegium enabled a fast run up Italy's west coast to Puteoli (modern Pozzuoli, near Naples), the main Italian port for Alexandrian grain ships. The two-day run from Rhegium to Puteoli is consistent with favorable southerly winds through the Strait of Messina.
28.14: 'There we found some brothers and sisters who invited us to spend a week with them. And so we came to Rome' — the Christians at Puteoli (not otherwise mentioned in the NT) represent the first Roman Christians Paul encounters: the gospel reached Italy before Paul did, through the network of travelers and traders. The seven-day stay (epimeinai par' autois hēmeras hepta — to remain with them seven days) required Julius's permission — he continues to treat Paul with the philanthropia established at Sidon. 'And so we came to Rome' — Luke's matter-of-fact announcement of the journey's end: Rome, the goal since 19:21.
28.15: 'The brothers and sisters there had heard that we were coming, and they travelled as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns to meet us. At the sight of these people Paul thanked God and was encouraged' — Roman Christians (not yet met by Paul) came 40+ miles (Forum of Appius on the Appian Way) and 30+ miles (Three Taverns, further toward Rome) to meet him. The coming-to-meet (eis apantēsin — for a meeting, for an encounter; this is the technical term for the formal civic reception of a dignitary — the parousia-reception formula) mirrors the language of 1 Thess 4:17 ('to meet the Lord in the air'). Paul's encouragers are the ones who came to escort him in to Rome. 'Thanked God and was encouraged' — the two responses to divine faithfulness: gratitude and renewed courage.
28.16: 'When we got to Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with a soldier to guard him' — 'live by himself' (menein kath' heauton — to remain by himself, privately) — Paul is not imprisoned in the public jail but lives in his own rented accommodation (v.30 — en idiō misthōmati — in his own hired dwelling) under house arrest, with a single military guard. This custodia militaris (military custody) allowed him to receive visitors, write letters, and continue preaching — the form of imprisonment that produced Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and Philemon.
28.17: 'Three days later he called together the local Jewish leaders. When they had assembled, Paul said to them: My brothers, although I have done nothing against our people or against the customs of our ancestors, I was arrested in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans' — Paul's immediate initiative toward the Roman Jewish community (synagogē archous — the synagogue leaders, the plural implies multiple Roman synagogues) follows his invariable pattern: to the Jew first. 'My brothers' (andres adelphoi — men, brothers) is Paul's standard synagogue address (13:15; 22:1; 23:1, 6; 28:17). His defense is pre-emptive: he establishes innocence (nothing against the people or the ancestral customs) before any accusation arrives.
28.18: 'They examined me and wanted to release me, because I was not guilty of any crime deserving death' — Paul's summary of the Roman judicial process (Felix, Festus, Agrippa — Acts 24-26) in one sentence: the consistent conclusion of three Roman officials was innocence (cf. 23:29 — Claudius Lysias; 25:25 — Festus; 26:31-32 — Agrippa). Luke has documented the legal innocence of Paul at length so that Paul's arrival at Rome as a prisoner cannot be misread as evidence of guilt; it is evidence of Jewish political pressure, not Roman legal condemnation.
28.19: 'When the Jews objected, I was compelled to make an appeal to Caesar. I certainly did not intend to bring any charge against my own nation' — Paul's appeal to Caesar (Acts 25:11) was defensive, not offensive: it was not to accuse the Jews before Caesar but to prevent the Jerusalem Sanhedrin from acquiring jurisdiction over his case. 'I certainly did not intend to bring any charge' (ōs tou ethnous mou echōn ti katēgorein — not as having anything to accuse my nation of) — the syntax of denial; Paul approaches Rome's Jews as a fellow Jew, not as an adversary.
28.20: 'For this reason I have asked to see you and talk with you. It is because of the hope of Israel (tēn elpida tou Israēl — the hope of Israel; disp=2 for elpis) that I am bound with this chain' — elpis tou Israēl (the hope of Israel) is the theological summary of Paul's entire mission: the hope = the resurrection (Acts 23:6; 24:15; 26:6-7), which is Israel's covenantal expectation. Paul's chain is not a Roman punishment for crime but a paradoxical badge of fidelity to Israel's own hope. The chain that binds him binds him to the promise that bound Abraham.
28.21: 'They replied: We have not received any letters from Judea concerning you, and none of our people who have come from there has reported or said anything bad about you' — the Roman Jewish community's ignorance of Paul is surprising given his prominence in Jerusalem's temple riots (21:27-36); either the Jerusalem leadership chose not to pursue the matter to Rome, or communications had not yet arrived. The Romans' openness (v.22 — 'we want to hear what your views are') gives Paul the opportunity he has had in every city: the receptive audience that precedes the decisive division.
28.22: 'But we want to hear what your views are, for we know that people everywhere are talking against this sect (haireseōs tautēs — this sect/choice/party)' — hairesis (sect, school of thought, disp=0 here but theologically significant) was the standard Greek term for a philosophical or religious movement defined by distinctive doctrines. The same word is used for the 'sect of the Nazarenes' (24:5) and Paul's own self-description of 'the Way which they call a sect' (24:14). The Roman Jews' description ('people everywhere are talking against this sect') confirms the movement's wide, if controversial, recognition.
28.23: 'They arranged to meet Paul on a certain day, and came in even larger numbers to the place where he was staying. He witnessed to them from morning till evening, explaining and testifying about the kingdom of God, and trying to persuade them about Jesus from the Law of Moses (nomou Mōyseōs — the Law of Moses; disp=3 for nomos) and the Prophets' — the 'morning till evening' exposition is the longest single preaching session recorded in Acts — Paul's full-day messianic demonstration from Torah and Prophets. The two-witness structure (Law of Moses + Prophets) is the standard prophetic-fulfillment argument (Luke 24:27, 44). The kingdom of God (v.23, 31) bookends the chapter: Acts begins with the disciples asking about the kingdom (1:3) and ends with Paul proclaiming it.
28.24: 'Some were convinced by what he said, but others would not believe' — the divided response (hoi men... hoi de — some... others) is the universal pattern of Paul's synagogue missions: Antioch (13:43-45), Iconium (14:1-2), Thessalonica (17:4-5), Berea (17:11-12), Corinth (18:4-8). The division is not accidental; it is the predicted consequence of the Isaiah word that Paul is about to quote. The division between believing Jews and unbelieving Jews does not end Jewish mission — it explains it theologically.
28.25: 'They disagreed among themselves and began to leave after Paul had made this final statement: The Holy Spirit (to pneuma to hagion — the Holy Spirit; disp=3 for pneuma, disp=2 for hagios) spoke the truth to your ancestors (dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou — through Isaiah the prophet) when he said' — Paul's attribution of Isaiah 6:9-10 to 'the Holy Spirit' makes the Spirit the author of OT prophecy: the prophetic word is simultaneously Isaiah's word and the Spirit's word. 'Your ancestors' (pateras hymōn — your fathers; disp=2 for patēr — the OT-covenant fathers to whom Isaiah originally spoke) — the quotation applies to both the original audience and their descendants who now stand before Paul.
28.27: 'For this people's heart has become calloused; they hardly hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts and turn, and I would heal them' — the Isaiah 6:9-10 citation (in its LXX form) is the most-quoted OT passage in the NT: Matthew (13:14-15), Mark (4:12), Luke (8:10), John (12:40), and now Acts 28:26-27 all apply it to Jewish unbelief. The LXX version emphasizes the people's own self-blinding ('they have closed their eyes' — ekammusan — aorist active, they closed) rather than the divine hardening of the Hebrew ('make their hearts fat'). The difference matters: the Greek version leaves more room for the culpability of the people who chose not to see.
28.28: 'Therefore I want you to know that God's salvation (to sōtērion tou theou — the salvation of God; disp=2 for theos) has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen!' — to sōtērion (the salvation, used as a noun) echoes the Nunc Dimittis (Luke 2:30 — 'for my eyes have seen your salvation') and Simeon's identification of Jesus as the sōtērion prepared before all peoples. Paul's final declaration to Rome's Jewish leaders repeats what has been said since Acts 13:46: the Gentile turn is a consequence of Jewish unbelief, not a replacement of Jewish priority. 'They will listen' (autoi kai akousontai — they indeed will hear/listen) — the Gentile receptivity that Isaiah's hardened-Israel passage implies by contrast.
28.29: 'After he said this, the Jews left, arguing vigorously among themselves' — verse 29 is omitted in most early MSS (it does not appear in p74, Aleph, A, B, E, or 33); it is present in later MSS and the Byzantine tradition. Many commentators consider it a scribal addition clarifying that the Jews departed. Whether original or not, its content is consistent with the narrative pattern: the divided-and-departing response (hoi men... hoi de — some... others, as in v.24) has occurred at every Paul-synagogue encounter; the argument among themselves is the community's internal division that the gospel always produces.
28.30: 'For two whole years Paul stayed there in his own rented house and welcomed all who came to see him' — the 'whole years' (holēn dietian — an entire two-year period) at Rome in custodia militaris produced the prison letters (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) and the ongoing mission summarized in v.31. 'In his own rented house' (en idiō misthōmati — at his own expense) — Paul paid for his own lodging even under arrest; the manual labor and tent-making trade sustained him even in Rome. 'Welcomed all who came to see him' — the open-door policy of Paul's imprisonment: the chain does not imprison the gospel.
28.31: 'He proclaimed (kērassōn — heralding, preaching) the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ — with all boldness (meta pasēs parrēsias — with all openness/freedom of speech) and without hindrance (akōlytōs — unhindered, prevented-by-no-one; the final word of the book)' — the book of Acts ends on akōlytōs — a single adverb meaning 'without hindrance, unimpeded.' The gospel has traveled from Jerusalem to Rome without final suppression; Paul preaches the kingdom and teaches the Lord Jesus with full parrēsia (bold free-speech) in the capital of the empire. The open ending — no mention of Paul's trial outcome, no resolution — is Luke's deliberate conclusion: the word of God is not bound (2 Tim 2:9); the story continues beyond any document's last page.

6.1: Hellēnistai pros tous Hebraious ('Hellenists against the Hebrews'): the two groups are Greek-speaking diaspora Jews who had settled in Jerusalem versus Aramaic-speaking native Palestinian Jews. Both were Christians; the division reflects the sociolinguistic fault line within Second Temple Judaism rather than any ethnic distinction. The complaint about the daily food distribution (en tē diakonia tē kathēmerinē) identifies the church's first internal administrative failure — not a theological dispute but a logistical inequity with demographic dimensions.
6.2: kataleipsantas ton logon tou theou ('leaving/neglecting the word of God'): the Twelve's reasoning prioritizes the ministry of the word over administrative management. diakonein trapezais ('serve tables'): diakonia (service/ministry) will be applied both to the Twelve's ministry of the word (v.4) and to the Seven's table service — Luke does not demean the Seven's work but establishes a differentiation of function. Both are forms of diakonia.
6.3: martyroumenous (martyroumenous, 'well-attested/of good reputation'): the qualifications for the Seven — good reputation, Spirit-filling, wisdom — are the same qualities required for leadership throughout the Pastoral Epistles and point to the proto-diaconal office. Plēreis pneumatos hagiou kai sophias ('full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom'): the pairing of Spirit and wisdom reflects the OT endowment of the craftsmen of the Tabernacle (Exod 31:3; 35:31) and the Servant (Isa 11:2), marking these men as set apart for divine service.
6.4: proskarterēsomen (proskarterēsomen, 'we will devote ourselves'): the same verb used for the community's prayer in 1:14 and for their adherence to the apostles' teaching in 2:42. The Twelve's commitment to prayer and the word-ministry is not a demotion of administrative work but a recognition of their specific calling. The word-and-prayer combination is the apostolic core from which everything else flows.
6.5: Stephanos, anēr plērēs pisteōs kai pneumatos hagiou ('Stephen, a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit'): Luke's introduction of Stephen singles him out from the Seven with the same Spirit-fullness language that will characterize his subsequent ministry (v.8) and his death (7:55). The Seven's names are all Greek (Nikolaos alone is explicitly identified as a proselyte), suggesting that the seven chosen to serve the Hellenist widows were themselves Hellenist Jews — an administratively elegant solution.
6.6: proseuxamenoi epethēkan autois tas cheiras ('having prayed they laid their hands on them'): the commissioning gesture combines prayer and handlaying. In the OT, handlaying transferred authority and blessing (Num 27:18-23, Joshua's commissioning; Deut 34:9, the Spirit of wisdom transferred to Joshua). Here it is not ordination in a later technical sense but a formal recognition and authorization of the Seven's specific task. The same gesture in 13:3 will commission Paul and Barnabas for the Gentile mission.
6.7: ho logos tou theou ēuxanen ('the word of God kept growing'): the third of Luke's Acts progress reports (2:47; 4:4; 5:14; 6:7 — and more to follow). Each summary follows resolution of a crisis: the Ananias crisis resolved → growth; the Sanhedrin crisis resolved → growth; the Hellenist administrative crisis resolved → growth. polys te ochlos tōn hiereōn hypēkouon tē pistei ('a large number of priests were obedient to the faith'): this detail is striking — members of the temple establishment, the class most implicated in Jesus's crucifixion, are being converted. The gospel is reaching the center of official Judaism.
6.8: plērēs charitos kai dynameōs ('full of grace and power'): Stephen's characterization shifts from administrative appointment to apostolic-level ministry — sēmeia kai terata megala ('great signs and wonders'). The same vocabulary used for the apostles (2:43; 5:12) is now applied to one of the Seven. Luke signals that the Spirit's work through the Hellenist leaders extends beyond the Twelve, preparing for the geographic expansion of the mission through Stephen's speech and Philip's ministry in ch.8.
6.9: Synagōgē Libertinōn ('Synagogue of the Freedmen'): likely descendants of Jews taken to Rome as slaves by Pompey in 63 BC and subsequently freed, who maintained their own synagogue in Jerusalem. The listing of multiple groups — Cyrenians, Alexandrians, Cilicians, Asians — indicates that Stephen's opponents were Diaspora Jews who had returned to Jerusalem, many of them Greek-speakers like Stephen himself. The opposition comes from within the Hellenist community, not from native Palestinians.
6.10: ouk ischyon antistēnai tē sophia kai tō pneumati ('they could not withstand the wisdom and the Spirit'): the opposition's inability to overcome Stephen's arguments fulfills Jesus's promise of Luke 21:15 ('I will give you a mouth and wisdom which none of your adversaries will be able to withstand or contradict'). Sophia (wisdom) and pneuma (Spirit) are the gifts that cannot be matched by the synagogue debaters' conventional learning.
6.11: blasphēmous logous eis Mōusēn kai ton theon ('blasphemous words against Moses and God'): the charge of blasphemy — the technical capital offence under Jewish law — is introduced through suborned false witnesses (hypebalan andras, 'they secretly persuaded men'). Luke's language explicitly marks the testimony as false (martyras pseudeis, v.13), paralleling Jesus's trial (Luke 22:66-71). Stephen's situation replicates his Lord's: falsely accused of blasphemy before the same body.
6.12: kinēsantes ton laon kai tous presbyterous kai tous grammateis ('stirring up the people and the elders and the scribes'): the progression is significant — the opposition cannot refute Stephen's arguments (v.10), so they resort to crowd agitation and official mobilization. The laos (people) who served as protection for the apostles in chs.4-5 is now being turned against Stephen. The populace is more volatile than the Sanhedrin's institutional opposition.
6.13: martyras pseudeis ('false witnesses'): Luke's explicit identification of the witnesses as false contrasts with the apostolic martyria (true witness). The parallel to Jesus's trial is precise: false witnesses at the Sanhedrin, the charge of threatening the temple, the high priest's questioning. Luke is constructing a deliberate parallel between Stephen's passion and Jesus's passion, preparing the reader to understand Stephen as the proto-martyr who follows his Lord's pattern fully.
6.14: katalysei ton topon touton ('will destroy this place'): the charge echoes the accusation at Jesus's trial (Mark 14:58; Matt 26:61). Stephen's actual theology of the temple — worked out in 7:44-50 — argues not for destruction but for transcendence: the temple was always a pointer to something greater that has now arrived. The accusation distorts his position. allaxei ta ethē ('will change the customs'): this is the deeper charge — that Jesus changes what Moses established — which ch.7 addresses as Torah correctly understood pointing to Christ.
6.15: hōsei prosōpon angelou ('like the face of an angel'): Stephen's transfigured appearance before the Sanhedrin prolepticly manifests the glory he will see in v.7:55-56. In the OT, the face-of-an-angel motif marks someone as a divine messenger in the presence of God (cf. Dan 3:25, 'the fourth man is like a son of the gods'; Judg 13:6, 'the man of God had the face of an angel'). The Sanhedrin is not simply judging a man; they are, unknowingly, facing a man who is standing at the threshold of the divine glory he is about to fully enter.

9.1: empneōn apeilēs kai phonou (empneōn apeilēs kai phonou, 'breathing out threats and murder'): empneō (breathe out) applied to threats makes Saul's hostility atmospheric — violence is his very breath. Luke's physicality intensifies the contrast with the new breath (pnoē) of the Spirit at Pentecost. Paul's retrospective self-description ('beyond measure I persecuted', Gal 1:13) matches Luke's characterization.
9.2: hodos (hē Hodos, 'the Way'): Acts' preferred self-designation for the Christian movement (9:2; 19:9, 23; 22:4; 24:14, 22). The term may derive from Jesus's 'I am the Way' (John 14:6) or from Isa 40:3's 'way in the desert'; it presents the community as those traveling a specific path rather than holding a particular ideology — discipleship as direction of life. tēs synagōgēs grammata: letters to synagogues assert the high priest's judicial jurisdiction over diaspora Jews.
9.3: exaiphnēs te auton periēstrapsen phōs ek tou ouranou ('suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him'): exaiphnēs (suddenly, immediately) marks the theophanic disruption; periēstrapsen (flashed around) describes the surrounding light of divine presence. The Damascus theophany will be narrated three times in Acts (9, 22, 26) — each retelling emphasizes different aspects for different audiences. Luke's frequency signals its foundational importance for the Gentile mission.
9.4: Saoul Saoul ti me diōkeis ('Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?'): the double-name vocative is the biblical marker of divine address at decisive moments (Abraham in Gen 22:11; Moses in Exod 3:4; Samuel in 1 Sam 3:10). The Hebrew form Saoul (not the Greek Saulos) suggests the voice addressed him in his own language — confirmed in 22:7 and 26:14 (Hebrew/Aramaic). The question 'why do you persecute me?' identifies the community's persecution with Christ's own.
9.5: ego eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis ('I am Jesus whom you are persecuting'): the identification formula ego eimi (I am) echoes the divine self-disclosure of Exod 3:14 and the Johannine Christological formula. The risen Christ's identification of himself with his persecuted followers (not 'my followers' but 'me') is the foundation for the church's theology of suffering — to suffer for Christ is to suffer with Christ (Col 1:24; 2 Cor 1:5).
9.6: alla anastēthi kai eiselthe eis tēn polin ('but rise and enter the city'): anastēthi (rise) — the same imperative as resurrection proclamation — is the first word Saul hears from Jesus. He is directed to the city where he planned to arrest believers; the city becomes the place of his own transformation. The passive reception ('you will be told') keeps the initiative entirely divine.
9.7: hestekeisan de enneoī hoi andres hoi synoiporountes ('the men traveling with him stood speechless'): enneoi (speechless, struck dumb) is a rare word for the stupor of shock. The companions present a corroboration problem: 22:9 says they saw the light but did not hear the voice; 9:7 says they heard the sound but saw no one. The different objects of 'hear' (phōnē) suggest they heard a sound without understanding the articulate speech Saul received.
9.8: oiden oun Saulos apo tēs gēs ōnewgmenōn de tōn ophthalmōn autou ouden eblepēn ('Saul got up from the ground; when his eyes were opened he could see nothing'): the open eyes that see nothing are the paradox of the encounter — the persecutor who thought he saw clearly is now physically blind. Three days without sight or food mirrors the three days of the resurrection; Saul's transformation has a cruciform structure: the old Saul must die before the new Paul can rise.
9.9: kai ēn hēmeras treis mē blepōn kai ouk ephagen oud' epien ('for three days he was blind and neither ate nor drank'): the three-day fast parallels Jonah's three days (Jonah 1:17) and the resurrection's three-day pattern. The blindness removes all of Saul's previous competencies — he who pursued with authority is now led by the hand; the man who destroyed others' communities must now be cared for. The fast signals the gravity of the transition.
9.10: mathētēs de tis ēn en Damasko onomati Hananias ('there was a disciple in Damascus named Ananias'): Ananias is identified only as mathētēs (disciple) — not an apostle, not a prominent figure, but an ordinary member of the Damascus community. The Lord speaks to him in a vision (en horamati), using the same address-form as to Saul — the divine initiative reaches both persecutor and ordinary believer simultaneously.
9.11: Saulos gar idou proseuchetai ('for behold, Saul is praying'): the word the Lord gives Ananias as reassurance is that Saul is praying — the one who came to arrest those who call on the name is himself now calling on the name. The idou (behold) marks this as surprising evidence: the persecutor in prayer is the visible sign of his transformation before any baptism or anointing.
9.12: kai eiden andra onomati Hananias eiselthonta kai epithenta autō cheiras ('and he has seen a man named Ananias come and place hands on him'): the double-vision structure (Saul's vision of Ananias, Ananias's vision of the Lord) interlocks to confirm the divine coordination of the encounter. The Lord has prepared both parties simultaneously; neither is acting on their own discernment alone.
9.13: kyrie, ēkousa apo pollōn peri tou andros toutou ('Lord, I have heard from many about this man'): Ananias's hesitation is presented without editorial criticism — it is reasonable protest based on real intelligence. The community's experience of Saul's persecution is the valid ground for Ananias's fear; the Lord's response does not rebuke the hesitation but overrides it with a larger purpose.
9.14: tous epikaloumenous to onoma sou ('those who call on your name'): the community is defined by its practice of calling on the name — the Joel-quotation (2:21) made 'calling on the name of the Lord' the salvation criterion; the Damascus disciples are those who do this. Saul came to arrest those who call on the name; the Lord who is called upon is sending him back to serve the community he persecuted.
9.15: skeuos eklogēs estin moi houtos ('this man is my chosen instrument'): skeuos (vessel, instrument) is used for functional objects; Paul becomes in Acts the vessel through which the mission extends to Gentiles, kings, and Israel. Eklogē (election, choice) grounds the appointment in divine prerogative — Paul will later describe his conversion as grace 'from my mother's womb' (Gal 1:15-16). The three-part scope (Gentiles, kings, Israel) matches Acts' narrative structure through ch.28.
9.16: dei gar auton hypodeixai hosa dei auton hyper tou onomatos mou pathein ('for I will show him how much he must suffer for my name'): the repeated dei (it is necessary) frames Saul's future suffering as divinely ordained — the same necessity that governed Jesus's own passion (Luke 9:22; 22:37). The persecutor will become the persecuted; the man who made others suffer for the name will himself suffer for the name. Paul's theology of the cross is grounded in this commission.
9.17: adelphe Saoul ('Brother Saul'): Ananias's address is the decisive social act of reconciliation — the member of the community that Saul came to arrest calls him brother before the healing or baptism. The brotherhood precedes the restoration of sight; the community's embrace is the first sign of the transformation the Lord announced. The address echoes Peter's adelphoi to the Pentecost crowd.
9.18: hōsei lepides apo tōn ophthalmōn autou ('something like scales from his eyes'): lepides (scales, flakes) is a medical or descriptive term for the film or crust that covered sightless eyes; the imagery of scales falling away is vivid and physical. anablepsas (seeing again): the ana- prefix indicates the restoration of what was lost — not new sight but returned sight. Baptism follows immediately; the sequence (sight → baptism → food → strength) marks a complete restoration.
9.19: egeneto meta tōn en Damaskō mathētōn hēmeras tinas ('he spent some days with the disciples in Damascus'): Saul's first post-conversion community is the community he came to destroy. The 'some days' in Damascus before his synagogue preaching (v.20) and his later Arabian withdrawal (Gal 1:17) are reconciled if the Galatians period comes between the Damascus days and the Jerusalem visit.
9.20: eutheōs en tais synagōgais ekērysso ton Iēsoun ('immediately in the synagogues he was proclaiming Jesus'): eutheōs (immediately) is Luke's signal for Spirit-driven urgency; no apprenticeship intervenes. The content is specified as proclaiming Jesus — the same object as Philip's Samaritan mission (v.5) — but now from one who knew the OT scripturally as well as Ananias's community did experientially.
9.21: pantes de hoi akouontes existanto kai elegon ('all who heard were astonished and said'): the astonishment (existanto) is the same word used for Simon Magus's effect on the Samaritans (8:9, 11); the transformation of the destroyer into a proclaimer produces the same shock as a miracle. symbibazōn (demonstrating, proving): the verb means to bring together evidence to form a conclusion — Saul's method is demonstrative proof, not just assertion.
9.22: pleiō de mallon enedunamōto ('he grew more and more powerful'): enedunamoō (strengthen, empower) is the Pauline growth verb (Phil 4:13; 2 Tim 4:17); the same power that Saul was trying to suppress in the church now characterizes his own ministry. symbibazōn hoti houtos estin ho Christos ('proving that this is the Messiah'): the same symbibazōn as v.22 — Saul is an exegetical demonstrator, reasoning from texts.
9.23: hōs de eplērounto hēmerai hikanai ('when many days had passed'): the time-gap accommodates Paul's Galatians account of the Arabian withdrawal (Gal 1:17); Luke compresses a period that Paul describes as significant. synebouleusanto oi Ioudaioi anelein auton ('the Jews plotted to kill him'): the pattern will repeat throughout Acts — the same communities that opposed the apostles before Paul now oppose Paul. The persecutor suffers the persecution he inflicted.
9.24: pareterounton de kai tas pylas hēmeras te kai nyktos hopōs auton anelōsin (they were watching the city gates day and night to kill him): the continuous pareterounton (keeping watch) shows the siege-mentality of the plot. Paul's account in 2 Cor 11:32 adds that the ethnarch of Aretas was also involved — a confluence of Jewish and Nabatean opposition. The basket escape (v.25) is one of Paul's most remembered humiliations (2 Cor 11:33).
9.25: labontes de hoi mathētai autou nyktos dia tou teichous kathēkan auton chalasantes en spyridi ('his disciples lowered him through the wall at night in a basket'): spyris is a large wicker or rope basket; the escape method is both practical and ironic — the zealot who came bearing arrest-letters is smuggled out like cargo. The community (now hoi mathētai autou, 'his disciples') that he came to destroy now protects him.
9.26: peirāto kollasthai tois mathētais; kai pantes ephobounto auton ('he tried to join the disciples, but all were afraid of him'): kollaomai (attach oneself to, associate with) is the social-bonding verb; the Jerusalem community's fear is entirely understandable. The irony continues: Saul whose letters authorized the arrest of believers is now himself treated as a security threat by the community he wants to join.
9.27: Barnabas de epilabomenos auton ēgagen pros tous apostolous ('but Barnabas took hold of him and brought him to the apostles'): epilambanomai (take hold of, grasp) implies an initiative Barnabas took without being asked. Barnabas (named 'son of encouragement' in 4:36) becomes the advocate whose personal vouching enables Saul's reception. The report he gives — Damascus road event, Damascus preaching boldly — establishes the apostolic authentication Saul needed.
9.28: eisporeuomenos kai ekporeuomenos eis Ierousalēm parrēsiazomenos en tō onomati tou kyriou ('moving freely in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord'): eisporeuomenos kai ekporeuomenos (going in and out) is the Septuagintal idiom for active public ministry (the same phrase Ananias used to describe Jesus's ministry in 1:21). Parrēsiazomenos (speaking boldly) is Acts' marker of Spirit-empowered public testimony.
9.29: elalei te kai sunezētei pros tous Hellēnistas ('he was speaking and debating with the Hellenistic Jews'): Saul's natural constituency — Greek-speaking diaspora Jews in Jerusalem — is the same community that martyred Stephen (6:9). The debate partners who killed Stephen now try to kill Saul; his conversion has transferred him from persecutor to persecuted within the same Hellenistic Jewish milieu.
9.30: katēgagon auton eis Kaisareian kai exapesteilan auton eis Tarson ('they brought him down to Caesarea and sent him off to Tarsus'): Tarsus is Saul's home city (22:3), a safe haven that also positions him in the region of Cilicia and Syria where Gal 1:21 places his early ministry. The Caesarea → Tarsus route (by sea) removes him from the Jerusalem danger; God has arranged Saul's brief Jerusalem visit (15 days, Gal 1:18) and now provides the escape route.
9.31: hē men oun ekklēsia kath' holēs tēs Ioudaias kai Galilaiias kai Samareias eichen eirēnēn oikodoumoumenē ('the church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace and was built up'): Luke's summary statement covers the three regions of Acts 1:8's mandate. Oikodomeō (build up) is the Pauline church-building metaphor (1 Cor 3:9-15; Eph 2:20-22); the church's growth in peacetime has the same architectural structure. tō phobō tou kyriou ... tē paraklēsei tou hagiou pneumatos: the twin modes of community formation — reverential fear and Spirit-encouragement.
9.32: egeneto de Petron dierchoumenon dia pantōn katelthein kai pros tous hagious tous katoikountas Lydda ('Peter went to visit the saints who lived in Lydda'): tous hagious (the saints/holy ones) is the covenant-community designation derived from OT qedoshim; it is Paul's standard address in his letters and appears here in Luke for what seems the first time in Acts. The Lydda-Joppa-Caesarea narrative (9:32-10:48) traces Peter's coastal journey toward the pivotal Cornelius encounter.
9.33: Iēsous Christos iatai se ('Jesus Christ heals you'): Peter does not invoke the name as a formula but makes a direct declarative statement — the healing is Jesus Christ's action, with Peter as the instrument. Strōson seautō ('make your bed/mat'): the practical instruction confirms the healing's completeness — not merely restored sensation but immediate functional capacity.
9.34: pantes hoi katoikountes Lydda kai ton Sarōna hoi tines epestrepsan epi ton kyrion ('all who lived in Lydda and Sharon turned to the Lord'): the geographic specificity (Lydda and the Sharon plain) grounds the mission in identifiable territory; these villages would later become significant in the Jewish-Christian communities of the second century. The mass turning (epestrepsan) precedes the Cornelius episode — the coastal plain is being prepared for the Gentile breakthrough.
9.35: mathētria tis ēn en Ioppē onomati Tabitha ('there was a disciple named Tabitha'): mathētria (female disciple) is a hapax in the NT — the only occurrence of the feminine form. Luke's use of it for Tabitha is deliberate: she is not merely a helper or patron but a disciple in her own right. The bilingual name (Dorkas in Greek = gazelle) shows her integration into both Jewish and Hellenistic communities. Her ergōn agathōn kai eleēmosynōn (good works and almsgiving) are the marks of active covenant-community life.
9.36: egeneto de en tais hēmerais ekeinais asthenēsasan autēn apothanein ('in those days she became ill and died'): the temporal marker en tais hēmerais ekeinais connects Tabitha's death to the Lydda mission, setting up Peter's proximity as providential. The preparation of her body (lousantes ethekan en hyperōō, washed and placed in the upper room) is Jewish burial preparation; the upper room is the same distinctive Jerusalem Christian meeting space (1:13; 20:8).
9.37: aposteilantes duo andras pros auton parekaloun ('sending two men they urged him'): the two-man delegation mirrors the pattern of official mission (10:7; Luke 10:1); the urgency of their request — Peter is nearby, the death is recent — suggests they expected or hoped for resurrection, not merely comfort. The appeal to 'not delay in coming' (mē okneō dielthein heōs hēmōn) is the language of life-or-death urgency.
9.38: pasai hai chērai pareistēkeisan autō klaious ai kai epideiknymena chitōnas kai himatia ('all the widows stood around him weeping and showing tunics and cloaks'): the widows' display of Tabitha's work is both a testimony to her ministry and an appeal grounded in relationship. The clothing she made for them represents her eleēmosyne (almsgiving) made concrete; the community's grief is proportional to the loss of a specific, irreplaceable benefactor.
9.39: ekteinasa de Petros ta gonata proseuxato ('Peter knelt down and prayed'): Peter's kneeling prayer before the miracle reproduces the pattern of Jesus's prayer before raising Lazarus (John 11:41-42). The dependence on the Father is explicit: Peter does not command the resurrection but prepares through prayer. The contrast with Jesus's immediate word of command (Luke 8:54) is intentional — the apostle acts in Christ's name and power, not in his own authority.
9.40: Tabitha, anastēthi ('Tabitha, arise'): the Aramaic retention matches Jesus's Talitha koum at Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41); the parallel is deliberate — Peter raising Tabitha is the apostolic continuation of Jesus's resurrection-ministry. aneblepsas kai idousa ton Petron anekathisen ('opening her eyes and seeing Peter, she sat up'): the sequence — eyes open, recognizes Peter, sits up — mirrors awakening from sleep, which is Acts' consistent resurrection imagery.
9.41: dous de autē cheira anestēsen autēn ('taking her by the hand he helped her up'): the raising-by-the-hand echoes Jesus's gesture with Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41; Luke 8:54) and Peter's raising of the lame man (3:7). The bodily, physical touch is the sign of genuine resurrection — not a vision or spiritual experience but a hand grasped and a person lifted. Tois hagiois kai tais chērais: Peter presents her to both the whole community and the widows specifically — the most affected are the first to receive her back.
9.42: gnōston de egeneto kath' holēs tēs Ioppēs kai episteusan polloi epi ton kyrion ('this became known throughout Joppa and many believed in the Lord'): the Tabitha resurrection is the anchor event for the Joppa community; the same pattern of miracle → belief → community-expansion governs the Lydda and Sharon response (v.35). Joppa is the city where Jonah boarded the ship to flee; it becomes the city where Peter receives the vision that sends him to the Gentiles (ch.10).
9.43: egeneto de hēmeras hikanas meinai en Ioppē para tini Simōni bursει ('he stayed in Joppa for some time with a tanner named Simon'): a tanner's work with animal hides made him ritually impure by Jewish standards; Peter's willingness to lodge with him is a first step toward the boundary-crossing that the Cornelius episode will complete. Luke's geographic and social placement of Peter here prepares the reader for the vision of unclean animals that will challenge Peter's purity-map in ch.10.

12.1: Hērōdēs ho basileus ('King Herod'): this is Herod Agrippa I (grandson of Herod the Great), who ruled Judea from AD 41-44 after receiving the kingdom from Claudius. Unlike the tetrarchs of Luke's Gospel, he held the full title of king over the entire Herodian territory. His persecution of the church is politically motivated — v.3 shows he acted because it 'met with approval among the Jewish leaders' — establishing him as an opportunistic rather than principled opponent of the movement.
12.2: aneilen de Iakōbon ton adelphon Iōannou machaira ('he killed James the brother of John with the sword'): the death of James son of Zebedee — the first of the Twelve to die — is reported without commentary or martyrdom account. Luke's laconic treatment contrasts sharply with the extended Stephen narrative; perhaps the brevity reflects the speed and summary nature of James's execution, or a Lucan preference to focus on the continuing mission rather than deaths. Machaira (sword) suggests decapitation — the execution method for enemies of the state under Roman law.
12.3: hēmerai tōn azymōn ('days of Unleavened Bread'): the Passover-season arrest of Peter deliberately echoes the Passover-season arrest of Jesus (Luke 22:1-2). Luke's temporal note is not merely historical precision but typological signaling: God delivers his servant at Passover, as he has before. The Exodus pattern structures both the Passion and the apostolic mission.
12.4: tetradion stratiōtōn tessarsin ('four squads of four soldiers'): sixteen soldiers in rotating shifts of four meant continuous guard coverage. The military detail is historically precise; Roman custodial practice for high-value prisoners required soldiers chained directly to the prisoner (v.6). Meta to Pascha ('after the Passover'): Herod postpones the trial until after the festival, unwilling to execute during the feast — the same political calculation as before the Passion (Luke 22:2).
12.5: proseuchē de ēn ektenōs ginomenē ('earnest prayer was being made'): ektenōs (intensely, fervently, stretched-out) appears only twice in the NT — here and Luke 22:44, where Jesus prayed ektenōs in Gethsemane. Luke's parallel is deliberate: at the crisis point, the response is prayer stretched to its limits. The community's prayer for Peter mirrors Jesus's Gethsemane prayer before his Passion; the outcome — miraculous deliverance — is the resurrection pattern applied to the apostle.
12.6: hē de nyx ekeinē ēn ho Petros koimōmenos ('that very night Peter was sleeping'): Peter's sleep the night before his anticipated execution recalls the disciples' sleep in Gethsemane but inverts its significance. Where the disciples' sleep in Gethsemane represented failure of vigilance, Peter's sleep represents the peace of one entrusting himself to God (Ps 4:8: 'in peace I will lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety'). The chains and guards frame the setting's impossibility; the sleep frames Peter's trust.
12.7: angelos Kyriou epestē kai phōs elampsen en tō oikēmati ('an angel of the Lord appeared and light shone in the cell'): the angelic appearance with light is a theophanic marker throughout the OT and Luke-Acts (1:11-12; 2:9; Acts 10:3). Pataxas de tēn pleuran ('striking his side') — Luke's vocabulary is neutral but the physical gesture is intimate; the angel wakes Peter directly. The sequence — light, striking, waking, chains falling — is structured as a miniature liberation narrative.
12.8: The angel's instructions — dress, put on sandals, wrap your cloak — are practical and specific, in contrast to Peter's dreamlike disorientation (v.9). The mundane detail of clothing has been read as an Exodus allusion (Exod 12:11: 'eat it with your cloak tucked into your belt, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand'), though Luke does not make the connection explicit. The specificity authenticates the account's grounding in eyewitness memory.
12.9: ouk ēdei hoti alēthes estin to ginomenon dia tou angelou ('he did not know that what the angel was doing was real'): Peter's cognitive state is horama-like — he processes the experience as vision rather than event. This is Luke's narrative concession to the marvel: even the recipient did not initially believe what was happening. The use of horama (vision, v.9) connects to the Acts 10 vision language; divine communication and divine action blur at their edges.
12.10: aute automate ēnoigē autois hē pyle ('the gate opened to them by itself'): automate (automatically, by itself) is a rare word in biblical Greek; it appears in Mark 4:28 for the earth producing grain 'by itself' as a natural process. Here the automatic opening is miraculous — no agent, no mechanism. Luke's vocabulary choice emphasizes that the gate's opening was neither accidental nor humanly engineered. The iron gate (sidarean pylēn) marks the definitive outer boundary; once through it, Peter is in the open city.
12.11: en emautō genomenos ('having come to himself'): the Greek idiom (en emautō ginomai) means to recover one's senses, to return to normal consciousness. It appears in Luke 15:17 for the prodigal son's moment of recognition. Peter's coming-to-himself marks the transition from dreamlike disorientation to clear-eyed recognition of divine rescue. Exeleto me ek cheiros Hērōdou ('rescued me from Herod's hand'): the OT idiom of God's hand rescuing from a king's hand (2 Sam 3:18; 1 Kgs 8:53).
12.12: Mary the mother of John Mark is identified as the host of a prayer meeting, suggesting a house-church gathering. The name-chain — Mary, John called Mark — introduces John Mark, who will become significant as a point of contention between Paul and Barnabas (15:37-39) and who is identified with the author of the second Gospel in later tradition (Papias, ca. AD 130). Luke's attention to names in Acts reflects careful sourcing; his information about the Jerusalem community is consistently specific.
12.13: Rhodē (Rhoda, meaning 'rose'): a maidservant who is named — unusual for slaves in ancient texts. The naming signals that Rhoda's role in the story was remembered in community tradition. Luke's narrative here is unusually novelistic — the servant's astonishment, her running back without opening the door, her excited announcement — suggesting a story told and retold within the community with affection for its vivid details.
12.14: Rhoda's failure to open the door from sheer joy (apo tēs charas) is Luke's most humanly comic scene in Acts. She recognizes Peter's voice before seeing him, races back to report, and leaves him knocking at the gate — a detail that the community apparently told for years. Luke's inclusion of this human particularity suggests that his sources included firsthand testimony from participants; it is precisely the kind of detail that survives in oral tradition because of its combination of joy and absurdity.
12.15: ho angelos autou estin ('it is his angel'): the community's explanation — that it must be Peter's guardian angel — reflects the first-century Jewish tradition of personal angels modeled on Dan 10:13-21 and the OT divine-messenger tradition. They prayed for Peter but do not immediately believe he has been delivered; they posit that the angel they associate with Peter has taken his form. Luke records the belief without endorsing or dismissing it; the narrative's irony is that the actual Peter is outside knocking while they debate his angelic substitute.
12.16: exestēsan ('they were astonished/beside themselves'): Luke's characteristic vocabulary for the response to miraculous divine action (2:7; 8:13; 9:21; 10:45). The astonishment of those who have been praying earnestly for Peter's release is Luke's quiet theological comment: prayer is offered in faith, but the faith of those praying is itself often smaller than the God to whom they pray. The community's astonishment is not a failure but a measure of the miracle's magnitude.
12.17: kataseisas autois tē cheiri sigān ('motioning to them with his hand to be quiet'): the oratorial gesture — right hand raised, palm forward — is the same as Paul's gesture in 13:16 and 21:40. It is the standard ancient rhetorical signal for attention and silence before speaking. Iakōbō kai tois adelphois ('to James and the brothers'): this is James the Lord's brother (Gal 1:19), who is emerging as the leader of the Jerusalem church. The instruction to report to him signals the authority structure Luke is tracking toward the Jerusalem Council of ch.15.
12.18: tarache ouk oligē en tois stratiōtais ('no small commotion among the soldiers'): Luke's characteristic litotes — 'not small' = considerable — understates the military crisis. Ti ara ho Petros egeneto ('what had become of Peter'): the question is unanswerable within the framework available to the soldiers; their prisoner is gone from a locked, guarded cell. The commotion is the mirror image of the community's prayer meeting — there, joy and astonishment; here, confusion and crisis.
12.19: anakrinas ('having cross-examined'): the legal term for thorough judicial interrogation. Herod's response to failing to find Peter is to execute the guards — standard Roman military discipline for soldiers who allowed a prisoner to escape (cf. 16:27, where the Philippian jailer assumes the same fate and considers suicide). The detail authenticates Luke's knowledge of Roman military practice. Katēlthen apo tēs Ioudaias eis Kaisareian: Herod's withdrawal to Caesarea sets up his fatal speech in vv.21-23.
12.20: thymomachōn ('extremely angry'): a hapax in the NT; the compound — thymos (passion/rage) + machomai (fight) — describes violent, passionate anger. Herod's quarrel with Tyre and Sidon is an economic dispute (they depended on Herod's territory for food imports, v.20b). The deputation's mission — securing audience through Blastus the chamberlain — uses the standard vocabulary of Hellenistic diplomacy. Luke's knowledge of this internal dynastic and commercial detail suggests he had access to Herodian court sources.
12.21: endysas esthēta basilikēn ('dressed in royal robes'): Josephus's parallel account (Antiquities 19.8.2) describes silver robes that gleamed brilliantly in the morning sun, causing the crowd's exclamation. Luke's account and Josephus's are independent corroborations of the same event, each preserving different details. Dēmēgorein ('delivered a public address') is a technical term for formal civic oratory; Herod's speech is a set-piece of Hellenistic royal self-presentation.
12.22: theou phōnē kai ouk anthrōpou ('the voice of a god, not of a man'): the crowd's acclamation is the precise form of blasphemy — attributing divine status to a mortal. Josephus adds that Herod saw an owl and took it as an omen of death (recalling an earlier omen), but Luke focuses on the theological issue: Herod accepted acclamation that should be given to God alone. The structural echo of Isa 14:13-15 ('I will make myself like the Most High') and Ezek 28:2 is the theological framework Luke invokes without citing.
12.23: parachrēma de epataxen auton angelos Kyriou, anth' hōn ouk edōken tēn doxan tō theō ('immediately an angel of the Lord struck him, because he did not give glory to God'): the causation is explicit — anth' hōn ('because') names the theological reason. skōlēkobrōtos genomenos ('becoming eaten by worms'): a distinctive vocabulary item (hapax in NT) that Josephus's account corroborates (violent intestinal pain, five-day illness, death at Caesarea). The divine judgment is immediate, precise, and publicly witnessed — the inverse of the community's miraculous deliverance in vv.7-11.
12.24: ho de logos tou theou ēuxanen kai eplēthyneto ('the word of God kept growing and multiplying'): the fourth of Luke's Acts growth-summaries (2:47; 4:4; 5:14; 6:7; 12:24). Luke places it immediately after Herod's death — the persecutor is gone; the word continues. The pairing of auxanō (grow) and plēthynō (multiply) echoes the language of Israel's growth in Egypt (Exod 1:7 LXX: auxanon kai plēthynomenon), framing the church's expansion as the continuation of God's people-multiplying work.
12.25: hypostrepsan ex Ierousalēm ('having returned from Jerusalem'): the reference to the relief mission of 11:29-30; Barnabas and Saul had completed the famine-relief delivery and now return to Antioch with John Mark. This transitional verse closes the Jerusalem section (chs.11-12) and opens the Antioch mission section (ch.13). Luke's structure moves the narrative's center of gravity from Jerusalem to Antioch, as the Gentile mission is about to begin.

11.1: 'The apostles and the believers throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God' (ton logon tou theou — the word of God; disp=3 for logos) — the 'word of God' as received-message is the standard Acts phrase for the gospel proclamation and its reception (Acts 6:7; 8:14; 13:5, 7, 44, 46, 48; 17:13). The news travels quickly: the entire Judean church hears about the Gentile reception. The 'also' (kai ta ethnē — even the Gentiles) captures the surprise: the word has crossed the ethnic boundary.
