11.2: 'The circumcised believers (hoi ek peritomēs — those of the circumcision) took issue with him' — oi ek peritomēs (those from circumcision) is the technical designation for Jewish believers who maintained circumcision as a boundary-marker; they appear again in Gal 2:12. 'Took issue with' (diekrinonto — were discriminating against/disputing with) uses the same root as diakrinō in 10:20 (do not discriminate/hesitate): the very people who make ethnic-distinctions dispute with Peter for not making them.
11.3: 'You went into the house of uncircumcised men and ate with them' — the charge focuses on table-fellowship rather than the baptism. In purity-law terms, entering the home of the uncircumcised and sharing a meal was the greater social/ritual transgression. Peter's defense (vv.4-17) will recount the vision, the Spirit's direct instruction, the Spirit's falling on Cornelius's household before baptism, and the disciples' testimony — a fourfold defense for a single act of table-fellowship.
11.4: 'Starting from the beginning, Peter explained everything to them in order (kathexēs exetitheto autois — he was setting it out in order for them)' — exetitheto (he was explaining/setting out, imperfect = continuous explanation) and kathexēs (in order, systematically — Luke's characteristic word for sequential narrative, Luke 1:3; Acts 18:23) describe a methodical defense. Peter does not abbreviate; he goes from the beginning, ensuring every element of the divine initiation is presented before the conclusion is drawn.
11.5: 'I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision' — Peter's first-person retelling repeats the key elements of the 10:9-16 account but from the perspective of testimony rather than narration. 'In a trance' (en ekstasei — in an ecstasy/standing-outside-oneself) — the same ekstasis as 10:10. Peter's identification of the visionary state is part of his defense: this was not a dream or an ordinary perception but a Spirit-given vision.
11.6: 'I looked into it and saw four-footed animals of the earth, wild beasts, reptiles and birds' — the expansion of the animal-list (tēs gēs ta tetrapoda kai ta thēria kai ta herpeta kai ta peteina tou ouranou — adding thēria, wild beasts) compared to the original account (10:12 — no thēria listed) may reflect a different tradition, or Peter's own recollection adding detail. Either way, the point is the same: all animals, clean and unclean together, presented without category-distinction.
11.7: 'I also heard a voice telling me, Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' — Peter's retelling uses the same command-words as 10:13 (Anastas, Petre, thuson kai phage) verbatim. The repetition of the divine command in testimony is the standard confirmatory structure: the same words God said, repeated exactly, constitute the divine evidence for what followed. The unchanged divine word is the unchanging divine will.
11.8: 'I replied, Surely not, Lord! Nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth' — Peter's protest is retold with a nuance: 10:14 said 'I have never eaten anything impure'; 11:8 says 'nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth' (eis to stoma mou — into my mouth). The 'into my mouth' addition may reflect the purity-law's emphasis on the oral reception of impurity (Lev 11 concludes with food entering the body). Peter's reformulation of his original refusal strengthens the case for what followed: his objection was total; God's response was decisive.
11.9: 'The voice spoke from heaven a second time, Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' — the heavenly voice (phōnē ek tou ouranou — voice from heaven, the bath qol equivalent) repeating the command emphasizes divine authority. 'A second time' (ek deuterou — from the second) — the full three repetitions of 10:15-16 are compressed here to the essential theological statement. What God has cleansed (ekatharistern — perfect tense: completed, permanent cleansing) must not be called common by any human authority.
11.10: 'This happened three times, and then it was all pulled back up to heaven' — the threefold repetition and withdrawal are stated concisely in testimony. Peter presents the triple-confirmation as the divine insistence that overrode his initial refusal. The withdrawal-to-heaven (anespasthē panta eis ton ouranon) finalizes the vision's message: what came from heaven has returned to heaven, having delivered its meaning.
11.11: 'Right then three men who had been sent to me from Caesarea stopped at the house where I was staying' — the 'right then' (kai idou — and behold!) is Luke's particle of providential coincidence: the three-men's arrival precisely as the vision ends is the divine punctuation that interprets the vision. The Cornelius-connection (men from Caesarea) becomes the vision's interpretation: the sheet's animals = the uncircumcised Cornelius and his household.
11.12: 'The Spirit told me to have no hesitation about going with them (mēden diakrinanta — discriminating nothing)' — the participial mēden diakrinanta (making no distinction) extends and clarifies the 10:20 command: the Spirit explicitly named the ethnic-distinction impulse as what must be overridden. 'These six brothers also went with me, and we entered the man's house' — the six witnesses (hexakis adelphoi — six brothers; Acts 10:23 says 'some of the believers from Joppa') provide the Deuteronomic witness-number for the defense.
11.13: 'He told us how he had seen an angel appear in his house and say, Send to Joppa for Simon who is called Peter' — the angel's instruction to Cornelius, reported by Cornelius, reported now by Peter to Jerusalem, constitutes a triple-verified divine initiation: the angel told Cornelius, Cornelius told the messengers, the messengers told Peter, Peter now tells Jerusalem. Every link in the chain independently confirms the divine origin of the Cornelius encounter.
11.14: 'He will bring you a message (rhēmata — words/things said) through which you and all your household will be saved' — rhēmata (specific spoken words, things said) rather than logos (word/message generally) — the angel specifies the form of salvation: through words spoken by Peter. The 'and all your household' anticipates the household-salvation pattern: Cornelius's salvation extends to the entire social unit under his headship.
11.15: 'As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit came on them as he had come on us at the beginning' — 'as I began to speak' (en tō arxasthai me lalein — in my beginning to speak) emphasizes the Spirit's interruption of the sermon: before Peter finished, the Spirit fell. The en archē (at the beginning) parallel — 'as he had come on us at the beginning' — identifies the Cornelius event as a second Pentecost. Pneuma hagiou (Holy Spirit, disp=3 and disp=2 for hagios) — both terms capitalized in the MKT.
11.16: 'Then I remembered what the Lord had said: John baptised with water, but you will be baptised with the Holy Spirit' — the verb emnēsthēn (I remembered) marks the moment of pneumatological recognition: Peter interprets what he is witnessing through Jesus's words. The Lord's prophetic word (rhēmatos — specific spoken word) becomes the interpretive key for understanding the Spirit's falling on Gentiles. Both 'Holy Spirit' instances (pneumatos hagiou, disp=3 and 2) are capitalized in the MKT.
11.17: 'So if God gave them the same gift he gave us, who believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to think that I could stand in God's way?' — the 'same gift' (tēn isēn dōrean — the equal/identical gift) asserts the qualitative equivalence of the Cornelius Pentecost with the Acts 2 Pentecost. 'Who was I to think that I could oppose God' (egō tis ēmēn dynatos kōlysai ton theon — I who was I able to prevent God?) — kōlyō (prevent) echoes the baptism-prevention question of 10:47: no one can withhold baptism from those whom God has already baptised with the Spirit.
11.18: 'When they heard this, they had no further objections and praised God, saying, So then, even to Gentiles God has granted repentance that leads to life' — hēsychasan (they became quiet, ceased objecting) is the silence of conviction before the evidence. 'Life' (zōēn — life, disp=2) in 'repentance unto life' (metanoian eis zōēn) is eschatological life — the same zōē used for eternal life throughout John's Gospel. The Jerusalem church's doxology names the theological conclusion: God has granted the Gentiles repentance (the gift of being able to turn) and life (the result of turning).
11.19: 'Those who had been scattered by the persecution that broke out when Stephen was killed traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus and Antioch, spreading the word (ton logon — the word; disp=3) only among Jews' — the Stephen-persecution scattering (8:1 — 'they were all scattered') continues to produce mission. The scattered ones travel the Mediterranean coastal route. 'Spreading the word only among Jews' (mēdeni lalountes ton logon ei mē monon Ioudaiois — speaking the word to no one except Jews only) — the ethnic restriction of the initial mission is the baseline from which the next verse departs.
11.20: 'However, some of them... began to speak to Greeks also, proclaiming the good news about the Lord Jesus' — the 'however' (de — but) introduces the innovative deviation: some men from Cyprus and Cyrene spoke to Hellenistai (Greek-speakers, likely Gentile Greeks rather than Greek-speaking Jews). The transition from Jewish-only to Greek-inclusive proclamation happens not through a Jerusalem decision but through unnamed Cypriot and Cyrenian missionaries following the Spirit's impulse. The Cornelius episode has cleared the theological air; these missionaries breathe it.
11.21: 'The Lord's hand was with them, and a great number of people believed and turned to the Lord' — 'the Lord's hand' (cheir Kyriou — the hand of the Lord) is the OT power-formula for divine intervention (Exod 9:3; 1 Sam 5:6; Isa 59:1; Ezek 1:3). Luke uses the OT power-language for the Antioch mission: the same divine hand that accomplished the exodus and the prophets' ministry now accompanies the Gentile-inclusive gospel-mission. Epistrepsas (turned/turned back) — the conversion-verb used for Israel's return to YHWH in the prophets (Hos 6:1; Zech 1:3-4).
11.22: 'News of this reached the church at Jerusalem (ēkousthē ho logos — the word/report was heard) and they sent Barnabas to Antioch' — logos (disp=3) here is the 'report/news' sense, not the gospel-proclamation sense: the Jerusalem church heard the news-word about Antioch. They sent Barnabas (the 'Son of Encouragement,' 4:36) — the choice of Barnabas, himself a Cypriot diaspora Jew (4:36), to investigate the Cypriot-Cyrenian mission to Gentile Greeks is providentially apt.
11.23: 'When he arrived and saw what the grace of God had done (tēn charin tēn tou theou — the grace of God), he was glad and encouraged them all to remain true to the Lord with all their hearts' — the 'grace of God' is both what Barnabas observes and what he names: he sees the changed lives and calls them grace. 'With all their hearts' (tē prothesei tēs kardias — the purpose/intention of the heart) — prothesis (purpose, resolve, intention) indicates deliberate commitment rather than emotional response: the kind of covenant-faithfulness that the heart-circumcision language of Acts 7:51 points toward.
11.24: 'He was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a great number of people were brought to the Lord' — Luke's character-summary of Barnabas (agapethos anthropos — a good man) uses the same vocabulary applied to Cornelius (agathos kai dikaios — good and righteous in secular Greek, though not exactly in this text). 'Full of the Holy Spirit and faith' — the double fullness (Spirit and faith) is the apostolic qualification formula (Stephen 6:5; 7:55). The great-number result (prosetethe ochlos hikanos — a sufficient crowd was added) is the standard Lukan response to Spirit-filled ministry.
11.25: 'Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul' — Barnabas's initiative in searching for Saul (anazētein Saulon — to search up/out Saul) recalls that Barnabas was the one who first vouched for the converted Saul to the Jerusalem church (9:27). Barnabas recognizes that Antioch's Gentile-inclusive mission needs Paul's particular gifting: the apostle to the Gentiles (Gal 1:16; Rom 11:13) for the church that is learning to be Gentile-inclusive. The Spirit-directed appointment of the right person for the right mission is a Lukan pattern.
11.26: 'And when he found him, he brought him to Antioch. So for a whole year Barnabas and Saul met with the church and taught great numbers of people' — the year-long intensive teaching ministry (eniautos holon — an entire year) in Antioch is the first concentrated church-planting effort in a predominantly Gentile context. 'The disciples were called Christians (Christianous — ones of Christ) first at Antioch' — Christianos (a Latin-style formation: one belonging to Christus) is either a name given by outsiders or adopted by the community. The suffix -ianos (-ian) is used for the partisans of a political leader or household; 'Christians' = those of the household of Christ.
11.27: 'During this time some prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch' — the movement of prophets (prophētai) from Jerusalem to Antioch is the inverse of the typical pilgrimage direction: Jerusalem's spiritual resources flow toward the new Gentile center. Acts consistently shows the charisms of the Jerusalem community (prophets, apostles) traveling to confirm and strengthen the mission churches that the Spirit is planting.
11.28: 'Agabus stood up and through the Spirit predicted (esēmanen dia tou pneumatos — he indicated/signified through the Spirit) that a severe famine would spread over the entire Roman world' — sēmainō (to signify, indicate) is John's vocabulary for prophetic sign-speech (John 12:33; 18:32; 21:19; Rev 1:1). Agabus's prophecy is a sēmeion-speech: not merely information but a divine sign-communication. 'Through the Spirit' (dia tou pneumatos) is the authentic-prophetic formula — his word comes through the Spirit's mediation, not his own insight.
11.29: 'The disciples, as each one was able, decided to provide help for the brothers and sisters living in Judea' — kathōs euporeito tis (as each one prospered/was able) is the voluntary, ability-proportioned giving that the Corinthians will be instructed in (1 Cor 16:2 — 'each of you should put aside a sum in keeping with his income'). The Antioch collection for Judean famine-relief is the first instance of what will become the characteristic inter-church collection Paul organizes throughout his ministry (1 Cor 16:1-4; 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28).
11.30: 'This they did, sending their gift to the elders by Barnabas and Saul' — the 'elders' (presbyterous — the same term used for the Ephesian elders in 20:17) receive the collection for distribution. This is the first mention of 'elders' as a leadership structure in the Jerusalem church; the term brings Jewish synagogue-governance into the church's administrative vocabulary. Barnabas and Saul carry the collection: the relief mission to Jerusalem will end with Paul's arrest (21:27-36), making this first Jerusalem visit a foretaste of Paul's final Jerusalem visit.

14.1: kata to auto (kata to auto, 'in the same way/together'): the phrase connects to Acts 2:1's homothymadon epi to auto — the mission pattern is consistent: synagogue-first, combined community, word and signs. hōste pisteuēsai ('so that they believed'): the immediate result clause; the word was so effective that faith was the natural outcome. Iconium's divided response (v.4) is the standard pattern of Acts proclamation.
14.2: hoi de apeithountes Ioudaioi ('the unbelieving Jews'): apeitheo (disobey, refuse to believe) is the Acts term for willful rejection rather than ignorance; the same root as peithō (persuade) with the alpha privative. ekakōsan (embittered, stirred up against): the verb means to harm or embitter — a medical term for the effect of a toxic substance. The pattern of Jewish instigation against Gentile sympathizers recurs at Thessalonica (17:5) and Beroea (17:13).
14.3: hikapon chronon ('considerable time'): the duration signals genuine community formation, not hit-and-run preaching. parrēsiazomenoi (speaking boldly): the Acts boldness-word for Spirit-empowered testimony. epimartyrounti tō logō tēs charitos autou didōnti sēmeia kai terata ('bearing witness to the word of his grace by granting signs and wonders'): the Lord is the subject who confirms his own word — the apostles are instruments, not the confirming agents themselves.
14.4: eskhisthē de to plēthos tēs poleōs ('the population of the city was divided'): schizō (split, divide) is used for divided crowds at Jesus's ministry (John 7:43; 9:16; 10:19) and becomes Acts' diagnostic for gospel impact — the word does not pass through neutrally but divides. The division tracks the existing social fault-lines: those aligned with the synagogue opposition vs. those aligned with the apostles.
14.5: hormē (hormē, 'rush, assault, violent impulse'): the word describes the surge of a mob — the same word used for the Gadarene pig-rush (Mark 5:13) and Acts 19:29. hybrisai kai lithobolēsai (to mistreat and stone): the infinitives describe the planned outcome; they fled before execution, not after. The Gentile-Jewish coalition against the apostles shows that opposition to the gospel unites otherwise antagonistic social groups.
14.6: synidontes katephygon eis tas poleis tēs Lykaonias ('having become aware, they fled to the cities of Lycaonia'): synidon (become aware together) is the apostles' situational intelligence. Lystra and Derbe are the next mission stations — the flight is also the mission's advance. Luke names the surrounding region (Lykaonian) to orient his geographically-aware reader; Lycaonia was a distinct linguistic and cultural region in the Anatolian plateau.
14.7: kakeipheudangelizomenoi ēsan ('and there too they were preaching the gospel'): the imperfect tense marks the ongoing character of the proclamation — not a single event but a continuous ministry in the surrounding countryside. Luke's one-verse summary of the Derbe/Lystra initial mission telescopes what must have been weeks of work; the healing episode (vv.8-18) is the signature event that both advances and complicates the mission.
14.8: achri adynatos en tois posin autou ('lame in his feet'): Luke's description echoes the Lydda healing of Aeneas (9:33) and the Jerusalem temple healing (3:2); the pattern of Acts healing narratives uses congenital disability (ek koilias mētros autou, from his mother's womb) to exclude natural recovery. The lame man sitting (ekathēto) is the posture of one who has never known upright movement.
14.9: houtos ēkousen tou Paulou lalountos; hos atenisas autō kai idōn hoti echei pistin tou sōthēnai ('he heard Paul speaking; Paul looked at him and saw that he had faith to be healed'): the faith (pistis tou sōthēnai, faith to be saved/healed) is what Paul sees — the same perceptual verb as Peter's gaze at the Jerusalem lame man (3:4). Sōthēnai carries both healing and salvation in a single word.
14.10: eipen megalē phōnē ('said in a loud voice'): the loud voice is the public, authoritative mode of command — not a whispered prayer but a herald's declaration. anastēthi epi tous podas sou orthon ('stand upright on your feet'): the triple emphatic command (stand/arise + on your feet + straight) leaves no ambiguity. The man's hēlato kai periepatei (he leaped up and walked) mirrors the Jerusalem man's leaping (3:8) — the Diaspora mission reproduces the Jerusalem signs.
14.11: Lykaonikē phōnē (in the Lycaonian language): Luke's notation that the crowd shouted in their regional dialect signals that the apostles did not immediately understand the worship-danger they were in — they couldn't comprehend the Lycaonian. The misidentification (Barnabas as Zeus, Paul as Hermes) reflects the local religious framework; the missionary who speaks effectively in a foreign tongue may still be heard through a foreign interpretive filter.
14.12: Barnaban men ekaloun Dia, Paulon de Hermeian ('they called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes'): Zeus is the chief god, Hermes his messenger-herald. Barnabas is assigned the senior deity role (suggesting he cut a more imposing physical presence, or was older); Paul's assignment to Hermes is ironic — the god of eloquence and divine communication is precisely what Paul has been exercising. The local identification shows the Gentile audience mapping the apostles onto their existing divine category.
14.13: ho de hiereus tou Dios tou ontos pro tēs poleōs ('the priest of Zeus whose temple was in front of the city'): Zeus Propolis (Zeus of the city-gate) was the patron deity of Lystra; the priest brings the official civic religious response. steimmata (wreaths/garlands): the sacrificial animals and garlands were the full preparation for a public divine welcome-sacrifice. The civic officials are now participating — not a fringe crowd but an official religious act.
14.14: diarrexantes ta himatia autōn ('tearing their garments'): the gesture of tearing clothes is the OT sign of extreme horror at blasphemy (2 Kgs 18:37; Matt 26:65) — the apostles react to being treated as gods with the same gesture the high priest used at Jesus's supposed blasphemy. The irony is sharp: the ones being worshipped respond as if they had heard the worst possible sacrilege.
14.15: hēmeis homoiopatheis esmen hymin anthrōpoi ('we too are human beings with the same nature as you'): homoiopatheis (of like passions, same nature) appears only here and Jas 5:17 (Elijah as homoiopathēs). The word insists on full human solidarity — not semi-divine messengers but fellow mortals. The content of the good news is specified: epistrepheite apo toutōn tōn mataōn epi theon zōnta (turn from these empty things to the living God) — the first explicitly Gentile evangelism without OT scaffold.
14.16: en tais paroichoumenais geneais ('in past generations'): the temporal marker acknowledges a real history of Gentile ignorance that God permitted — not approved but allowed. eiasen panta ta ethnē poreuēsthai tais hodois autōn ('he allowed all nations to go their own ways'): the contrast with Israel (to whom God gave his word and law) is implied; the Gentile nations were not abandoned but left to their own religious systems for a defined period now ending. Paul's Areopagus speech (17:30) develops the same theme.
14.17: agathourgōn (agathourgōn, 'doing good'): a hapax in the NT, combining agathos (good) and ergon (work). God's providential goodness to the nations — rain, harvests, gladness — was itself a testimony that should have directed them toward the source. empiplan trophēs kai euphrosynēs (filling with food and gladness): the creation-goodness is God's ongoing witness to his character; natural revelation is real if insufficient. The argument from providence is the philosophical entry-point Paul uses with philosophically-formed audiences.
14.18: molis katepausan tous ochlous tou mē thyein autois ('they barely restrained the crowds from sacrificing to them'): molis (barely, with difficulty) intensifies the scene — the religious enthusiasm of the crowd was so strong that it took sustained verbal effort to prevent the sacrifice. Luke's detail implies the apostles' dismay was complete; the contrast with the response they sought (repentance from idols) and the response they got (divine honors) is Acts' sharpest missiological irony.
14.19: epelithon lithois ton Paulon eseisan te exō tēs poleōs nomisantes auton tethnanai ('they stoned Paul and dragged him outside the city, thinking he was dead'): the same crowd that barely couldn't worship Paul now stones him. The speed of reversal mirrors Lystra's fickle religious energy; the mob that was preparing sacrifices becomes the mob that prepares an execution. Paul's own catalog of sufferings (2 Cor 11:25: 'once I was stoned') certifies this event's historicity.
14.20: anastas eisēlthen eis tēn polin ('rising up he went back into the city'): anastas (having risen/gotten up) — the same verb as resurrection proclamation; Luke does not attribute a miracle here, but the reader familiar with the resurrection vocabulary registers the resonance. Whether the stoning was near-lethal or (miraculously) survived, Paul's return to the city the same day it tried to kill him is an act of apostolic courage that confirms the commission of 9:16.
14.21: euangelisamenoi tēn polin ekeinēn kai mathēteusantes hikanous ('having preached the gospel in that city and made many disciples'): mathēteuō (make disciples) is the Great Commission verb (Matt 28:19); it appears here and in Acts 14:21 as the goal of mission — not conversions counted but disciples formed. The return route (Lystra → Iconium → Antioch) deliberately retraces the dangerous path, visiting the communities that formed in the mission's wake.
14.22: dei hēmas dia pollōn thlipseōn eiselthein eis tēn basileian tou theou ('we must through many tribulations enter the kingdom of God'): dei (it is necessary) applied to the suffering-path into the kingdom is Paul's compressed theology of the cross-shaped mission. Thlipsis (tribulation, pressure) is not incidental to the Christian life but the appointed road to the kingdom — the same dei that governed Jesus's passion (Luke 9:22) governs his apostles' path.
14.23: cheirotonesantes de autois kat' ekklēsian presbyterous ('having appointed elders for them in each church'): cheirotoneō (originally to vote by raising the hand, then to appoint) is the ordination verb; it appears for Paul and Barnabas's mission-sending (13:3 uses different vocabulary) but here for their appointment of local leadership. The pattern is: preach → make disciples → appoint elders → commit to the Lord. The kat' ekklēsian (church by church) indicates each local community receives its own eldership.
14.24: dielthontes tēn Pisidian ēlthon eis tēn Pamphylian ('passing through Pisidia they came into Pamphylia'): Luke's route-tracking is geographical specificity that serves narrative and historical purposes simultaneously — the reader can trace the mission on a map. Pisidia and Pamphylia are successive Roman administrative zones; the journey demonstrates the mission's systematic coverage of the Anatolian interior before returning to base.
14.25: lalesantes en Pergē ton logon ('having spoken the word in Perga'): Perga is where Mark deserted the mission (13:13); now the apostles preach there on the return — completing what they skipped before. Luke's brevity (lalesantes, having spoken) contrasts with the extended Pisidian Antioch narrative; the return journey consolidates what the outward journey inaugurated without repeating detailed speech-accounts.
14.26: hothen ēsan paradedomenoi tē chariti tou theou ('where they had been committed to the grace of God'): the Antioch church's sending-prayer committed Paul and Barnabas to the grace of God (not to their own resources); the mission's completion is also its accountability-return. eis to ergon ho eplērōsan ('for the work that they had completed'): plēroō (fulfill, complete) frames the entire first missionary journey as a divinely assigned task brought to completion.
14.27: anēngeilan hosa epoiēsen ho theos met' autōn ('they reported what God had done with them'): the meta-report is theocentric — God did these things with them, not merely through them; the preposition meta suggests partnership rather than pure instrumentalism. hoti ēnoixen tois ethnesin thyran pisteōs ('that he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles'): thyra pisteōs (door of faith) — Paul uses the open-door metaphor elsewhere (1 Cor 16:9; 2 Cor 2:12; Col 4:3) for gospel-opportunity.
14.28: dietribon de chronon ouk oligon syn tois mathētais ('they spent not a little time with the disciples'): ouk oligon (not a little = considerable) is Lukan understatement for what was likely several months. The Antioch home base provides the breathing space between missions; the community life with disciples is itself the mission's fruit — not just individuals converted but a community formed. The Jerusalem council crisis (ch.15) interrupts this interlude.

16.1: mathētēs tis ēn ekei onomati Timotheos ('a certain disciple was there named Timothy'): Luke's first introduction of Timothy. Gynaikos Ioudaias pistēs ('son of a Jewish woman who was a believer') — Timothy's mixed parentage (Jewish mother, Greek father) creates the legal and pastoral problem of v.3. In Jewish law, Jewish identity follows the mother; Timothy was therefore technically Jewish but uncircumcised. He will become Paul's most significant co-worker (Phil 2:19-22; 1 Cor 4:17) and the addressee of two NT letters.
16.2: emartyreito hypo tōn en Lystrois kai Ikonion adelphōn ('well-attested by the brothers at Lystra and Iconium'): the same martyreō vocabulary used for witness and testimony throughout Acts now describes Timothy's community reputation. The practice of commendation across churches — a written or oral recommendation from the home community — will become formalized in Paul's letters (2 Cor 3:1-3; Rom 16:1-2). Timothy's acceptability to the mission depends on the established community's verification.
16.3: perietemen auton dia tous Ioudaious ('he circumcised him because of the Jews'): Paul's circumcision of Timothy is not a theological contradiction of Galatians 2:3-5 (where Titus, a Gentile, was not compelled to be circumcised) but a pragmatic pastoral act. Timothy's mother was Jewish, making him legally Jewish; his uncircumcision was a social-religious disability for synagogue ministry. The circumcision facilitates access to the synagogue, not salvation. Paul's principle was 'to the Jews, I became as a Jew' (1 Cor 9:20).
16.4: ta dogmata ta kekriminena hypo tōn apostolōn kai presbyterōn ('the decisions reached by the apostles and elders'): the Jerusalem Council's rulings (15:29) are here transmitted in written form (paradidōsin phylassein, 'delivered for them to keep') to the Gentile churches. Dogma (decree/decision) in Acts is used for both Roman imperial decrees (17:7) and the apostolic council's ruling — a subtle leveling: the church's apostolic decisions carry the same force as imperial edicts, but from a higher authority.
16.5: hai men oun ekklēsiai estereunto tē pistei kai eperisseuon tō arithmō kath' hēmeran: another growth summary (cf. 2:47; 4:4; 6:7; 12:24; 13:49). Steredō (strengthened/consolidated) is the same root as the healing of the lame man in 3:7 — a community being made firm in faith. The delivery of the apostolic decrees produces consolidation, not fragmentation; the Council's rulings built up rather than divided.
16.6: kōlythentes hypo tou Hagiou Pneumatos lalēsai ton logon en tē Asia ('having been prevented by the Holy Spirit from speaking the word in Asia'): the Spirit's prohibition of a seemingly strategic mission field (the province of Asia, whose capital Ephesus would later become Paul's major base) illustrates Acts' theology of divine mission direction. The Spirit does not merely guide positively; it also prohibits. Asia Minor will receive the gospel extensively later (19:10); the Spirit's timing, not Paul's initiative, governs the sequence.
16.7: to pneuma Iēsou ouk eiasen autous ('the Spirit of Jesus did not allow them'): the only occurrence in Acts of 'the Spirit of Jesus' — Luke's most Christologically explicit pneumatology. The Spirit who guides the mission is identified directly with the risen Jesus; the post-Pentecost Spirit is Jesus's own presence and agency in the community (cf. John 14:16-18; 2 Cor 3:17). Paul's letters use 'Spirit of Christ' (Rom 8:9) and 'Spirit of Jesus Christ' (Phil 1:19); Luke here uses the precise christological form.
16.8: katabantes eis Trōada ('having gone down to Troas'): the passive drift westward — prevented from going east into Asia (v.6), north into Bithynia (v.7), now at the Aegean port — positions Paul at the westernmost accessible point. The geography is providentially constructed: the Spirit closes every door except the one facing Europe. Troas was the ancient site of Troy; its association with East-West exchange makes it the fitting threshold for the European mission.
16.9: horama dia tēs nyktos ('a vision through the night'): Acts' directional visions occur at transition points in the mission (9:10-12; 10:3, 10-16; 16:9; 18:9; 22:17-21; 23:11). Anēr Makedōn tis ēn ('a certain Macedonian man'): the figure's identity is unspecified — whether a person in need, a symbolic figure, or an angelic messenger is left open. The appeal — diabas eis Makedonian boēthēson hēmin ('come over to Macedonia and help us') — is the mission's commissioning for Europe.
16.10: eutheos ezētēsamen exelthein eis Makedonian ('we immediately sought to go to Macedonia'): the first-person plural 'we' marks the beginning of Luke's eyewitness travel narrative. Luke, the author, is apparently at Troas and joins the party here. Symbibasontes hoti proskeklētai hēmas ho theos euangelisasthai autous ('concluding that God had called us to proclaim the good news to them'): the mission team's theological interpretation of the vision — they reason that the vision is divine calling, not merely natural dreaming. Their conclusion-language (symbibazō) reflects deliberate discernment.
16.11: euthudromēsamen ('we ran a straight course'): nautical vocabulary for sailing directly downwind — the ancient equivalent of a fast, favorable passage. The detail (two-day voyage mentioned by implication) is characteristic of the 'we' passages' travel specificity; contrast the lengthier indirect journey in the reverse direction (20:6 — five days instead of two). The favorable wind of v.11 will become an unfavorable passage on the return; the difference in journey time marks the providential opening of the European mission.
16.12: Philippoi, hētis estin prōtē tēs merides Makedonias polis, kolōnia ('Philippi, which is the leading city of that district of Macedonia, a Roman colony'): Philippi's status as a kolōnia (Roman colony) is significant. Roman colonies were settlements of Roman citizens, governed by Roman law, with the rights and obligations of Roman citizens. They replicated Rome in miniature in the provinces. The legal and social dynamics of a colony — Roman citizenship, magistrates with full civic authority, privilege of Roman customs — will drive the narrative of vv.19-40.
16.13: proseuche ('a place of prayer'): Jewish communities that lacked the ten adult males required to constitute a synagogue used outdoor prayer places, often near water for ritual washing. Luke's note that the missionaries 'expected to find' (enomizomen proseuchen einai) such a place shows their strategic intelligence about diaspora Jewish practice. Kathisantes elalomen tais synelthousais gynaixi ('sitting down we spoke to the women who had gathered') — the initial audience at Philippi is an exclusively female gathering.
16.14: Lydia onomati, porphyropōlis ('a woman named Lydia, a dealer in purple'): purple dye and purple-dyed cloth were luxury goods associated with wealth and power (purple = imperial color). Lydia's trade connected her to the aristocracy and the imperial system; she is economically significant, not a marginal figure. Sebomenē ton theon ('a worshipper of God') identifies her as a Gentile God-fearer. diēnoixen ho kyrios tēn kardian ('the Lord opened her heart'): divine causation of receptivity — faith is a gift of opened perception, not merely human decision.
16.15: ei kekrikate me pistēn tō kyriō einai, eiselthontes eis ton oikon mou menete ('if you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house and stay'): Lydia's invitation is conditional on her hosts' assessment of her — not false humility but the social form of ancient hospitality. Her home becomes the base for the Philippian mission and the meeting place of the church (v.40). The first European church is hosted by a businesswoman; Luke's narrative attention to women throughout Acts-Luke is consistent and intentional.
16.16: pneuma python ('a python spirit'): in Greek religion, the Python was the serpent at Delphi through whom Apollo spoke via the oracle (Pythia). The term python identified divination spirits; the slave-girl's owners exploited her for financial gain. Her spirit's theological accuracy — 'servants of the Most High God, who tell you the way of salvation' — is ironic: a demonic spirit advertises the truth it serves to undermine. Paul's eventual exorcism removes the truth-proclamation the spirit was making, which provokes the commercial backlash of v.19.
16.17: houtoi hoi anthrōpoi douloi tou theou tou hypsistou eisin ('these men are servants of the Most High God'): the demon's acclamation identifies both Paul's mission (douloi, servants) and its source (theou tou hypsistou, God Most High). Theos hypsistos was a title used across the Greco-Roman world for a supreme deity — by Jews for YHWH, and by Gentiles for Zeus or a syncretistic supreme god. The slave-girl's cry could be heard in multiple registers; her proclamation is accurate but misleading about which 'Most High God' is meant.
16.18: paraggellō soi en onomati Iēsou Christou exelthein ('I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out'): the exorcism formula — authority invested in the name — is the consistent pattern of apostolic deliverance in Acts (3:6; 4:10; 16:18). Paul's eventual annoyance (diaponētheis, 'being thoroughly troubled/worn down') after 'many days' is psychologically realistic; the spirit's harassment was sustained, not instantaneous. The exorcism succeeds that very hour (tē autē hora) — Luke's precision on the immediacy of the Spirit's power.
16.19: idōntes hoi kyrioi autēs hoti exēlthen hē elpis tēs ergasias autōn ('her owners saw that their hope of profit was gone'): the economic motivation is named without apology — the exorcism's social consequence is financial loss. Epilabomenoi ton Paulon kai ton Silan ('seizing Paul and Silas'): the economic grievance produces the physical assault. Acts repeatedly shows that the gospel's disruption of exploitative economic systems (idolatry, divination, magic) generates the most violent responses. The persecution here is not religious but commercial.
16.20: Ioudaioi hyparchontes ('being Jews'): the charge before the magistrates deploys ethnic hostility as its rhetorical weapon. In the Roman world, being Jewish was not illegal, but promoting 'customs unlawful for Romans' was. The accusers weaponize Paul's Jewish identity to trigger Roman civic anxiety. Luke's recording of this charge documents the social dynamics of early Roman anti-Judaism and shows how the gospel mission navigated ethnic suspicion.
16.21: ethē ha ouk exestin hēmin paradechesthai oud' poiein Rhōmaiois ousin ('customs which it is not lawful for us who are Romans to accept or practice'): the charge is technically about illegal foreign religious promotion, but its real content is economic grievance dressed in civic rhetoric. Roman law protected traditional religious practices but was suspicious of new Oriental cults that disrupted the social order. The magistrates (strategoi, the two ruling magistrates of a Roman colony) respond to the crowd's excitement, not to a carefully weighed legal case.
16.22: rhabdouchoi ('lictors/rod-bearers'): the magistrates' officers who carried the fasces (bundle of rods symbolizing Roman authority to punish). The public flogging without trial (perirrēxantes autōn ta himatia, stripping off their garments; ekeleuon rhabdizein, ordered them beaten with rods) was the Roman punishment fustigatio. That this happened to Roman citizens without due process is the legal violation Paul will invoke in v.37.
16.23: pollas epithenthes autois plēgas ('inflicting many blows upon them'): Luke's understated vocabulary for a severe public beating. The subsequent imprisonment — desmophylaki (the prison) with orders for maximum security (v.24) — treats the missionaries as dangerous criminals requiring the most stringent custody. Paul will later summarize this experience as one of five Roman beatings received (2 Cor 11:23-25), placing it in the context of his catalog of apostolic suffering.
16.24: esō phylakēn ('inner cell') + ta xyla ('the stocks'): the inner cell was the most secure part of the prison, typically underground. The stocks were a wooden restraint locking the feet apart and preventing any movement — a device of immobilization and humiliation as much as security. The extreme security measures set up the earthquake miracle's dramatic contrast: maximum human restraint, effortlessly overturned by divine action.
16.25: kata de to mesonyktion Paulos kai Silas proseuchomenoi humnoun ton theon ('about midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God'): hymnousin — the only occurrence of this verb in Acts — describes liturgical song. The midnight prayer-and-song in a Roman prison cell has been read against the background of the Psalter (Ps 119:62, 'At midnight I rise to give you thanks'), the prophetic tradition (Job 35:10, 'songs in the night'), and the Pauline instruction to sing (Eph 5:19; Col 3:16). The other prisoners' listening (epēkroōnto) is noted without further comment — their receptivity has been prepared by the missionaries' public testimony.
16.26: seismos megas ('a great earthquake') + eutheos de aneōchthēsan pasai ai thyrai ('immediately all the doors were opened'): the seismic theophany echoes the Sinai tradition (Exod 19:18; Ps 68:8) and the Pentecost wind (Acts 2:2). The earthquake's simultaneous effect on all doors and all chains is physically impossible in normal terms — not a coincidental earthquake but a directed divine intervention. Luke does not distinguish between natural earthquake and supernatural liberation; both are the same God acting.
16.27: mellōn heauton anairein ('about to kill himself'): the jailer's response to apparently empty cells is conditioned by Roman military law — a soldier who allowed a prisoner to escape faced the same punishment as the prisoner. Suicide was preferable to the degradation of execution for dereliction. Paul's cry from inside (mēden praxēs seautō kakon, 'do yourself no harm') stops him before he can act — Paul can see in the darkness what the jailer cannot. The scene's dramatic irony is that the prisoners' voluntary presence saves the jailer's life before he knows it.
16.28: Paul's shout — phone megalē (a loud voice) — penetrates the dark: 'Don't harm yourself! We are all here!' The unexpected words carry double weight: the prisoners have not fled (a miracle in itself given open doors) and the jailer's life is preserved. Luke does not explain why the prisoners did not flee; Paul's presence and presumably authority kept them. The scene reverses the normal prison dynamic: the prisoner saves the jailer.
16.29: aitēsas phōta eisepēdēsen ('having called for lights, he rushed in'): the jailer's sequence — requesting lights, rushing in, falling trembling before Paul and Silas (entromos genomenos prosepesen tō Paulō kai tō Silā) — shows progressive recognition of divine presence. The trembling (entromos) is the biblical response to theophany (Dan 10:11; Rev 1:17). He does not yet know who these men are beyond their miraculous non-escape; his trembling is the beginning of the awe that will issue in conversion.
16.30: kyrioi, ti me dei poiein hina sōthō ('Sirs, what must I do to be saved?'): the most direct articulation of the soteriological question in Acts. Dei (it is necessary) carries the weight of genuine urgency. Kyrioi ('sirs/lords') addresses Paul and Silas with respect; the word will be echoed in the answer's 'Lord Jesus' (v.31). The jailer asks as an individual; the answer encompasses his household — salvation's scope is simultaneously personal and communal.
16.31: pisteuson epi ton kyrion Iēsoun, kai sōthēsē sy kai ho oikos sou ('believe on the Lord Jesus and you will be saved — you and your household'): the Philippian kerygma in its most compressed form. Pisteuson epi (believe on/in) is trust directed toward a person, not assent to propositions. Ho oikos sou ('your household') extends the salvation promise to the household; the ancient household was a corporate social unit, and faith professed by the head opened the household to instruction and conversion (v.33-34). This is the basis of infant-baptism arguments and household-solidarity theology.
16.32: elalēsan autō ton logon tou kyriou syn pasin tois en tē oikia autou ('they spoke the word of the Lord to him and to all those in his household'): the conversion process is not instantaneous — it involves instruction (elalēsan, speaking/teaching). The word is delivered to the entire household, establishing that the conversion is both individual (the jailer's personal question) and communal (the household's instruction). Faith and baptism follow teaching; the Philippian church is founded on adequate instruction, not crisis-decision alone.
16.33: kai elou en tē nykti apo tōn plēgōn autōn ('he washed their wounds at that hour of the night'): the jailer washes their wounds — the very wounds inflicted by the city's civic machinery that his imprisonment was enforcing. His care for Paul and Silas is the concrete act that demonstrates the genuineness of his conversion before his baptism. Ebaptisthē autos kai hoi autou apantes parachrēma ('he and all his people were baptized immediately'): the immediacy of baptism in Acts reflects the apostolic practice of baptizing upon profession of faith, not after a lengthy catechesis.
16.34: hēgalliastō pepisteukas tō theō ('he was filled with joy because he had come to believe in God'): the joy of new faith is a Lukan signature (Luke 2:10; 15:7, 10, 23-24; Acts 8:8, 39; 13:52; 16:34). The jailer's joy is explicitly connected to his belief: pepisteukas is the perfect tense — having believed with completed action in the present. The midnight scene ends with a meal hosted by the jailer for his prisoners — the table fellowship that marks conversion throughout Luke-Acts (Luke 5:29-32; 15:23; 19:6).
16.35: hēmeras de genomenēs ('when day came'): the narrative resumes in daylight, shifting from the intimate midnight scene of conversion to the civic-legal setting of the missionaries' release. Apelysan tous anthrōpous ekeinous ('release those men'): the magistrates' order uses the bare pronoun — 'those men' — without acknowledgment of the night's events. Their pragmatic order sets up Paul's challenge in v.37.
16.36: exelthe nyn kai poreuesthe en eirēnē ('go now and leave in peace'): the jailer's transmission of the release order uses the standard peace-dismissal. His communication is honest and complete; he has no reason to withhold the order. Paul's refusal to accept quiet release is not vindictiveness but strategic: a silent departure would leave the impression that the apostles were guilty of the charges, harmful to the reputation of the church they leave behind at Lydia's house.
16.37: Rhōmaious huparchontas ('being Romans/Roman citizens'): Paul's disclosure of Roman citizenship is strategically timed — withheld during the beating to establish the injustice, deployed after to create legal and diplomatic pressure. Akatakritous ('uncondemned/without trial'): the technical legal term for summary punishment without due process — a serious violation of Roman law (the Lex Valeria and Lex Porcia protected citizens from summary flogging). Paul's demand for a public acknowledgment protects the emerging church's civic reputation and sets a precedent for Christian legal standing.
16.38: ephobēthēsan de akousantes hoti Rhōmaioi eisin ('they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans'): the magistrates' fear reflects their legal exposure — flogging Roman citizens without trial was a punishable offense. The reversal of power — the imprisoned apostles now holding civic leverage over the magistrates — is Luke's ironic demonstration that the missionary movement operates within Roman law while transcending Roman authority. The empire that persecuted the apostles must apologize to them.
16.39: parakalountes ('entreating/appeasing'): the magistrates' escorting the apostles out is an act of public appeasement, not merely legal compliance. The verb parakaleō (exhort, beseech, comfort) is used throughout Acts for the community's encouragement; here it is deployed by civic officials forced into a supplicant position. Erōtōntes apelthein apo tēs poleōs ('requesting them to leave the city'): a polite expulsion — they want the problem resolved and the troublesome missionaries gone, but cannot force them.
16.40: eisēlthon pros tēn Lydian kai idontes parekalesan tous adelphous ('they went to Lydia's house, and having seen the brothers, they encouraged them'): Paul and Silas do not accept the magistrates' request to leave until they have returned to the church, reported what happened, and provided pastoral encouragement. Parekaloun ('encouraged') is the apostolic task — the same word as Barnabas's name. The church at Philippi will remain one of Paul's most beloved communities (Phil 1:3-8; 4:10-20). The 'we' passage ends here — Luke remains at Philippi.

21.1: anachthentes de apespasmen ap autōn (anachthentes de apespasmen ap' autōn): 'after tearing ourselves away from them, we set sail'. Apospaō (pull away/tear away) is the word for forcible separation — the departure from Miletus is not a clean send-off but an emotionally costly severance. The 'we' narrator is present throughout ch. 21. The coastal route — Cos, Rhodes, Patara — mirrors the Aegean navigation of ch. 20 in reverse direction, now southward toward Phoenicia.
21.2: heurontes ploion diaperan eis Phoinikēn: at Patara, the party transfers to a larger ship sailing directly to Phoenicia, abandoning the coastal hop. The discovery of a direct-passage vessel is providential for Paul's Pentecost urgency (20:16). Phoenicia — the Syro-Lebanese coast — was evangelized by those scattered in Acts 11:19; Paul traverses communities already established.
21.3: anaphananteS de ten Kypron kai katalIpontes auten euōnymon: passing Cyprus to the left (south), keeping the island on the port side. Luke's nautical precision — eualōnymon (left side) is the sailor's bearing — is consistent throughout the sea-voyage sections. Tyre is identified as the unloading destination; the ship's commercial cargo determines the port of call that shapes Paul's pastoral itinerary.
21.4: anesantes tous mathetas emeINamen autou hemeras hepta... hoitines tō pneumati elegon tō Paulō mē epibainein eis Hierosolyma: 'finding the disciples, we stayed seven days... who through the Spirit were telling Paul not to go up to Jerusalem'. The Tyre disciples' Spirit-inspired warning creates the exegetical tension: the Spirit in 20:22-23 compels Paul toward Jerusalem; the Spirit through these disciples urges avoidance. The resolution is that the prophecy accurately disclosed danger (fulfilled) without constituting a divine prohibition — the disciples' mē was their pastoral application of the prophetic data, not the Spirit's direct command.
21.5: ote de egeneto hemas exartisai tas hemeras (hote de egeneto hēmas exartisai tas hēmeras): 'when our days there were completed'. Exartizō (complete/fit out) has a finishing-work sense — the seven days in Tyre were a purposeful stay, not an accident. The departure scene mirrors Miletus: the whole community, wives and children included, escorted Paul to the shore. The beach prayer — kneeling together at the water's edge — is the image of the Pauline churches at their most tender.
21.6: aspAsamenos allēlous epebemen eis to ploion: the mutual farewell embrace and embarkation. Aspázomai (greet/embrace) is the Christian greeting-word that frames community encounters throughout Acts. The Tyre disciples 'returned to their own homes' (eis ta idia) — the farewell is formal and final; they returned to ordinary life while Paul continued toward the extraordinary destination.
21.7: hēmeis de ton ploun dianysantes apo Tyrou katerchomen eis Ptolemaida: Ptolemais (modern Akko/Acre) is the last major port before Caesarea. 'One day' stop — the brevity suggests a less established community there; Paul greets and departs in the time a ship takes to unload and reload. The laconic notation preserves the travel diary's detail without elaborating what was clearly a brief pastoral touch.
21.8: eis Kaisareian kai eiselthontes eis ton oikon Philippou tou euangelistou (eis Kaisareian kai eiselthontes eis ton oikon Philippou tou euangelistou): Philip 'the evangelist' — the title distinguishes him from the apostle Philip. He was one of the Seven deacons (Acts 6:5), the first to bring the gospel to Samaria (8:5-8), the evangelist of the Ethiopian eunuch (8:26-39). His Caesarean household has been established since at least 10:1 (Cornelius's conversion); Philip has apparently settled there. Euangelistēs appears in Eph 4:11 and 2 Tim 4:5 as a distinct ministry-category.
21.9: touto de ēsan thygateres tessares parthenoi prophēteuousai: 'this man had four virgin daughters who prophesied'. Propheteuousai (prophesying, present participle) indicates ongoing prophetic ministry, not a one-time gift. Luke records the fact without describing their ministry — the four daughters fulfill the Joel 2:28 promise of daughters prophesying (Acts 2:17). Their presence in Caesarea makes Philip's household a prophetic center, which makes Agabus's arrival (v.10) a convergence of prophetic voices.
21.10: ēlthen tis apo tes Ioudaias prophētes onomati Agabos: Agabus last appeared in Acts 11:28 prophesying the Claudius famine; here he reappears with a personal prophecy for Paul. The two Agabus prophecies bracket Paul's missionary journeys: the first (famine) prompted the collection that Paul now carries to Jerusalem; the second announces the binding that awaits him. Agabus functions as the Jeremianic prophet who speaks the difficult word to God's servant.
21.11: aron ten zōnēn tou Paulou, desas heautou tous podas kai tas cheiras (arōn tēn zōnēn tou Paulou, dēsas heautou tous podas kai tas cheiras): the prophetic sign-act — binding himself with Paul's belt — echoes OT prophetic dramatization (Isa 20; Jer 13; Ezek 4-5). Houtōs dēsousin hoi Ioudaioi en Hierousalēm ton andra: 'thus the Jews in Jerusalem will bind the man'. The prophecy is specific and accurate (Acts 21:33). The sign-act makes the word visible; Agabus embodies in his own person what Paul will endure.
21.12: eklIomen hēmeis te kai hoi entopIoi tou mē anabainein auton eis Hierousalem: 'we ourselves and the local people begged him not to go up to Jerusalem'. Eklion (beg/beseech) is the emotional appeal-word. The community's response to Agabus's prophecy is protective — they hear the warning and try to prevent the fulfillment. Paul's refusal (v.13-14) distinguishes his obedience to divine necessity from pastoral self-preservation, the same tension that characterized Jesus's own Jerusalem journey.
21.13: ti poieite klaiOntes kai sykryptOntes mou ten kardian (ti poieite klaiontes kai synkryptontes mou tēn kardian): 'what are you doing weeping and breaking my heart?' Synkryptō (crush/shatter together) — a compound expressing the pain of communal grief pressing in on Paul's resolve. His declaration: egō gar ou monon dethēnai alla kai apothanein eis Hierousalem hetoimōs echō hyper tou onomatos tou kyriou Iēsou: 'I am ready not only to be bound but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus'. The martyr-vocabulary (hetoimōs echō, ready; apothanein, to die) frames Paul as the Suffering Servant-pattern apostle.
21.14: mē peithomenou de autou hesychAsamen eIpontes, Ho tou kyriou thelēma ginesthō: 'since he would not be persuaded, we kept quiet, saying, Let the will of the Lord be done'. Hēsychazō (become quiet/cease) — the community's acceptance. Tou kyriou thelēma ginesthō is the echo of the Lord's Prayer (genēthētō to thelēma sou, thy will be done) and Gethsemane — the shape of Christian submission. Luke presents Paul's community as learning through this episode what Jesus modeled: the will of God is not circumvented but embraced.
21.15: episkeusAmenoi anebainomen eis Hierousalēm (episkeuasamenoi anebainomen eis Hierousalēm): 'having prepared/packed, we went up to Jerusalem'. Episkyeuázomai (prepare/equip for travel) — the practical preparation that follows the spiritual resolution. The 'we' narrator continues; the community from Caesarea accompanies Paul, including Mnason of Cyprus (v.16), a disciple from the earliest days of the church.
21.16: Mnasōni tini Kyprīō archaiō mathētē: 'Mnason, a certain Cypriot, an early disciple'. Archaios mathētēs (old/early disciple) — one who was converted in the earliest days of the Jerusalem church, possibly from Pentecost or shortly after. Cyprus was the origin of Barnabas (4:36) and the location of early mission activity (13:4-12). Mnason's household in Jerusalem provides a safe base for a party whose status in the Jerusalem community is about to be tested.
21.17: genomenōn de hēmōn eis Hierousalēm asmenos apedexanto hēmas hoi adelphoi: 'when we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers received us gladly'. Asmenōs (gladly/warmly) signals genuine hospitality — the arrival is welcomed, setting the scene for the James meeting of the following day. The 'we' narrator is still present. The journey that began in Acts 19:21 reaches its destination; Jerusalem is both the end of the third journey and the beginning of the passion-sequence parallels.
21.18: eiseiei de ho Paulos syn hēmin pros Iakōbon: the formal meeting with James and all the elders of the Jerusalem church. James is the acknowledged single leader of the Jerusalem community (15:13-21; Gal 2:12); the elders are the collegial body with him. The meeting mirrors the Acts 15 council structure. Paul's report — in sequence, all that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry — is the same missionary reporting pattern as 14:27 and 15:4.
21.19: eksēgeito kath hen hekaston hōn epoiēsen ho theos en tois ethnesin dia tēs diakonias autou: 'he related one by one what God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry'. Exēgeomai (narrate in detail/expound) — the same word Luke uses for the Emmaus disciples' account (Luke 24:35). Paul's report attributes every result to God (ho theos), not his own effectiveness; the dia tes diakonias autou (through his ministry) is the instrument, not the agent. The Jerusalem elders hear a Gentile mission summary they could only partially know from hearsay.
21.20: hoi de akousantes edoxazon ton theon: 'when they heard, they glorified God'. The reaction to Paul's report mirrors 11:18's response to Peter's Cornelius account: when God's work is rightly reported, the proper response is doxology. The elders immediately follow with pastoral intelligence: tens of thousands of believing Jews are zealous for the law. The juxtaposition sets up the tension — Gentile-mission success alongside Torah-observant Jewish Christianity. Both exist; both glorify God; the accommodation question must be managed.
21.21: katēchēthēsan de peri sou hoti apostasian didasKeis apo Mōuseōs tous kata ta ethnē pantas Ioudaious: 'they have been informed about you that you teach all the Jews among the Gentiles to forsake Moses'. The charge is a misrepresentation of Paul's actual teaching (he never told Jewish believers to stop circumcising their children as a cultural practice) but reflects the rhetorical damage done by opponents. The elders are managing both Paul's real position and its popular distortion — a classic pastoral-diplomatic challenge.
21.22: ti oun estin: 'what then is to be done?' The Jerusalem elders' direct question acknowledges the pastoral problem without accusation. Their proposed solution — Paul's participation in a Nazirite vow-completion rite (vv.23-24) — is a public gesture of Torah-identification designed to correct the misimpression. The proposal is practical and politically realistic; it does not require Paul to abandon his Gentile-mission theology, only to demonstrate that Jewish-Christian Torah-observance and Gentile freedom coexist within the one gospel.
21.23: eisin hēmin andres tessares eucHen echontes eph heautōn: four men completing Nazirite vows (Num 6). The rite required shaving the head and offering prescribed sacrifices at the temple's completion — expensive enough that wealthy patrons often covered the costs for those who had vowed. Paul's participation as patron for all four demonstrates both his solidarity with Torah-observant Jewish Christianity and his material use of the collection-funds he has brought.
21.24: stoicHeis kai autos phylassōn ton nomon (stoicheis kai autos phylassōn ton nomon): 'you yourself also walk orderly, keeping the law'. Stoicheō (walk in step with / conform to an order) — Paul is asked to perform what is true of him: he does not require Torah-observance from Gentiles but neither does he renounce it as a Jewish man. The Apostolic Decree (v.25) is referenced as the settled Gentile policy, distinguishing it from what is asked of Paul as a Jew.
21.25: peri de ton pepisteukoton ethnōn hēmeis epesteilamen krinantes phulassesthai autous: the Jerusalem elders reaffirm the Apostolic Decree (15:28-29) — no Torah-burden on Gentile believers except the four abstentions (idol food, blood, strangled meat, sexual immorality). The restatement here serves to clarify that Paul's participation in the vow rite is not a reversal of Gentile freedom; the two communities have different but equally valid practices within the one church.
21.26: tote ho Paulos paralabōn tous andras tē echomene hēmera syn autois hagnistheis eisēei eis to hieron: Paul's participation in the purification rites and temple presentation. Hagnizō (purify) follows the LXX pattern for Nazirite completion (Num 6:9-12). Paul's physical presence in the temple — the last time in Acts — is both his pastoral accommodation and his fatal exposure. The vow-completion brings him into contact with Asian Jews who recognize him.
21.27: hoi apo tes Asias Ioudaioi... suneHeon panta ton ochlon kai epebalon ep auton tas cheiras: the Asian Jews — likely from Ephesus — who recognize Paul as the synagogue-disturber of their region. Synecheon (stirred up / threw into confusion) — the same verb for the Ephesian riot (19:32). Epebalon ep' auton tas cheiras (laid hands on him) — the arrest is mob action, not legal process. The accusation structure mirrors the charges against Stephen (6:13-14): temple, people, Torah.
21.28: Andreson Israelitai, boētheite... houtos estin ho anthrōpos ho kata tou laou kai tou nomou kai tou topou toutou didaskon pantas pantachou: 'Men of Israel, help! — this is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place'. The three charges are the Jerusalem establishment's version of the Asian Jews' accusation. The third charge echoes the Hellenist accusation against Stephen (6:13) almost verbatim; Luke's parallel is intentional — Paul's passion-sequence mirrors Stephen's.
21.29: ēsan gar proheōrakotes Trophimon ton Ephesion en tē polei syn autō: the error behind the temple-defilement charge — the Asian Jews had seen Trophimus the Ephesian with Paul in the city and assumed he had brought him into the temple. Proheōraō (have seen previously) — eyewitness evidence drawn to a false conclusion. The irony is that Paul was in the temple demonstrating his Torah-observance; the very act of piety they now misrepresent as temple-defilement.
21.30: ekinēthē te hē polis holē: 'the whole city was stirred up'. Kineomai (set in motion) — the mob-energy that characterized the Ephesian riot now repeats in Jerusalem. Synchedromē tou laou (the people ran together) — the spontaneous mob-gathering. The temple doors are shut behind Paul (ekleisthēsan hai thyrai) — the symbolic closure: the temple that Paul sought to honor by the vow-rite now bars him out. The exclusion from temple is the beginning of his path toward Rome.
21.31: zētountōn de auton apokteinai anebē phasis tō chiliarchō tēs speiras hoti holē syngechytai Hierousalēm: the Roman tribune receives word that all Jerusalem is in confusion. The providential timing — the Roman garrison was stationed in the Antonia Fortress adjacent to the temple precisely to manage such disturbances — places Paul under Roman protection at the moment of maximum danger. The governor-apparatus that will frustrate justice in chs. 24-26 is simultaneously Paul's protection from mob execution.
21.32: hos exautēs paralabōn stratiōtas kai hekatontarchas katedRamen ep autous: 'who immediately took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them'. The Roman response — soldiers and centurions plural — reflects the seriousness of the riot. Exautēs (immediately, at once) and katadrameō (run down) convey urgency. The mob stops beating Paul on sight of the soldiers; Roman military presence enforces a peace the temple establishment failed to maintain.
21.33: tote eggisas ho chiliarchos epelAbeto autou kai ekeleusen dethēnai halysesi dysi: 'then the tribune approached and arrested him and ordered him bound with two chains'. Halysei dysi (two chains) — Agabus's prophecy (v.11: 'thus the Jews in Jerusalem will bind the man') is fulfilled in the letter. The double chaining may reflect Roman procedure for a prisoner of uncertain legal status, but Luke marks it as prophetic fulfillment. The tribune assumes Paul is a dangerous criminal (v.38).
21.34: alla men ti eboōn en tō ochlō: 'but some in the crowd were shouting one thing and some another'. The cacophony of conflicting accusations prevents the tribune from establishing facts. Mē dynamenos de gnōnai to asphales (unable to determine the truth) — the Roman judicial system is overwhelmed by the mob's noise. Paul is ordered to the barracks (eis tēn parembolēn) for interrogation under controlled conditions — procedurally correct but helpless before the crowd's confusion.
21.35: hote de egeneto epi tous anababathmons: 'when he got to the steps'. The Antonia Fortress had a grand staircase descending to the temple court — Paul is literally being carried (bastazesthai) by soldiers above the crowd pressing below. Bia tou ochlou (the violence of the crowd) — the mob's physical force required the soldiers to lift Paul to safety. The staircase becomes a pulpit (v.40): elevation that was forced becomes platform for proclamation.
21.36: ēkolouthei gar to plēthos tou laou krazon, Aire auton: 'the crowd of people followed, crying, Away with him!' Aire (take away/remove) is the same cry of the Jerusalem crowd at Jesus's trial (Luke 23:18; John 19:15). Luke's verbal parallel is unmistakable — Paul's passion-sequence is narrated with deliberate Jesus-parallel. The one who followed Jesus (22:3) is now addressed with the same rejection-shout as the one he persecuted.
21.37: Hellēnisti ginōskeis: the tribune's surprised question when Paul addresses him in Greek. The assumption was that Paul was the Egyptian revolutionary (ho Aigyptios, v.38) who had led a desert revolt with four thousand dagger-men (sikarioi). Paul's educated Greek (Hellēnisti) signals his diaspora identity; the tribune's surprise marks a status-recalibration. Greek is the language of the educated Roman world; Paul uses it to claim protected status.
21.38: ouk ara sy ei ho Aigyptios ho pro toutōn tōn hēmerōn anastatōsas kai exagagōn eis tēn erēmon tous tetrakischilious andras tōn sikariōn: the tribune's specific identification — the Egyptian prophet who led four thousand sikarioi (dagger-men, assassins). Josephus records this figure (Ant. 20.169-172; J.W. 2.261-263) — an Egyptian false prophet who led followers to the Mount of Olives promising the walls of Jerusalem would fall. Felix dispersed the movement; the Egyptian escaped. The tribune's mistaken identity is both realistic and ironic: Paul is the true prophet, mistaken for the false one.
21.39: Ioudaios eimi, Tarseus tes Kilikias, ouk asēmou poleōs politēs: 'I am a Jew, a citizen of Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no obscure city'. Ouk asēmou poleōs (not of an obscure city) — the litotes is Paul's educated self-presentation. Tarsus was a major intellectual and commercial center, ranking with Alexandria and Athens in Strabo's assessment. Jewish citizenship in Tarsus was a recognized legal status. Paul deploys all three identities — Jewish, Tarsian, and (implicitly) Roman citizen (22:25-29) — in sequence to command respect and legal protection.
21.40: epistrepsamenos de kai kataseisas tē cheiri tō laō, polles de sigēs genomenēs, prosefonēsen tē Hebraidi dialektō legōn: 'turning and waving his hand to the people, and when great silence fell, he addressed them in the Hebrew dialect'. Kataseisas tē cheiri (waving the hand for silence) is the orator's call-to-attention gesture; Luke uses it for Paul at other speech-openings (13:16). Hebraidi dialektō (Hebrew/Aramaic dialect) — switching from Greek to Aramaic silences the crowd more effectively than the soldiers; language is Paul's most effective crowd-control. The speech that follows (22:1-21) is Paul's autobiography as Christian calling.

18.1: 'After this Paul left Athens and went to Corinth' — the departure from Athens ends the Areopagus mission (17:16-34) and begins the eighteen-month Corinthian ministry. The transition marks a strategic shift: Corinth is a commercial crossroads, not an intellectual center; Paul's subsequent letters reveal that his Corinthian preaching deliberately avoided rhetorical display (1 Cor 2:1-4). The Athens-to-Corinth movement also corresponds to a shift from the philosophical register of 17:22-31 to the cross-centered proclamation of 1 Cor 1:23.
18.2: 'A Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus, who had recently come from Italy with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had ordered all Jews to leave Rome' — the expulsion under Claudius (ca. 49-50 AD) is corroborated by Suetonius (Claudius 25.4 — 'Since the Jews constantly made disturbances at the instigation of Chrestus, he expelled them from Rome'). 'Chrestus' is likely 'Christus' — the dispute over the messianic claims of Jesus was splitting Roman synagogues. Aquila and Priscilla are likely already believers when Paul meets them; their synagogue dispute would explain their expulsion alongside other Jewish Christians.
18.3: 'Because he was a tentmaker as they were, he stayed and worked with them' — skēnopoios (tentmaker, leather-worker) is Paul's trade, consistent with his manual-labor principle (1 Thess 2:9; 1 Cor 9:12-15; 2 Thess 3:8). The philosophical tradition required teachers to receive fees; Paul's refusal to take support from Corinth (2 Cor 11:7-9) was a deliberate counter-convention: the teacher who works with his hands is not a sophist selling wisdom. The trade-based friendship with Aquila and Priscilla becomes one of the most enduring partnerships of Paul's ministry.
18.4: 'Every Sabbath he reasoned (dielēgeto — was dialoguing/disputing) in the synagogue, trying to persuade Jews and Greeks' — dielēgeto (imperfect = ongoing, repeated action) is the characteristic Lukan verb for Paul's synagogue method: not monologue proclamation but dialogical reasoning. 'Persuade' (epeithein — to persuade, imperfect = kept attempting) combined with dielēgeto creates the portrait of a reasoned, interactive theological engagement. 'Jews and Greeks' — the synagogue's Gentile adherents (sebomenoi — God-fearers) are included in Paul's audience from the beginning of the Corinthian synagogue phase.
18.5: 'When Silas and Timothy came from Macedonia, Paul devoted himself exclusively to preaching (syneicheto tō logō — was constrained/pressed by the word), testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah' — the textual tradition at syneicheto offers two readings: some MSS read tō logō (by the word) and others tō pneumati (by the Spirit, disp=3); the MKT follows 'Spirit' as primary. The arrival of Silas and Timothy (with financial support from Macedonian churches — 2 Cor 11:8-9) freed Paul from tentmaking, enabling full-time proclamation. 'Testifying that Jesus was the Messiah' — the content: the specific claim that Jesus of Nazareth is the Christ (ton Christon einai Iēsoun) is the central Jewish stumbling block.
18.6: 'When they opposed Paul and became abusive, he shook out his clothes in protest and said, Your blood be on your own heads!' — the symbolic action (ektinaxamenos ta himatia — shaking out the garments) echoes Nehemiah's curse-gesture (Neh 5:13 — 'I shook out the fold of my garment') and is the escalated form of the dust-shaking from feet (Acts 13:51). 'Your blood be on your own heads' — the blood-guilt formula draws on Ezek 33:4-5: if the watchman warns and the people do not hear, 'his blood shall be on his own head.' Paul has fulfilled his prophetic-watchman duty; the guilt for rejection now belongs to the rejectors. 'I am innocent; from now on I will go to the Gentiles' — the Gentile-turn formula repeated from 13:46.
18.7: 'Paul went to the house of Titius Justus, a worshipper of God (sebomenou ton theon — one fearing/revering God)' — the God-fearer designation (sebomenos) marks Titius Justus as a Gentile synagogue-adherent: he has adopted monotheism and Jewish ethics but is uncircumcised. His house is strategically located 'next door to the synagogue' — Paul's Gentile base of operations is literally adjacent to the Jewish community he has just left. The geographical irony (the synagogue's Gentile neighbors receive what the synagogue rejected) is characteristic of Luke's narrative theology.
18.8: 'Crispus, the synagogue leader (archisynagōgos), and his entire household believed in the Lord; and many of the Corinthians who heard Paul believed and were baptised' — Crispus's conversion is corroborated by 1 Cor 1:14 (Paul baptised Crispus himself, one of the very few he personally baptised). The synagogue leader's conversion is the highest-profile loss the Corinthian Jewish community sustained. 'His entire household believed' — the household-unit conversion pattern consistent throughout Acts. 'Many of the Corinthians' — the city's general population begins to respond beyond the synagogue community.
18.9: 'One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision: Do not be afraid; keep on speaking, do not be silent' — the Lord's vision (en horamati — in a vision) uses the standard prophetic-vision vocabulary (horama: Acts 10:3; 16:9; 18:9). The double negative command (mē phobou, mē siōpēsēs — do not fear, do not be silent) addresses two temptations that would naturally follow synagogue opposition: fear of violence and the temptation to withdraw. The night timing and visionary form (as opposed to waking auditory experience) is the standard divine-warning vehicle (Gen 26:24; 46:2-3; 1 Kgs 3:5).
18.10: 'For I am with you, and no one is going to attack and harm you, because I have many people in this city' — 'I am with you' (egō eimi meta sou) is the divine-presence formula used at commissioning moments throughout the OT (Exod 3:12; Josh 1:5, 9; Jer 1:8, 19) — the Emmanuel-formula. 'I have many people in this city' (laos estin moi polys en tē polei tautē) — the divine claim over Corinth before they have converted: God's elect in Corinth are already his people who have not yet heard. The vision explains why Paul must not be silent: there is a harvest already prepared.
18.11: 'So Paul stayed in Corinth for a year and a half, teaching them the word of God' (logon tou theou — the word of God; disp=3 for logos) — the eighteen months (eniayton kai mēnas hax) is the longest fixed-duration stay Luke records in the second missionary journey. 'The word of God' as logos is the gospel-proclamation content: the message that Paul teaches is identified as God's own word. The long stay corresponds to the Lord's vision-promise: God has people here, so Paul must preach long enough to find them all.
18.12: 'While Gallio was proconsul (anthypatos) of Achaia, the Jews of Corinth made a united attack on Paul and brought him to the place of judgment (bēma — tribunal/judgment seat)' — Gallio's proconsulate is dateable from the Delphi Inscription (ca. 51-52 AD), providing one of the firmest chronological anchors for Pauline chronology. The bēma (raised judicial platform) is the same word used for God's judgment seat (Rom 14:10; 2 Cor 5:10) — Luke's placement of Paul before an earthly bēma prefigures the cosmic bēma before which all will appear.
18.13: 'This man is persuading the people to worship God in ways contrary to the law (para ton nomon — contrary to the law)' — the charge is deliberately ambiguous: para ton nomon could refer to the Jewish Torah or Roman law. The Jewish accusers probably intend the Torah (Paul's messianic preaching disrupts Jewish worship patterns); Gallio will interpret the charge as referring to internal Jewish disputes (v.15). The nomos (disp=3) here is the Jewish Law — the word that governs the covenant community's religious practice.
18.14: 'If you Jews were making a complaint about some misdemeanour or serious crime (adikēma ti ē rhadiourgēma ponēron), it would be reasonable for me to listen to you' — Gallio distinguishes between actionable legal offenses (adikēma: a wrong done; rhadiourgēma ponēron: a vicious/serious villainy) and matters of Jewish internal dispute. His willingness to hear genuine criminal complaints (v.14) vs. his refusal to adjudicate religious questions (v.15) establishes the legal precedent that Paul's movement does not constitute a Roman criminal offense.
18.15: 'But since it involves questions about words and names and your own law (tou nomou hymōn — your own law), settle the matter yourselves. I will not be a judge of such things' — Gallio identifies the dispute as involving logos (disp=3 — 'words': the meaning of terms), onomata (names: the identity-claim about Jesus as Messiah), and nomos (disp=3 — 'your law': internal Jewish legal interpretation). All three belong to Jewish internal discourse; Roman magistrates have no jurisdiction over theological disputes. The Gallio ruling implicitly classifies Christianity as an intra-Jewish matter, providing de facto legal protection for the early mission.
18.16: 'So he drove them off (apēlasen autous — dismissed/drove them away)' — Gallio's dismissal (apēlasen, aorist of apelaunō — to drive/push away) is abrupt and decisive. The proconsul exercises his authority to clear his tribunal of cases that do not belong to it. The brevity of the verse mirrors the brevity of Gallio's engagement: he considers the matter not worth his attention, and says so by action rather than extended argument.
18.17: 'Then the crowd seized Sosthenes the synagogue leader and beat him in front of the proconsul; and Gallio showed no concern whatever' — the beating of Sosthenes (etypton — kept striking, imperfect) by 'the crowd' is ambiguous: some MSS specify 'the Greeks,' suggesting a spontaneous anti-Jewish action by the Corinthian populace seizing on Gallio's dismissal as permission for mob violence. Gallio's indifference (ouden toutōn tō Galliōni emelen — none of these things concerned Gallio) is the Roman administrator's calculated non-intervention in non-Roman affairs. Sosthenes may be the same person addressed in 1 Cor 1:1 as co-sender — perhaps his persecution became the occasion of his conversion.
18.18: 'Paul stayed on in Corinth for some time. Then he left the brothers and sisters and sailed for Syria, accompanied by Priscilla and Aquila. Before he sailed, he had his hair cut off at Cenchreae because of a vow he had made' — the hair-cutting (keiramenos tēn kephalēn) at Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern port) is a Nazirite vow-completion gesture (Num 6:18 — the Nazirite shaves his head at the vow's end).
